Beyond that, their choices are as eclectic as their various motivations for going renegade. On Alacar, Chira is a fairly common choice among good renegade demons, as she represents the possibility of redemption and rebirth associated with spring, while Lilin is a favorite all around, with her teachings centering around creation and personal freedom.

What Makes a Sin?

The list of sins that can form a demon is as varied as it is enigmatic. Some demons are formed from sins that represent corrupt internal qualities, like lust or sloth, which many societies find immoral. Other sins, however, are much more specific in the actions they embody, such as arson.

Names

Renegade demons usually keep their original name in Abyssal, but some choose to give themselves a new name once they've transitioned completely away from their sin, to signify the start of a new life. Even when they keep their original name, they might remove any epithets they had earned (such as "The Torturer of Innocent Souls") that seem especially unfitting to their new beginnings.

Sample Names

Aylurra, Barxilaz, Laughter, Mandrilzor, Odium, Second Chance, Silent Shadow, Vilgix, Will, Yaaahn, Zorgoth.

Demon Heritages

Each demon heritage is incredibly distinctive, formed from a different sin. Choose one of the following demon heritages at 1st level, which each includes a size. If you choose a different heritage than those below without a listed size, such as a versatile heritage, your demon is typically Medium, and you might need to determine your associated sin.

Babau

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.hx8ZD5p3uaylKP8z]{Babau}

You are a babau, a demon formed from the sin of bloody murder, and you can drip with acidic slime when needed. Your size is Medium. You gain the @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-actions.Item.xorlcY1YBzidwL6C]{Reactive Slime} reaction.

Brimorak

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.vFp4b9BOVJmUnaLe]{Brimorak}

You are a brimorak, a demon formed from the sin of arson, and you can see through all kinds of smoke. Your size is Small. Smoke doesn't impair your vision; you automatically succeed at the flat check to target a @Compendium[pf2e.conditionitems.Concealed]{Concealed} creature if that creature is concealed only by smoke.

Dretch

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.EgyeeOFe7Etmjw8Y]{Dretch}

You are a dretch, a demon formed from the sin of sloth, and you know how to cower from your foes in order to protect yourself. Your size is Small. You gain the @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-actions.Item.WYCzIh9CiXm6xDhA]{Cower} action.

Hezrou

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.LQmBNyziBBeYexF2]{Hezrou}

You are a hezrou, a demon formed from the sin of corruption, and your toadlike form allows you to move on both land and water. Your size is Large. You gain a swim Speed of 10 feet and the amphibious trait. Like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air.

Marilith

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.fGxttYCKzl1Q9izt]{Marilith}

You are a marilith, a demon formed from the sin of pride. You have a snake lower body and tail, and as long as you remain confident, additional spectral arms occasionally flash around your torso. Your size is Medium. Your tail makes you especially stable. You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to your Fortitude or Reflex DC against attempts to @Compendium[pf2e.actionspf2e.Shove]{Shove} or @Compendium[pf2e.actionspf2e.Trip]{Trip} you. This bonus also applies to saving throws against spells or effects that attempt to knock you @Compendium[pf2e.conditionitems.Prone]{Prone}. In addition, if any effect would force you to move 10 feet or more, you are moved only half the distance.

Omox

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.J64dvqkooT62J7N4]{Omox}

You are an omox, a demon formed from the sin of pollution, and your humanoid form composed of acidic sludge is resistant to all forms of acid while allowing you to breathe easily in air or underwater. Your size is Medium. You gain resistance to acid equal to half your level (minimum 1). You also gain the amphibious trait. Like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air.

Succubus

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.NDiPaKN6bEE72zPL]{Succubus}

You are a succubus, a demon formed from the sin of lust, and you are able to befriend, deceive, and seduce others with ease. Your size is Medium. You gain the trained proficiency rank in Deception and Diplomacy. If you would automatically become trained in one of those skills (from your background or class, for example), you instead become trained in a skill of your choice.

Vrock

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.cmcHp4a8qhl7Xvyj]{Vrock}

You are a vrock, a demon formed from the sin of wrath, and the storm of rage and ruin you once contained within you inures you to electricity. Your size is Medium. You gain resistance to electricity equal to half your level (minimum 1). You also gain the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.feats-srd.Item.LQw0yIMDUJJkq1nD]{Cat Fall} skill feat as a bonus feat, whether or not you meet the feat's prerequisites.

Doppelganger Ancestry.name: 变形怪族裔 Doppelganger Ancestry
Doppelganger Ancestry.text:
变形怪（Doppelganger），或如一些个体更喜欢被称为棱魂（prismsoul），是一群拥有无数面孔、也仿佛全无面孔的人。在他们千变万化的伪装与转化之中，在面具之下，许多变形怪都在追寻属于自己的独特身份。另一些则乐于忘却这些，拥抱每一次改变当下的瞬间。

尽管变形怪极为罕见，他们仍然遍布世界上几乎每一个类人生物社会。即便如此，由于他们的变形能力，他们往往不为人所察觉；其次也因为他们很少建立完全由变形怪组成的聚居地或组织。变形怪的标志性能力，是将自身形态改变为其他类人生物，并随心所欲地在社会中行动——这份强大的能力是该族裔最大的祝福，但在一些变形怪看来，也同样是他们最大的诅咒。

刻板印象中的变形怪，充其量是个缺乏道德的投机者：利用变形能力进行渗透、剥削，并以各种方式欺骗他人以谋取自身利益。确实，这种力量让人很容易以这种方式过上舒适的生活，尽管风险不小。然而，有些变形怪甚至觉得这样做既令人愉悦又令人满足，每当他们完成一次骗局时都会感到一阵兴奋。

不过，世界上还有许多其他类型的变形怪。其中一些希望在人生中走出属于自己的道路，而不是仅仅滑入借来的生活，沉溺于冒充与背叛。这些非传统的变形怪，除了这一核心信条之外并没有统一的共同信仰；他们各自都有独特的观点可供分享。

如果你想扮演一个能够在多种类人生物形态间改变，并有机会作为一个能轻易自我重塑的人去探索身份与自我的意义的角色，你就应该扮演变形怪。

你可能会……

  • 将改变形态作为表达情绪、兴致或审美的一部分。
  • 开始采纳你所选择形态的各种性格特质与其他心理特性，“成为那张面具”。
  • 默认采用包含冒充在内的计划或策略。

他人可能会……

  • 认为你能胜任他们所需的任何渗透或诡计。
  • 在你学会他们的举止或得知私人信息后，担心你会变成他们的样子。
  • 一开始甚至根本不知道你是变形怪。

外形描述

在其自然形态下，变形怪拥有光滑的肌肤，颜色不定且偏淡。有些变形怪的肤色几乎接近其他类人生物族裔的肤色；另一些则呈灰色、白色、银色，或其他被别的类人生物认为“不自然”的色调。

变形怪的身体，尤其是面部，具有风格化的特征，却缺少雀斑、毛发等细微细节。其他族裔的一些成员（包括研究变形怪的学者）声称，这种自然形态看起来像一幅尚在绘制中的画作或雕塑——仿佛某位宇宙艺术家在完成之前停笔，留下了最后一步的缺口。

然而，许多变形怪往往认为这种看法既侮辱人也不准确。“不完整”的观念，本质上建立在“只有人类及其近亲才算完整”的前提之上，并暗示差异意味着变形怪在各方面有所欠缺。赞同学者描述的变形怪中，有些只是并不在意“未完成”所带来的负面含义；也有些不仅认同这些含义，还对此极为在意，因为他们为自己的自然形态感到羞耻。最后这类变形怪有时会厌恶自己的自然形态，倾向于始终维持他人的形态，或为自己艺术性地打造理想化、往往十分美丽的类人生物形态，并将其视为真正的自我形态。

变形怪聚居地

变形怪不会建立属于自己的聚居地，他们甚至很少在其他类人生物的大型聚居地中形成子社群。然而，当变形怪确实聚集在一起时——通常是由于阴谋家的计划——他们的社会结构往往会随情境而转变与变动，其方式令其他族裔难以理解。有时，一个主要由其他一个或多个族裔组成的聚居地，会在不知情的情况下被某个变形怪统治一段时间；此时，该变形怪可能会将自身的一些偏好施加在聚居地的组织与阶层结构上。

社会

变形怪并不拥有一个单一而统一的社会；相当数量的变形怪甚至除了自己的父母之外几乎不认识其他同族。这使得变形怪拥有极其多样的生活方式与社会动态。以下各群体分别代表了相当数量变形怪的观点，但它们都不是会定期聚会、共享目标或进度报告的铁板一块或社交俱乐部。事实上，只有阴谋家（由其天性决定）以及一些棱魂典范，才倾向于形成规模较大的志同道合者团体。这些群体并非完全互斥；尤其是，阴谋家往往同时也是其他群体的成员。

群体

以下各群体分别代表了相当数量变形怪的观点，但它们都不是会定期聚会、共享目标或进度报告的铁板一块或社交俱乐部。事实上，只有阴谋家（由其天性决定）以及一些棱魂典范，才倾向于形成规模较大的志同道合者团体。这些群体并非完全互斥；尤其是，阴谋家往往同时也是其他群体的成员。

阴谋家

阴谋家是少数希望将其他变形怪组织成派系、秘密社团或类似组织的变形怪。尽管这个名称会让人联想到阴影交易与卑劣阴谋，但阴谋家的动机多种多样，对理想中的变形怪组织也有着广泛的设想。当然，其中一些阴谋家确实邪恶，其目标从秘密统治整个聚居地或国家，到获取财富与权力，或传播某种恶意的宗教不等。然而，另一些阴谋家只是寻求喘息之处或一个家——一个能够归属的地方。阴谋家往往也属于其他群体，因为他们并不认为“阴谋家”这一称呼排斥其他追求。例如，棱魂典范经常结成团体以彼此支援，并教育他人了解变形怪；而纵欲者则利用合并的影响力来获取更多世俗享乐。

阴谋家共同的一点是偏好结构与对更大整体的归属感，因此他们往往比起混乱更倾向守序。尽管如此，混乱的阴谋家同样存在：他们以互利为目的联合在一起，规则松散，让最善操控、最强大的人随心所欲。

无面者

无面者是那些因各自不同的缘由而失去、遗忘，或刻意抛弃自身身份的变形怪。对无面者而言，生命是一条意识之流，唯一的连续性来自经验与记忆。由于缺乏成熟的自我或身份概念，无面者只是人群中一张普通面孔，与所有人混在一起。许多人甚至会随着时间推移放弃偏好形态，或选择一种绝对平淡且令人过目即忘的形态。当他们从一处到另一处时，会采用最不显眼的形态，如同迷失在人潮中的变色龙。由于这些倾向，无面者比其他变形怪更可能生活在高密度的城市区域。他们往往是无比出色的刺客：他们完全普通的外貌，以及近乎冥想般地清空自我，使他们能够在接近刺杀目标或得手后脱身时完美融入人群。

但这并不意味着无面者天生恶毒。事实上，他们更可能是中立而非其他阵营，因为多数阵营所需的信念与坚守，以及邪恶阵营所需的自私或恶意，有时都超出了他们的范围。善良阵营的无面者会匿名行善，不期待被认可或称赞，只是在完成工作后悄然退回人群之中。那些因某种原因加入组织的无面者——例如阴谋家的秘密社团或冒险队伍——很快就会证明自己是优秀成员。由于个人身份感极弱，他们的隶属关系往往会成为替代品，使他们成为相当狂热的拥护者。

人格面具

人格面具是那些最强烈地认同自己并非变形怪（甚至也不认同棱魂或其他族裔名称），而是认同为其偏好形态所对应族裔的变形怪。人格面具已完全成为那个族裔，不仅在变形上如此，也在性格、心理与文化上如此。变形怪可以且常常与其他族裔繁衍后代，而他们的子女也往往仍是变形怪。这些变形怪常常偏好其另一位亲本所属族裔的形态，因此更可能成为人格面具。人格面具倾向于以其偏好形态度过大半生，并轻视变形怪的自然形态，认为那不过是身体能采取的另一种形态。人格面具比其他变形怪更可能觉得变形怪的自然形态令人反感或可耻，尽管这仍只是他们中一部分人的观点。即便如此，也不代表人格面具从不使用变形能力；他们只是把自己视为人类、精灵、矮人或其他族裔的一员，只是恰好拥有变换为多种形态的能力。但对人格面具而言，偏好形态才是真形态，其余皆只是伪装。这让人格面具几乎成为无面者的完全对立面，两者往往相处不佳。

人格面具也是所有变形怪中最不可能成为阴谋家的一类。毕竟，阴谋家观点的核心主张是变形怪应组织起其他变形怪的团体，但人格面具并不怎么把自己当作变形怪，而更把自己当作其偏好形态的族裔。这往往使他们与棱魂典范以及部分不变者也产生冲突。

真·人格面具

人格面具的选择与身份认同如此强烈，以至于他们往往能将某一种形态——其他变形怪仅将其视为偏好形态——强化到魔法与形而上层面成为他们的自然形态，使他们在解除其他形态时总会回到这一形态。与此同时，对他们而言，传统的变形怪自然形态反而只是另一种他们也能轻易采用的形态，就像偏好形态对其他变形怪的通常作用一样。不必多言，这种强大的魔法转变只会在某种信念与形态对某个变形怪的身份如此核心，以至于连魔法法则本身都承认其选择并予以回应时才会发生。它不是变形怪能为自身利益而更改的东西，也不是为了规避真知术或其他别有用心的目的。尽管如此，若这样的人人格面具成为不变者，他们将难以与该族裔的其他成员区分开来。

棱魂典范

棱魂典范以身为变形怪为傲，或如他们更喜欢被称呼的那样——棱魂。他们并不总使用“棱魂”这一名称，因为拒绝这一名称的变形怪并未为自己的族裔达成一个统一称谓。事实上，在远古时代接触其他族裔之前、并被他者赋予“变形怪”这一标签之前，变形怪由于其变形天性，根本不觉得需要一个族裔名称。但即便如此，一个意为“双重行者”的名字，对那些拒绝仅因能成为他人替身而被定义的变形怪而言不可接受。最早对“变形怪”一名感到不满的棱魂典范，选择“棱魂”这一名称是基于棱镜的作用：棱镜折射光，使白光分裂成彩虹中所有可能的色彩。这意味着棱镜依赖光而存在，同时揭示了光如何运作的隐藏秘密。以同样方式，棱魂典范认为变形怪引导并折射周遭的社会，以及他人的思想、情感与动机，从而体现一切可能的形态，并揭示社会运作的隐藏秘密。但棱魂并非仅在折射与揭示物理真相，因此这个合成词中才使用了“魂（soul）”一词。

使用“棱魂”一名的并非只有棱魂典范，而且他们并非都同意这一称呼（因此他们甚至对自己群体的名称也不一致）。然而，他们一致认为变形怪理应得到他人的尊重，并共同将改变其他族裔看待变形怪的方式置于优先地位。他们希望确保他人明白：变形怪拥有独特的文化与世界观，并非只是为了背叛、替换与诈骗其他族裔而存在的冒名顶替者。棱魂典范常常与完全否认变形怪族裔身份的人格面具发生分歧，但大多数棱魂典范也乐于接纳人格面具所选择的身份。与人格面具恰恰相反，棱魂典范最可能向世界展示自己变形怪的自然形态，以宣告自己是谁。他们认为这种形态极其美丽，如同优雅的风格化雕塑，并希望他人也能理解这一点。各种阵营的棱魂典范皆存在；即便是邪恶的变形怪，也可能自尊心强烈，并为自己的族裔不只是“替身族裔”而自豪。不过，他们更常见为善良而非邪恶，更常见为混乱而非守序。

不变者

在所有变形怪群体中最为异类的一支，不变者的动机与理由千差万别，但他们都共有一项使其与其他变形怪截然不同的怪癖：他们无论如何都不改变形态，始终保持变形怪的自然形态。鉴于改变形态是变形怪作为族裔的决定性特征，成为不变者从不会在没有充分理由的情况下发生。许多不变者出于宗教或哲学信念而做出选择，类似于他人可能立下的沉默誓言。也有人这样做是为了表态或推动其目标。例如，一个同时也是棱魂典范的不变者，可能始终保持变形怪形态，以强调棱魂之所以为棱魂，关键在于“是谁”，而不仅是“能变成什么”。

由于必须具备极强的纪律性才能做出如此坚定而绝对的承诺，不变者比起混乱更可能倾向守序。他们也往往不邪恶，因为邪恶个体通常会动用其工具包中的一切手段来推进自私或恶意的目标。

篡位者

篡位者最接近经典印象中的变形怪。他们会把某个人的一切都学得一清二楚，从举止、说话习惯到个人偏好。随后他们会完整复刻那个人，常常甚至走到以冒名者的身份取而代之的地步，并可能杀害对方。大多数人认为这一过程本质上是邪恶的；确实，篡位者是最可能邪恶的变形怪群体之一，因为这种生活方式很容易被用于阴险目的。然而，仍有许多中立的篡位者，甚至还有少数善良者。

例如，纪念录（Memoriam）这一尤为值得注意的善良篡位者阴谋团体，会专门学习如何替代已故或身患绝症之人，作为“活的墓志铭”以纪念其记忆。迄今为止，他们已收集了数量惊人的活墓志铭。

孤身的善良篡位者可能会通过替代逝者来完成阵亡同伴的临终遗愿，以照料其所爱之人并让他们免于失去亲人的悲痛。尽管篡位者的行为背后出于善意，或在某些情况下甚至是应他人请求而为之，许多社会仍认为这种选择的伦理可疑，根本上扎根于欺骗。因此他们认为，无论对家人还是对变形怪而言，维持骗局更为残酷。

从事这类行为的善良篡位者并不这样看待此事；篡位者往往相信自己确实已经体现了目标，甚至吸收了对方真实的一部分进入自身。另一些善良篡位者会以其他方式保护身边的人，例如充当可能遭刺杀目标的替身。替身篡位者有时更进一步：他们协助受保护者采用不起眼的伪装，而自己则伪装成受保护者，使两人能够一同旅行而不让受保护者置于危险之中。这类搭档有时甚至会成为冒险者——当秘密揭开时，常令队伍中的同伴大吃一惊。

纵欲者

纵欲者在所有变形怪中动机最为简单：他们只想享受世上最精致的奢华与欢愉。纵欲者通常会为自己创造一个性感或迷人的偏好形态，花上数百小时对镜揣摩，将偏好形态微调为完美的化身。然而情况并非总是如此，因为纵欲者也很容易同时属于其他偏好采用不同类型形态的群体。例如，篡位者纵欲者进行身份盗用，主要是为了沉溺于目标所拥有的财富与享乐，并在转移前卷走贵重财物。任何族裔都可能对纵欲者追求的目标感兴趣，但变形怪族裔为他们提供了独特的机会去追逐这些目标。能改变身心成为任何人的理想伴侣，使他们成为诱惑的典范；在缺乏道德约束的情况下，获取财富也变得轻而易举。由于纵欲者的目标从根本上是自私的，他们往往偏向邪恶，但各种阵营的纵欲者仍然存在。善良的纵欲者只是不允许自己对欲望的追逐凌驾于他人的福祉之上。

超小型（体型）PC

PC通常为小型或中型（体型），但拥有 @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-feats.Item.scwc8liB6xUBkrDI]{Tiny Shift} 专长后，你可以改为超小型（体型）！成为超小型（体型）会带来一套关于空间与触及的规则。超小型（体型）PC可以进入另一名生物的空间——这很重要，因为其近战打击通常没有触及，这意味着PC必须进入生物的空间才能攻击。和其他超小型（体型）生物一样，PC在更大生物的空间中并不会自动获得次等掩蔽，但情境可能允许他们在此情况下 @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.ust1jJSCZQUhBZIz]{Take Cover}。他们可以为其体型购买武器、护甲与其他物品，其数据与正常道具相同，但近战武器对他们而言触及为0（若武器具有触及特征，则其触及比正常短5尺）。记得为超小型（体型）调整物品的负重以及PC的负重上限。

阵营与宗教

变形怪灵活的观点使他们拥有各种阵营，尽管追随刻板印象式变形怪生活方式（冒充他人）的变形怪几乎不会是善良，反而倾向于任何中立或邪恶阵营。然而，作为冒险者的变形怪，或那些尝试以不同方式生活的非冒险者，更可能为善良而非邪恶。毕竟，变形怪天生的技能组合让他们以那种方式生活、拿走自己想要的东西变得太容易；因此做出不同选择的变形怪通常是有原因的。尽管如此，这并不意味着没有也以自己的理由选择不同道路的邪恶变形怪。一些变形怪若将某个掩蔽身份维持过久，可能会发现自己的阵营逐渐漂移，趋向与该掩蔽相一致。

变形怪对神祇的信仰同样多样，因此许多变形怪会根据自身处境、形态与情境崇拜不同的神祇与信仰。那些倾向把信仰集中在单一神祇上的变形怪，通常会选择：要么专注于变形或变化、能理解变形怪视角的神祇；要么若变形怪也在日常生活中拥抱诡计与欺骗策略，则选择擅长诡计或欺诈的神祇。在阿拉卡尔（Alacar），无赖赛瓦（Sewa the Rogue）深受喜爱；一些变形怪认为她是其族裔的始祖，尽管另一些认为这不可能，并坚信在这位女神初诞之前他们就早已存在。

变形怪冒险者

变形怪冒险者倾向选择能最大化其变形能力的职业。游荡者与吟游诗人尤为常见，其次是其他以魅力为主的施法者。由于一些变形怪拥有先天的心灵魔法，灵能者职业也相对常见。在变形怪之中，几乎任何背景都可能出现，尤其是那些曾一度假扮他人身份者（往往更富有或更具影响力）。典型背景包括：艺术家、理发师、酒保、江湖骗子、罪犯、侦探、艺人、占卜师、商人、贵族、破坏者、仆从或街头孤儿。

名字

许多变形怪会随兴而改名，或为适应其当前形态与处境而改名。他们往往有某种化名或代号，方便他人联系他们或雇佣他们完成工作。有时他们连这些也没有，使自己成为迷失在诸多伪装中的彻底幽灵。另一些则用名字来锚定身份，让自己与当前所具备的形态保持区分。对自己的名字感到自豪的变形怪，可能会把它编入各种化名之中。例如，若他们自称琳（Rin），他们可能以马厩男孩塔琳（Tarin）、贵族女士米尔琳（Myrrhine）以及侍者卡特琳娜（Katrina）的身份出现。

示例名字

317、Facade、Mar、Oma、Rin、Twilight、Zenos。

变形怪传承

变形怪永远在改变与转化，因此他们多年来发展出多种传承也不足为奇，每一种都能做到不同的事。你在1级时从以下变形怪传承中选择其一。

刃爪变形怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.8ctk3pb9JrG4zPS8]{Bladeclaw}

无论你当前为何种形态，你都能将双手转化为剃刀般锋利的利爪。在单手或双手上在“有爪”与“无爪”之间切换是一个单动作，并具有专注（特征）。当你任一只手有爪时，你获得一次爪的徒手攻击，造成1d6挥砍伤害。你的爪属于斗殴武器组，并具有灵巧与徒手特征。

暗移变形怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.8Mpjlv1giIVOv0AQ]{Darkshift}

你的双眼能够转变以适应任何程度的光照，甚至完全无光。你获得黑暗视觉。

心触变形怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.64GunSTjLuxNk5p2]{Mindtap}

你拥有轻微的共感能力，使你能轻松读懂社交情势并与他人打成一片。你可以将 @Compendium[pf2e.spells-srd.lg73SvJZno1ypPAj]{Read the Air} 作为先天奥术戏法施放。

三重变形怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.2MJPQ8naGAf0zlT5]{Thricefold}

自你出生起，学习多种形态对你而言一直很容易。你的变形怪伪装能力拥有三个偏好形态，而非一个。

Doppelgangers, or as some prefer to be known as, prismsouls, are a people with many faces and none at all. Amidst their various guises and transformations, underneath their masks, many doppelgangers seek their own distinct identity. Others are happy to forget such things and embrace the moment in each shift.

While doppelgangers are quite rare, they are still present in almost every humanoid society across the world. Even so, they often remain unnoticed due mainly to their shapeshifting abilities, and secondarily due to the fact that they rarely form settlements or organizations composed strictly of doppelgangers. The hallmark of a doppelganger is the ability to change their form into that of other humanoids and move about society as they please, a potent ability that is the ancestry's greatest blessing, but according to some doppelgangers' views, also their greatest curse.

The stereotypical doppelganger is an amoral opportunist at best, using their shapeshifting capabilities to infiltrate, exploit, and otherwise deceive other people to their own advantage. Indeed, their powers make it easy to live a comfortable life in this fashion, albeit a risky one. However, some doppelgangers even find it enjoyable and satisfying to do so, feeling a rush whenever they complete a con.

However, there are many other sorts of doppelgangers in the world. Among them, there are those who wish to find their own paths to follow through life, rather than simply sliding into a borrowed life of impersonation and betrayal. These nontraditional doppelgangers don't have a single unifying belief beyond that central tenet, however. They each have their own unique perspective to share.

If you want to play a character capable of changing forms into various humanoid shapes with the potential to explore the meaning of identity and self as someone who can reinvent themselves with ease, you should play a doppelganger.

You Might...

  • Change forms as part of expressing your mood, whims, or aesthetics.
  • Begin to adopt various personality traits and other mental features of the form you choose, "becoming the mask."
  • Default to plans or strategies involving impersonation.

Others Probably...

  • Assume you are capable of any sort of infiltration or subterfuge they need.
  • Worry that you might take their form after learning their mannerisms or personal information.
  • Aren't even aware that you're a doppelganger in the first place.

Physical Description

In their natural form, doppelgangers have smooth flesh of an indeterminate and subdued color. Some doppelgangers' flesh is almost close to the skin tone of other humanoid ancestries, while others are gray, white, silvery, or other shades that other humanoids find "unnatural."

Doppelgangers' bodies, especially their faces, possess stylized features without any smaller details such as freckles, hairs, and other such things. Some members of other ancestries, including scholars who study doppelgangers, claim that this natural form appears like an in-process painting or sculpture that wasn't quite finished, left with one step incomplete by a cosmic artist.

Many doppelgangers, however, tend to find this perspective highly insulting and inaccurate. The very idea of their appearance being "incomplete" is centered around the idea that only humans and their kin are "complete" and that being different means that doppelgangers must be lacking in various ways. Those doppelgangers who agree with the scholars' characterization include some who simply don't care about the negative implications of being "unfinished," as well as those who agree with and deeply care about the implications, as they are ashamed of their natural forms. Those last doppelgangers sometimes treat their natural forms with disgust and tend to either wear the forms of others all the time or to artistically craft their own idealized, often beautiful, humanoid form for themselves, which they consider to be their true form instead.

Doppelganger Settlements

Doppelgangers don’t build settlements of their own, and they rarely even form subcommunities within a larger settlement of other humanoids. However, when doppelgangers do gather, typically due to the plans of conspiracists, their social structures tend to shift and change depending on the context, in a way that is difficult for those of other ancestries to understand. Sometimes an entire settlement of predominantly another ancestry or ancestries will be unknowingly ruled by a doppelganger for a time, in which case the doppelganger might exercise some of their own preferences upon the settlement’s organization and hierarchy.

Society

Doppelgangers don't possess just one single unifying society, and a fair number of doppelgangers barely know any other members of their ancestry beyond their own parents. This leads to doppelgangers having a wide variety of lifestyles and social dynamics. While each of the following groups represents the perspectives of a significant number of doppelgangers, none of them is a monolithic bloc or social club that meets and shares goals or progress reports. In fact, only conspiracists (by their very nature) and some prismsoul exemplars tend to form large groups of like-minded individuals. These groups aren't all mutually exclusive; in particular, conspiracists are often members of other groups.

Groups

While each of the following groups represents the perspectives of a significant number of doppelgangers, none of them is a monolithic bloc or social club that meets and shares goals or progress reports. In fact, only conspiracists (by their very nature) and some prismsoul exemplars tend to form large groups of like-minded individuals. These groups aren't all mutually exclusive; in particular, conspiracists are often members of other groups.

Conspiracists

Conspiracists are the few doppelgangers who want to organize other doppelgangers into a faction, secret society, or the like. Despite the name, which evokes shadowy dealings and underhanded conspiracy, conspiracists have a variety of motives and a broad swathe of visions for what their ideal doppelganger organization would be. Certainly, some conspiracists are nefarious, with goals ranging from secret domination of an entire settlement or nation, to the acquisition of wealth and power or to the promulgation of a malevolent religion. However, other conspiracists simply seek a respite or a home, a place in which to belong. conspiracists tend to be members of other groups in addition to conspiracists, as they don't consider the designation as a conspiracist exclusive of other pursuits. For instance, prismsoul exemplars often band together to support each other and to educate others about doppelgangers, while voluptuaries use their combined influence in order to acquire more worldly pleasures.

One thing conspiracists have in common is that they prefer structure and belonging to something larger, which means they tend to be more lawful than chaotic. Still, even chaotic conspiracists exist, uniting for mutual benefit with loose rules that allow those who are the most manipulative and powerful to have their way.

Faceless

Faceless are doppelgangers who, through various circumstances distinct between each faceless, have lost, forgotten, or intentionally disposed of their identity. To a faceless, life is a stream of consciousness, with the only continuity being that of experience and memory. Without a developed concept of self or identity, faceless simply become a generic face in the crowd, blending in with all the others. Many even drop their preferred form over time or choose one that is absolutely bland and forgettable. As they move from place to place, they take a form that stands out the least, like a chameleon lost amidst the masses. Because of these tendencies, faceless are even more likely to live in densely populated urban areas than other doppelgangers. They tend to make absolutely superb assassins, as their completely generic appearance and almost meditative emptying of self allow them to blend in for the approach to the assassination target or the getaway after getting the job done.

That's not to say that faceless are inherently malicious, however. In fact, they are much more likely to be neutral than they are to be other alignments, as the beliefs and convictions necessary for most alignments, and the selfishness or malice necessary for evil alignments, are sometimes beyond them. Good- aligned faceless perform anonymous good deeds with no expectation of recognition or praise, simply slipping back into the crowd after a job well done. Faceless who, for whatever reason, fall in with an organization, such as a conspiracist's secret society or an adventuring party, quickly reveal themselves to be excellent members. With such a weak sense of personal identity, their affiliation manages to function as a replacement, causing them to be rather zealous adherents.

Personas

Personas are doppelgangers who identify most strongly not as doppelgangers (or even prismsouls or another different name for the ancestry) but instead as the ancestry of their preferred form. Personas have become that ancestry completely, not just through shapeshifting but also through personality, psychology, and culture. Doppelgangers can and often do reproduce with other ancestries, with their children tending to be doppelgangers as well. Those doppelgangers often have a preferred form of their other parent's ancestry, and they are more likely to be personas. Personas tend to spend most of their life in their preferred form, and they look down on their natural doppelganger form as merely another form their body can take. Personas are more likely than other doppelgangers to find the natural doppelganger form to be repulsive or shameful, though that's still only an opinion held by some of them. Even so, that doesn't mean personas never use their shapeshifting abilities. They just see themselves as a human, elf, dwarf, or member of another ancestry who happen to have the ability to change their shape into various others. But to a persona, their preferred form is their true form, and the rest are merely disguises. This makes personas nearly the complete opposite of faceless, and the two don't tend to get along well.

Personas are also the least likely of any doppelgangers to become conspiracists. After all, the central thesis of the conspiracist perspective is that doppelgangers should organize groups of other doppelgangers, but personas don't really see themselves as doppelgangers as much as they are their preferred form's ancestry. This often puts them at odds with prismsoul exemplars and some unchanging as well.

True Persona

Personas' choices and identities are so strong that they are often capable of adopting the single form that other doppelgangers treat as their preferred form, to the point that it magically and metaphysically becomes their natural form, which they always return to when dismissing other forms. Meanwhile, for them, the traditional natural doppelganger form is simply another form they can easily adopt, just like the preferred form normally functions for other doppelgangers. Needless to say, this powerful magical shift only happens due to a belief and form so central to a doppelganger's identity that the laws of magic themselves accept their choice in answer. It isn't the kind of thing a doppelganger can change for their own advantage, to evade true seeing, or for other ulterior motives. Nonetheless, if such a persona becomes unchanging, they can't easily be distinguished from other members of that ancestry.

Prismsoul Exemplars

Prismsoul exemplars are proud to be doppelgangers, or prismsouls as they prefer to be called. They don't always use the name prismsoul, as doppelgangers who reject that name haven't really settled on a single name for their ancestry. In truth, before meeting other ancestries in ancient times and having the label of doppelganger chosen for them, doppelgangers' shapeshifting nature meant they hadn't seen a need for an ancestry name for themselves at all. But still, a name meaning "double goer" isn't acceptable to those doppelgangers who refuse to be defined merely for the fact that they can become someone else's double. Those early prismsoul exemplars who first took umbrage to the name doppelganger chose the name prismsoul based on the function of prisms. Prisms refract light, allowing white light to be split into light with every possible color of the rainbow. This means prisms function only because of light while revealing the hidden secrets of how light functions. In the same way, prismsoul exemplars feel that doppelgangers channel and refract society around them and other people's thoughts, feelings, and motivations to embody every possible form, revealing the hidden secrets of how society functions. But a prismsoul isn't merely refracting and revealing physical truths, hence the use of the term "soul" in the portmanteau.

Prismsoul exemplars aren't the only doppelgangers who use the name prismsoul, and they don't all agree with it (and thus don't even agree on a name for their group). However, they do agree that doppelgangers deserve respect from others, and they share a deep priority towards changing the way other ancestries view doppelgangers. They want to make sure others understand that doppelgangers share their own unique culture and worldview and that they aren't simply impostors who exist to betray, replace, and scam other ancestries. Prismsoul exemplars often take issue with personas, who abjure the doppelganger ancestry entirely, but most other prismsoul exemplars are happy to accept the personas' chosen identity. Quite the contrary to personas, prismsoul exemplars are the most likely to show off their natural doppelganger form to the world to announce who they are. They consider this form to be incredibly beautiful, like an elegant stylized sculpture, and they want others to be able to understand that too. Prismsoul exemplars of every alignment exist; even evil doppelgangers can have a strong ego and pride in their ancestry as more than just an ancestry of replacements. However, they tend to be good more often than evil and chaotic more often than lawful.

Unchanging

The most unusual of all doppelganger groups, unchanging have a wide variety of motivations or reasons, but they all share a quirk that sets them apart from all other doppelgangers. They don't change forms, no matter what, remaining in their natural doppelganger form. Given that changing forms is a defining characteristic of doppelgangers as an ancestry, becoming an unchanging never happens without a very good reason. Many unchanging make their choice due to a religious or philosophical belief, akin to how others might take a vow of silence. Still, others do so to make a point or to promote their goals. For instance, an unchanging who is also a prismsoul exemplar might remain in their doppelganger form at all times in order to make the point that what makes a prismsoul is who they are, not just the ability to change shapes.

Due to just how disciplined a doppelganger must be in order to make such a strong and absolute commitment, unchanging are more likely to be lawful than chaotic. They also tend not to be evil, simply because evil individuals tend to use every possible means in their toolkit to further their selfish or malevolent goals.

Usurpers

Usurpers are the most similar to the classic conception of a doppelganger. They learn everything there is to know about someone, down to their mannerisms, speech patterns, and personal preferences. Then they replicate that person completely, often going so far as to replace them as an impostor, and possibly even murder them. Most people think of this process as inherently evil, and it's true that usurpers are among the most likely doppelganger groups to be evil, since this lifestyle lends itself to nefarious purposes. However, there are many neutral usurpers, and even a few who are good.

For instance, Memoriam, one particularly noteworthy conspiracy of good usurpers specifically learns how to replace deceased or terminally ill people as a living epitaph in order to honor their memory. And to date, they've collected an incredible number of living epitaphs.

Solitary good usurpers might fulfill a fallen comrade's dying wish by replacing the deceased in order to take care of their loved ones and spare them the grief of their loss. Despite the good intentions behind the usurper's actions, or in some cases even the request of others to do so, many societies consider the ethics of such a choice to be questionable and rooted fundamentally in deception. Thus, they say, it's crueler to both the family members and the doppelganger to perpetuate the ruse.

Good usurpers who engage in such practices don't see it that way, as usurpers often believe that they have truly come to embody their target, even to the point of absorbing a part of their truth into themselves. Other good usurpers help protect those around them by other means, such as by acting as body doubles for likely assassination targets. Body double usurpers sometimes take things a step further by helping their ward adopt an unassuming disguise while they disguise as their ward, thus allowing them to travel together without putting the ward at risk. Such pairs even sometimes become adventurers, much to the surprise of their fellow party members once the secret is revealed.

Voluptuaries

Voluptuaries have the simplest motivation out of all doppelgangers: they simply wish to enjoy the finest luxuries and pleasures the world has to offer. Voluptuaries typically create a sensual or attractive preferred form for themselves, spending hundreds of hours looking at a mirror and tweaking their favored form into a vision of perfection. That isn't always the case, however, as voluptuaries can easily be members of another group that prefers adopting a different sort of form or forms. For instance, usurper voluptuaries perform identity theft mainly to indulge in the wealth and pleasures available to their targets and abscond with their valuables before moving on. While any ancestry can be interested in the same sort of goals that voluptuaries seek, their doppelganger ancestry provides them unique opportunities to pursue those goals. The ability to alter their mind and body to become anyone's ideal partner makes them paragons of seduction, and in the absence of morals, it's easy to acquire wealth. Given that the goal of a voluptuary is fundamentally selfish, they tend towards evil, but voluptuaries of every alignment still exist. Good voluptuaries simply don't allow their pursuit of their own desires to come before others' wellbeing.

Tiny PCs

PCs are typically Small or Medium size, but with the @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-feats.Item.scwc8liB6xUBkrDI]{Tiny Shift} feat, you can become Tiny instead! Being Tiny comes with its own set of rules about space and reach. A Tiny PC can enter another creature's space, which is important because their melee Strikes typically have no reach, meaning the PC must enter a creature's space to attack it. Like other Tiny creatures, the PC doesn't automatically receive lesser cover from being in a larger creature's space, but circumstances might allow them to @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.ust1jJSCZQUhBZIz]{Take Cover} in this instance. They can purchase weapons, armor, and other items for their size with the same statistics as normal gear, except that melee weapons have a reach of 0 for them (or a reach 5 feet shorter than normal if they have the reach trait). Remember to adjust the Bulk of items and the PC's Bulk limit for Tiny size.

Alignment and Religion

Doppelgangers' flexible viewpoints lead them to have all sorts of alignments, although very few doppelgangers who pursue the stereotypical doppelganger lifestyle of impersonating others are good, instead tending towards any of the neutral or evil alignments. However, adventuring doppelgangers, or those non-adventurers who try to live different sorts of lives, are more likely to be good than evil. After all, a doppelganger's natural skill set makes it so easy to live life in that way and take what you want that doppelgangers who make different choices usually do so for a reason. Still, that's not to say there aren't also evil doppelgangers who have their own reasons for doing things differently. Some doppelgangers who adopt a cover identity for too long can find their alignment drifting towards matching their cover.

Doppelgangers' faith in deities is similarly diverse, and thus many doppelgangers worship a variety of deities and faiths depending on their circumstances, forms, and situations. Those doppelgangers who tend to focus their faith on a single deity typically choose either deities with a focus on shapeshifting or change who can understand a doppelganger's perspective, or those who specialize in trickery or deception if the doppelganger also embraces those tactics in their daily life. On Alacar, Sewa the Rogue is a major favorite, and some doppelgangers consider her to be the progenitor of their ancestry, though others believe that's impossible and that they existed long before the goddess was first born.

Doppelganger Adventurers

Doppelganger adventurers tend to choose classes that allow them to make the most of their shapeshifting abilities. Rogues and bards are especially common, with other Charisma-based spellcasters secondary. Since some doppelgangers have innate mental magic, the psychic class is relatively common as well. Among doppelgangers, almost any background is possible, especially for those who assumed another identity for a time (which tend to be wealthier or more influential). Typical backgrounds include artist, barber, barkeep, charlatan, criminal, detective, entertainer, fortune teller, merchant, noble, saboteur, servant, or street urchin.

Names

Many doppelgangers change their names on a whim or to suit their current forms and situations. They often have some sort of alias or call sign they can use if they want others to be able to contact them or hire them for a job. Sometimes they don't have even that, making them a complete ghost lost among their various guises. Others use a name to center their identity and keep themselves separate and distinct from whatever form they currently possess. A doppelganger proud of their name might weave it into their various aliases. For instance, if they call themselves Rin, they might appear as the stable boy Tarin, the noble lady Myrrhine, and the server Katrina.

Sample Names

317, Facade, Mar, Oma, Rin, Twilight, Zenos.

Doppelganger Heritages

Doppelgangers are always shifting and changing, and so it's no surprise that they've developed various heritages over the years, each capable of different things. Choose one of the following doppelganger heritages at 1st level.

Bladeclaw Doppelganger

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.8ctk3pb9JrG4zPS8]{Bladeclaw}

You can shift your hands into razor sharp claws, regardless of your current form. Shifting between having a claw and not having a claw on one or both hands is a single action, which has the concentrate trait. While you have a claw on either hand, you gain a claw unarmed attack that deals 1d6 slashing damage. Your claw is in the brawling weapon group and has the finesse and unarmed traits.

Darkshift Doppelganger

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.8Mpjlv1giIVOv0AQ]{Darkshift}

Your eyes are capable of shifting in order to adjust to any amount of light, or even no light at all. You gain darkvision.

Mindtap Doppelganger

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.64GunSTjLuxNk5p2]{Mindtap}

You have mild empathic abilities, allowing you to read the social situation with ease and ingratiate yourselves with others. You can cast @Compendium[pf2e.spells-srd.lg73SvJZno1ypPAj]{Read the Air} as an innate arcane cantrip.

Thricefold Doppelganger

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.2MJPQ8naGAf0zlT5]{Thricefold}

It's always been easy for you to learn multiple forms ever since you were born. You have three preferred forms for your doppelganger's disguise ability, instead of one.

When a Prison Becomes a Person.name: 当一座监狱成为一个人 When a Prison Becomes a Person
When a Prison Becomes a Person.text:
摘自艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯的档案
当一座监狱成为一个人

自我是什么？哲学家们提出这个问题已逾千年。若有人将你的心智与意识转移到一具人造躯体中，而你原本的身体——连同驱动它的生命活力——仍以一种新的意识活着，那么哪一个才是“你”？哪一个保有你的身份？答案会因灵魂的移动而改变吗？多数学者认为，你是身体、心智、精神与生命力合而为一的组合，因此这些组合中的任何一种都不会精准地等同于“你”；就像有人被转化为不死生物后，往往会与其生前的行为产生剧烈变化。为何我要你思考这些问题？因为我想让你逐步理解一个难以把握的概念。当我们思考自我时，通常想到的是一个身体，就像我们每个人都有的那样。但倘若一栋建筑拥有意识，并且形成了自我的概念呢？那若是一整座地城复合体又如何？

这个想法是否让你嗤之以鼻？我向你保证，这是一种真实的现象。关于异次元地城借由强大的魔法核心而获得知性的可靠报告不胜枚举；该核心使它们能够派出一个傀儡般的身体，或称“化身”，其命运与地城自身相连。若核心死亡，化身便会死，反之亦然。无论你愿不愿意相信我，都请稍作片刻思考：这样的揭示对你自身的自我概念意味着什么？它会改变你的想法，或动摇你本能的定义吗？真正的智慧地城——每一座都拥有单一、统一的心智、生命力与灵魂。它们如何诞生？看来答案会因地城而异。我曾设法通过信使与某座地城的化身通信，对方向我讲述了各式各样的故事。举例来说，有些地城与其核心一同进化出知性，源自一名强大生物在其内部死去时残存的意志；而最终出现的化身，也与那生物有几分相似。另一些地城则完全是拥有自身个性的全新存在。但即便如此，它们也未必是真正的白纸。地城之中许多生物的生命力成分，可能会对新生地城的个性与形态产生影响。现存的知性地城数量仍不足以检验或判定真相——至少据我们所知如此。

我无法在没有证据的情况下要求你信任我。我唯一能做的，是请求你保持开放的心态。这不是诡计、把戏或玩笑。地城正在觉醒。

——艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物的倡议者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
When a Prison Becomes a Person

What is the self? Philosophers have asked that question for millennia. If they moved your mind and consciousness into a constructed body while your own body, with its animating vital force, remained alive with a new consciousness, which one is "you?" Which one retains your identity? Does the answer change based on the movement of the soul? Most scholars believe that you are a combination of body, mind, spirit, and life force all in one, so none of those combinations would precisely be "you," in the same way that someone transformed into an undead often changes their behavior dramatically from their old life. Why am I asking you to think about these questions? Because I’d like to ease you into a difficult concept to understand. When we think of a self, we usually think of a body, like each of us has. But what if a building was conscious and had a conception of self? What about an entire dungeon complex?

Does the idea make you scoff? I assure you, this is a real phenomenon. There are numerous credible reports of extradimensional dungeons gaining sapience through a powerful magic core that allows them to send out a puppet body, or "avatar," whose fate is linked to the dungeon’s own. If the core dies, the avatar dies, and vice versa. Whether you’re willing to believe me or not, just think for a moment about what that revelation would mean for your own conception of self. Does it change your mind or alter your instinctual definition? Real intelligent dungeons, each with a single unifying mind, life force, and soul. How did they arise? It seems that the answer is different depending on the dungeon. I’ve managed to correspond with a dungeon’s avatar via messenger, and they told me all sorts of stories. For instance, some dungeons evolved sapience and a core together with the dying will of a powerful creature who perished inside, bearing a resemblance to the avatar who eventually emerged. Other dungeons are entirely new beings with their own personalities. But even then, they aren’t necessarily true tabulae rasae. Some components of the life forces of many creatures within the dungeon might have an influence on the new dungeon’s personality and form. There haven’t been enough sapient dungeons in existence to test or determine the truth, at least not that we know of.

I can’t ask you to trust me on this without evidence. All I can do is request that you keep an open mind. This isn’t a ruse, a trick, or a joke. The dungeons are awakening.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Dungeon Ancestry.name: 地城 族裔 Dungeon Ancestry
Dungeon Ancestry.text:
While originally synonymous with a jail or prison, the word dungeon has come to mean something altogether more dangerous and expansive. Ancient labyrinths filled with puzzles, traps, and monsters; mystical towers stretching into the sky above; cavern systems echoing with the sounds of distant danger—all of these and more are considered dungeons. A dungeon has come to mean just about any natural or artificial complex full of treasures and dangers. But to you, a dungeon means even more than that. A dungeon is also who you are.

While most dungeons, no matter how magical, are nothing more than buildings or natural formations, some dungeons are special. Through a set of strange circumstances, sometimes a dungeon awakens to sentience, and then to full sapience, after gaining a life force and soul. This could have been something the dungeon gained on its own, as an aftereffect from either the spirit of the dungeon’s builder, or vitae ("so-called nature spirits") or even eldamon for natural dungeons. Alternatively, it could have happened when the dungeon’s core, heart, or focal point absorbed the life force and spirit of an adventuring interloper or dungeon denizen. However a dungeon’s awakening occurred, an awakened dungeon is a living, albeit unusual, creature with an enormous body shaped out of stone or other material, its arteries and veins made up of magical conduits or ley lines.

Of course, awakened dungeons can’t get much done outside of their own environs if they leave it at that. So eventually, each awakened dungeon becomes able to manifest an avatar; it’s a rite of passage for awakened dungeons, similar to reaching adulthood for other creatures. This avatar is formed from the dungeon’s core and appears to be a member of a humanoid ancestry. At the same time, the awakened dungeon becomes extradimensional, if it wasn’t already, inextricably linking access to the dungeon to the avatar’s whims. A dungeon’s avatar can even leave the dungeon and explore the world, much like any other character.

If you want to play a character who is also secretly (or overtly) a dungeon that only you, and the ones you choose, can enter, you should play a dungeon.

You Might...

  • Be extremely interested in learning about the architectural styles and aesthetics of other dungeons, even blushing at times when you see an extremely beautifully designed dungeon.
  • Treat even unawakened dungeons with great respect, glaring at comrades whose solution to a maze is to bash down the wall with an adamantine pickaxe. You never know if the dungeon will awaken later, and it’s just good manners.
  • At times seem distant and detached to others who are used to thinking as a single person, rather than an entire dungeon.

Others Probably...

  • Don’t realize that your avatar isn’t just "you" without being reminded constantly.
  • Are highly confused when you try to tell them that you are really a dungeon, even after an explanation.
  • Appreciate the hidden mysteries you show them, once you trust them enough to let them in.

Physical Description

Dungeon avatars look like normal members of the ancestry they appear to be, though there is always an unusual feature or two that gives a hint as to the true nature of the dungeon within. As to the dungeons themselves, their physical appearances vary dramatically between heritages, and even within the same heritage from dungeon to dungeon. No two are alike. Dungeons as physical edifices don’t have the same senses as other creatures, and they don’t have the ability to move themselves except via their avatars. In this way, dungeons can still be surprised by their own contents, and most categories of dungeons don’t have a good sense of what they contain until they conquer each floor.

Dungeon Adventurers

Dungeons are likely to be pretty much any class, but the way they like to collect all sorts of things fits well with the thaumaturge class, and they appreciate the way rangers and summoners can lay traps or send out creatures to fight for them. Dungeons tend to have really unusual backgrounds, even rare backgrounds such as amnesiac, given that they are literally dungeons. That said, many dungeons, after having finally manifested their avatar, spend some time learning about their avatar and the world around them in a way that serves as a more mundane background, such as scholar for those who study about themselves, or any occupation for those who prefer to learn by doing.

Society

Sapient dungeons are exceptionally rare, and many never realize that their circumstances aren't unique. That said, there are still a variety of societal and psychological tendencies in dungeons that fall into one of seven categories.

Categories

These categories are relevant even to dungeons without peers because dungeons, unlike other characters, have not only the possibility of an external society but also an internal society within the dungeon itself. Each of these categories of dungeons has information on their personalities and societal implications, as well as a section on the Dungeon Within that provides tips for the GM on potential ways to construct the inner dungeons for PC dungeons of that category.

Enthusiasts

No other categories of dungeons are quite as excited about the sheer thrill of creating dungeon challenges and then overcoming them creatively as enthusiasts, or dungeons who are into the idea of dungeon delving as some kind of extreme sport. Enthusiast PCs have an unusual interaction with their inner dungeon, as they seek to overcome the challenges fairly through skill and wit, but they can’t do that if they know what’s coming. Thus, enthusiasts tend to sometimes form a binary dungeon core, where the PC core is a separated part of the dungeon’s sapience designed to test the challenges, while the dungeon has a corresponding second core that creates the challenges. Enthusiasts are also some of the only dungeons to network with other dungeons, so sometimes the true situation is a little different for them than it is for other dungeon characters: an enthusiast might challenge another dungeon to a "dungeon competition," wherein they perform a ritual linking their dungeons such that each of them can enter the other’s dungeon with their adventuring party, instead of their own dungeon. That would mean that the dungeon accessible via the dungeon within is actually their friend’s dungeon, and it might establish a rivalry wherein they try to defeat that dungeon before their friend defeats theirs. Enthusiasts can be of any alignment, though they’re slightly more likely to be chaotic.

The Dungeon Within: As a GM, the first step for an enthusiast dungeon is to decide whether the dungeon was designed by another core from the PC dungeon’s own dungeon or a friend or rival dungeon that challenged each other via a ritual. Either way, the dungeon is sure to have challenges. Often these are especially tricky traps, mazes, and other gimmicks designed to test the invaders’ ingenuity, but for some dungeons, it might be a matter of pure strength, with the hardest of the hardest battles and a straightforward layout. It all depends on the dungeon’s personality. In fact, with the idea of dungeon competitions, you could build an entire campaign around contests of dungeon characters challenging each others’ dungeons with various bets and rewards involved. Typically, the thrill of the challenge itself is part of the reward for an enthusiast, but they can rarely recruit allies for the promise of thrill alone, so special treasures found in the dungeon or bets to be paid out on completion make great rewards for an enthusiast’s dungeon. Bonus points if the reward is something that gets the recipients especially excited, as enthusiasts are all about the excitement. Just about any dungeon-delving game is good inspiration for an enthusiast’s dungeon, but especially useful are those involving a mix of combat and puzzles.

Fragments

Whether it's a creature, a plane, a magical force, or something even more esoteric, fragment dungeons have either their consciousness or their entire existence composed of shards of some other entity. In general, this means that fragment dungeons awaken with little or no awareness of that original entity and only slowly put the pieces together as they venture through their inner dungeon, building themselves up like a jigsaw puzzle. This makes them somewhat parallel to quandaries and psyches, but they aren't exploring pieces of their own mind, and the puzzle they're fitting together is one of how the fragments fit together, which usually isn't as esoteric or unfathomable as a quandary. As the fragments gather, floor by floor, things become clearer and clearer, and the fragment dungeon's mind and personality might change and grow as more and more of their underlying source comes into focus. Fragments have alignments that tend toward their original source, but sometimes the source is a secret even to the dungeon themselves (and the dungeon's player!). Fortunately, fragments can be of any alignment, even one diametrically opposed to the origin; in that case, absorbing the fragments might risk exposing the dungeon's avatar to an alignment shift toward the original or a personality clash.

The Dungeon Within: When GMing for a fragment dungeon PC, you'll want to decide on the source of the fragments, even if that's a secret from the players. This allows the fragment PC to potentially be connected to the mysteries in the campaign's plot even more than other dungeons would be. Is the fragment PC formed from a shattered demiplane? The villain's dead brother? The villain's lost capacity to love? The options are endless. From there, you can design floors based on discovering an interesting fragment of the whole, centering the theme of the floor around the fragment that the PCs will find at the end. Rewards for clearing floors generally depend on what sort of entity left the fragments. If it's a person, it might make sense to gain knowledge or capabilities; one possibility is to use the free archetype variant from the Pathfinder Gamemastery Guide, but with a twist: the characters only unlock the next free archetype feat after both reaching the appropriate character level (2, 4, 6, and so on) and clearing the next floor of the dungeon within and collecting the fragments. This works especially well if the free archetype is shared or otherwise connected to the source of the fragments. For instance, if the fragments came from a temporal anomaly or piece of the Dimension of Time, clearing floors might grant free archetype feats from the characters' choice of chronoskimmer or time mage from Pathfinder Dark Archive.

...and Dragons?

Awakened dungeons often travel alongside a variety of adventurers in groups containing a wide variety of other ancestries. However. whenever they're found alongside aeternal dragons, if they're to be successful and avoid trouble, typically one or more of them need to have the barrister background or otherwise learn the Legal Lore skill. Otherwise, if they're not careful about the exact name of their adventuring party or how they describe themselves in any bard songs, travelogues, or other publications, their adventures often end up mired in legal disputes until they're thrown into an entirely different sort of dungeon.

Museums

Museum dungeons are collectors, gathering all sorts of strange treasures and curios from across the world and sending them into the dungeon within, where they have mysterious and unpredictable effects, growing the dungeon and adding new features. In some ways, museums are parallels to soul prisons, but there are several important distinctions. The most obvious is that they collect items and objects of power rather than people. However, museums usually collect these objects intentionally, whereas soul prisons typically collect echoes of their defeated foes whether they want to or not. Museums tend toward a neutral alignment, but if they hold cursed, sanctified, or intelligent items, they might have an alignment associated with the items.

The Dungeon Within: As the GM, in some ways a museum requires the most coordination with the rest of your campaign out of all the dungeon categories. This is because they work best when the group is discovering interesting curios that serve as vague items of power for the dungeon, as most parties wouldn't be too keen with the idea of sacrificing usable magic items or other such loot to the dungeon's museum collection. A museum's dungeon within is constantly expanding as each new item added creates new floors, features, and other additions to the dungeon. This makes even otherwise uninteresting set dressing loot into an exciting new addition to the campaign, as, for instance, that ornate hourglass the party found might add a sandy desert floor to the dungeon with strange time magic effects. As a reward for clearing the floor, the objects in the museum might gain special powers and be returned to the PCs; for instance, the hourglass might become a relic or become capable of casting time magic spells. Another possibility is to use the museum as a hoard of power (Battlezoo Ancestries: Dragons page 126), granting the dungeon PC special powers the more they build up the value of the museum and defeat each floor; in that case, be sure to reward the entire party for completing the floor as well, perhaps granting all of them the hoard benefits. The concept of finding a special item to expand or unlock new regions of a larger dungeon is common in nearly all dungeon delving games (though sometimes this is done through the use of keys rather than specific curios or works in reverse with rewards found in the dungeon expanding things in the outside world, like in Soul Blazer), allowing for numerous sources for inspiration.

Overlords

Overlord dungeons are perhaps the most straightforward of the categories: now that they’ve awakened as a dungeon core with an avatar, an overlord dungeon’s goal is to clean out or conquer each floor of the dungeon and get it back under their own control. Central to that concept is the idea that some kind of invaders, squatters, or other denizens have come to be in control of the various floors within an overlord dungeon, often not out of any particular malice toward the newly- awakened overseer but simply because the dungeon was vacant and available before it gained sapience. This can lead to different kinds of interactions between an overlord dungeon and their floor denizens than in some other dungeons, since the invaders usually weren’t placed there as intentional challenges, but instead came there organically, meaning it might be possible to negotiate with them or reach some kind of compromise aside from one side annihilating the other. And to the overlord, hoping to build an army of minions within the dungeon (even though they can’t lead them forth from the dungeon’s walls), that possibility is exactly what they’d prefer. Overlord dungeons tend to be lawful, as they wish to bring a certain kind of order to the dungeon, but they can rule in different ways and be of any alignment. They’re also somewhat more likely to be evil than good, but even good dungeons might desire to take over a dungeon and make it a friendlier place for the denizens.

The Dungeon Within: When designing the inner dungeon for an overlord, it’s important to first consider what specific invaders or denizens came to live in the dungeon, either as a whole or on a floor-by-floor basis. There might even be a leader or group of leaders associated with a given cluster of floors or the denizens as a whole. This establishes the adversaries and gives the overlord dungeon something to oppose, as well as something to use as a measure of their own growing influence within their dungeon. An overlord dungeon wants minions and influence more than anything, so consider giving rewards for each floor using the leadership subsystem (Pathfinder Gamemastery Guide) or else a similar subsystem such as kingdom building in order to represent the overlord’s domain expanding. The theme of conquering a dungeon to build your own base nearby is a common one and can be found in all sorts of games for inspiration for an overlord dungeon, even if most of those have you building your base just outside of the dungeon. For the specific vibe of controlling monsters as a dungeon overlord, the Dungeon Keeper series and various similar games can provide fodder for your imagination.

That Time I Was Reincarnated As a Dungeon

When a dungeon owes its awakening to the life force and spirit of a creature that died within the dungeon, normally the newly awakened dungeon doesn’t share the creature’s original memories, at least at first. Over time, however, and by defeating floors, they might regain some of the original memories, which could lead to interesting character development or even an identity crisis.

Psyches

Psyches are dungeons whose awakening was tightly linked to mental states, usually those of a creature that died or merged with the dungeon, creating the original core or avatar. Because of this, psyches are especially unlikely to arise entirely naturally without an outside entity to spark their creation and become the genesis of the nascent dungeon. This means that psyches have a history, often one rife with drama, tragedy, or other sufficiently complex emotions or turmoil that led to the eventual creation of the dungeon. Often a psyche dungeon isn't fully aware of all this context, as the avatar might have only limited memories of the precipitating events that led to the dungeon's creation, or perhaps even no memories and nothing more than emotional instincts. In that case, each level of the dungeon they defeat reveals more of themselves and their feelings, opening up hidden depths and making the dungeon character even richer and more vibrant. In this way, psyches have some overlap with quandaries, and more than a few psyches are categorized as quandaries as well. There is some similarity between psyches and fragments, in that both are composed of pieces or facets of something, but the difference is that a psyche dungeon contains unexplored conflicts and dilemmas from the avatar's own mind, whereas a fragment instead contains literal shards of some other entity. Due to the way they expose conflicting psychological facets, psyches tend to be chaotic, though a lawful psyche dungeon might be trying to get their mind in order.

So, I’m A Dungeon, So What?

Most people find it inordinately strange that the person they’re talking to is a dungeon, and so many awakened dungeons hide their true nature, using the avatar as a mask. However, some awakened dungeons just don’t care if other people can’t understand them and proudly flaunt their status as a dungeon, as if daring others to gainsay their claim.

The Dungeon Within: When building a psyche dungeon, consider the fundamental psychological or societal conflicts of the creature whose mind seeded the dungeon's creation. Each of these could become a thematic floor in the dungeon, representing one of the struggles of identity and self for the dungeon PC must overcome. For some ideas, you could look up various real-world psychological or sociological theories (for example, psychologist Erik Erikson theorized struggles across life stages including trust vs. mistrust and autonomy vs. shame), but be sure to customize these as appropriate for the specific dungeon. By clearing a floor, often capped by a floor boss representing the ultimate incarnation of that struggle, the psyche dungeon takes one more step toward understanding themselves. For more inspiration when designing a psyche's dungeon, consider checking out the Persona and Ar Tonelico game series, which explore the idea of dungeons reflecting people's minds and inner selves. Major rewards for a psyche dungeon's floors are usually also thematic to the struggles overcome and are somewhat more likely to be intangible than tangible. Things like automatic bonus progression based on defeating the psyche dungeon's floors, soul seeds (Pathfinder Secrets of Magic) rather than physical relics, bonus skill proficiencies, access to unusual feats and archetypes, and the like are all perfect rewards for a psyche dungeon's floors.

Now I’m an Avatar:
Happily Ever After With My Party in the Dungeon

What if the party is so enamored with the idea of exploring the PC dungeon (or dungeons if more than one dungeon PC is in the same group) that they spend all their time there, and the campaign becomes one set entirely within the dungeon? That's quite all right, as long as all the players are interested! Many classic or old school adventures revolved around a giant dungeon as the backdrop, and there are countless possible adventures that mainline the PC dungeon. In fact, you could even run a published adventure based around a dungeon where the dungeon PC is the avatar of that dungeon, with some small tweaks around the usual extradimensional nature of a dungeon PC's dungeon.

Quandaries

Quandaries are reminders that not every dungeon character can be neatly explained by a simple underlying principle, making them both a "none of the above" category and an important category all their own. They represent the most unusual and inexplicable dungeons, presenting a striking mystery to all who would seek to explore them. While some dungeon categories are incredibly easy to understand, and even some of the more mysterious categories like fragments still have a comprehensible framework of a puzzle with pieces to find, quandaries defy such simple classifications. This might be because they have an even deeper and more mysterious meta-puzzle to solve hidden between their floors, but it might also be because they are simply unfathomable, formed from a variety of factors that defy a single, neat explanation. Quandaries have no particular tendency toward one alignment over another.

The Dungeon Within: Quandaries give you the most freedom as the GM, but since restrictions breed creativity, that can sometimes make them the most challenging to design. Every floor could be completely different, with no seeming rhyme or reason between the denizens, traps, decor, or even the rewards. On the other hand, there might be a hidden underlying connection, sometimes known as the quandary’s "meta-puzzle," that ties together the seemingly disparate elements and reveals a startling underlying truth. Whichever of those approaches you pick, work to show off the variety of the quandary’s dungeon. One great way to do that is to make sure the first two floors have a striking dissimilarity between them, which sets things off on the right foot when it comes to the players’ and their characters’ expectations of the quandary’s composition. If you choose to have a hidden connective meta-puzzle, you can sprinkle those elements slowly throughout the dungeon, and if the players aren’t picking up on it, consider making the clues more and more obvious as the PCs descend to deeper floors. This "pyramidal" clue structure is one of the best ways to reward clever puzzle solvers with early breakthroughs while ensuring that even in the worst case, the group has a very high chance of eventually solving it.

I Shall Survive Using Dungeons

The normal rules for entering a dungeon PC allow it to be an interesting side quest that the group can tackle at their leisure in between missions, but the timing is designed to make sure that it isn't abused as a way to avoid any threat or pressure from the world around them by simply retreating into an extradimensional dungeon... But perhaps the story your group wants to tell would be more interesting if the party could evade danger in that way. In that case, you can change up the restrictions on entering the dungeon to suit the group's preferences, reducing the time it takes, removing the requirement of a day of rest, or both.

As a group, including the GM, you might also decide to remove the requirement that a PC dungeon be an extradimensional dungeon only usually possible to enter via the dungeon's special power to do so. Consider carefully before relaxing that restriction, however. You'll likely want to include some sort of prevention from challenging the dungeon's first floor and then leaving as a means of fast travel or to gather resources from a confederate who waits on the first floor. Perhaps if the dungeon isn't extradimensional, the avatar can't send the party in to challenge it remotely, and they have to reach the dungeon by foot. Alternatively, perhaps the dungeon avatar's ability to challenge a floor removes all other intruders from that floor, and attempts to backtrack out of the floor or dungeon end the attempt on the floor and instead return the party to their original location. Or perhaps you just don't care if the dungeon's features can be used as a quick travel / market from all locations. This is a substantial power boost that pretty much negates wilderness travel, survival, and the like, but it's one that benefits the whole team, not just the dungeon PC, so it might be more fun for your group to play it that way.

Soul Prisons

Soul prisons are dungeons populated by the regrets, unfinished businesses, and untold stories of those who have died around the dungeon’s avatar. What caused the dungeon to become a magnet for these souls varies from dungeon to dungeon. It may come from a failed lich ritual to create a soul cage or an attraction through ley lines that creates an environment that draws in and embodies the defeated. Whatever the case, stepping into a soul prison’s dungeon means confronting one’s past and exploring other sides of those one has met and slain before. This means that the floors of a soul prison are often a chaotic hodgepodge of different themes, which might resemble a quandary at first glance, each demesne reflecting the will of a single master or group of masters. The only circumstance wherein the floors happen to all be related is when the dungeon PC and their party are defeating a series of related opponents across a long- term campaign. For instance, if every major encounter in a campaign is against the government of a tyrannical empire, the soul prison’s floors will naturally reflect the aesthetics of that empire’s defeated lieutenants, even across floors. Soul prisons usually don’t have any choice in the process of sealing fragments of the fallen’s souls, though those who do so on purpose are undoubtedly evil. Otherwise, soul prisons often wind up moving toward a neutral alignment even if they start with an extreme one, as the souls within, usually from an opposing alignment, have some influence and revert them to the mean.

The Dungeon Within: A soul prison dungeon generally forms out of the memories or wishes of the deceased to include trappings of their former life, with floors themed around particularly strong personalities or related groups of creatures. Consider the different NPCs or groups that the party has defeated and killed, and then use them to design floors. New floors will unlock naturally over the course of the campaign as the party continues their adventures. This is an especially good way to get across complex antagonist backstories, goals, or other rich details that were lost in the initial confrontation when the PCs killed the villain without spending much time talking to them, and it doesn’t require the stereotypical ham-handed solution to that conundrum of turning every villain into an avid diarist. The perfect reward for a soul prison is to give a free boost to an item or reward the PCs previously received when they defeated the antagonist the first time, as releasing their spirit or understanding their circumstances unlocks the treasure’s true potential. This can be as complex as you desire, but it can also be as simple as upgrading the storm flash found on the original villain to a greater storm flash after clearing their floor of the dungeon within. Few games are entirely themed around a dungeon built on those slain by the characters, but you can get a bit of inspiration from certain dungeons in various games, such as the Fortress of Regrets in Planescape: Torment or the Xin Arena in Children of Zodiarcs.

Alignment and Religion

Dungeons can be of any alignment, and their alignment is usually completely unrelated to the alignment of any monsters within the dungeon, though depending on the dungeon’s category, the creatures might share alignment components with aspects of the dungeon’s personality, or the special items within the dungeon. If a dungeon gained their life force and spirit from another creature, they often have an alignment similar to that creature. Similarly, dungeons worship all sorts of deities for all sorts of reasons. Depending on the type of dungeon, they might have a slight tendency to worship a nature deity for natural dungeons or a deity associated with doorways, thresholds, transitions, or crafting for artificial dungeons.

Is It Wrong To Pick Up Gold In A Dungeon?

Awakened dungeons have a philosophical question to answer about other dungeons: is it OK to loot them? Or would that be akin to stealing or disfiguring the other dungeon? In almost every case, awakened dungeons eagerly support the idea of exploring other dungeons, defeating their dangers, and plundering their treasures. But it's not hypocritical, as they enjoy doing so within their own dungeon as well!

Names

Dungeons have three major tendencies when it comes to names. They typically either keep the name that others commonly called the dungeon before they awakened, give themselves a mighty and impressive-sounding dungeon name, or adopt a name more suited to their avatar’s form. Dungeons sometimes have conflicting feelings about the names others had given them, especially if the name was dull (like the "Northern Cave") or heavily focused on something the dungeon doesn’t care about, like being named based on the types of external invaders who had seized control of the dungeon’s upper floors rather than the amazing architecture and traps that were actually part of the dungeon.

Sample Names

Acheron, Elysia, Hal, Gehenna, Portia, Steve, The Azure Depths, The Crystalline Caverns, The Great Sage Tree, The Twisting Labyrinth.

The Lazy Dungeon Gamemaster

If a player wants to play a dungeon PC, it isn't strictly necessary for the Gamemaster to invent an entire side dungeon for the party to explore, especially if that would be disruptive to the campaign or if the GM doesn't have the time or energy to do so on behalf of a single player. It's still possible to play a dungeon PC and ignore that aspect, for instance, though it does cut off some of the possibilities. Another option is for the player of the dungeon to help brainstorm elements of the dungeon within, or even to take turns GMing. For instance, perhaps whenever the party explores the PC dungeon, the dungeon avatar isn't present, and the usual GM plays an NPC or other character while the player of the dungeon PC takes the wheel. It could even be a good way to give the main GM a break if they need a week off to prepare, so they can ask the dungeon PC's player to run a bit more of the dungeon that week.

Dungeon Heritages

Dungeons manifest in all sorts of ways, rather than only a stereotypical underground building, and each form of dungeon has the possibility to awaken. Choose one of the following dungeon heritages at 1st level.

Archipelago

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.y8so5omtTAodYw08]{Archipelago}

While at first it might not seem like a chain of islands could be a dungeon, since islands are separated and each can be made to contain discrete challenges and rewards, it quickly becomes clear that this is indeed the case. You are one such dungeon island chain, and as such, your avatar is more effective in the water. You gain a swim Speed of 10 feet and the amphibious trait. Like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air

Caverns

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.dvUzrr8bNaHMnyZN]{Caverns}

You are a series of ever deeper caverns, potentially full of strange crystals or other unusual and mysterious objects, and your avatar is better able to handle the darkness within your cavern depths. You gain darkvision.

Labyrinth

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.Sjh2qR01UYrjC97o]{Labyrinth}

You are a labyrinth, an artificially constructed maze of traps, creatures, and treasure, making you one of the most traditional and stereotypical forms of dungeon. Due to your twisting paths, it’s hard for you to get lost anywhere else, as you have an innate sense of direction. You gain the trained proficiency rank in Survival, or if you would already be trained in Survival, you are trained in another skill of your choice. You gain a +2 circumstance bonus on all Survival checks to @Compendium[pf2e.actionspf2e.fJImDBQfqfjKJOhk]{Sense Direction}, and if you roll a success on a Survival check to Sense Direction, you get a critical success instead.

Leviathan

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.gD6nHxDfRSgQCipI]{Leviathan}

Like the mythical leviathan for which this heritage is named, you are the separate consciousness of an enormous extradimensional creature that contains a dungeon within its own body. While your dungeon core and avatar’s lives are linked, the creature is not in any danger if either of them dies, nor is it necessarily aligned with your interests; if the creature dies, you live on as a dungeon within a rotting corpse instead of a living creature. In fact, while some are ancient geniuses, many of these giant creatures are sentient but non-sapient. Your avatar likely has a small telltale sign connecting you to the great creature that forms your dungeon, such as an unusual eye color or a few small scales. Additionally, your avatar’s features closely resemble the creature’s, and you gain one of the following unarmed attacks, which you choose when you select this heritage to match the appearance of your dungeon’s body; once you choose it, you can’t change to a different unarmed attack. The attack is in the brawling group and has the listed damage die and traits.

Mausoleum

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.K6NC1rEamPRnXYYK]{Mausoleum}

You are a mausoleum, crypt, catacomb, or other religious site that typically houses the dead. Choose a cantrip from the divine spell list. You can cast this cantrip as an innate divine spell.

Tower

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.mcQ5a2VHmpAtbHXe]{Tower}

You are a giant tower that pierces the sky of your extradimensional home, with ever-ascending floors full of unusual magic. Choose a cantrip from the arcane spell list. You can cast this cantrip as an innate arcane spell.

Tree

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.KYCXStQ2cL3QRhO9]{Tree}

You are an enormous tree, which creatures can enter to explore a dungeon with numerous floors within the towering roots, trunk, and branches. As a giant tree, you have a natural advantage against other plants. You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to both Perception checks and saving throws against plants. In addition, whenever you meet a plant creature in a social situation, you can immediately attempt a Diplomacy check to @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.OX4fy22hQgUHDr0q]{Make an Impression} on that creature rather than needing to converse for 1 minute. You take a –5 penalty to the check. If you fail, you can engage in 1 minute of conversation and attempt a new check at the end of that time rather than accepting the failure or critical failure result. If you have the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.feats-srd.Item.cc8O47KFsODReoBe]{Glad-Hand} skill feat, you don't take the penalty on your immediate Diplomacy check if the target is a plant.

Ancestry Feats

The following feats are available to dungeon characters. Many of these feats refer to the listed heritages, with a default for an artificial or natural dungeon; if a dungeon has a versatile heritage, they should either default to the standard effect or trait based on whether the dungeon is natural or artificial, or choose an appropriate option based on the nature of the versatile heritage (for instance, an aasimar dungeon might use divine magic). If you're using one of the listed heritages, archipelagos, caverns, leviathans, and trees use primal magic; mausoleums use divine magic; and labyrinths and towers use arcane magic. This information is also repeated in each feat for ease of reference.
