Gremlin Ancestry.name: 小魔怪族裔 Gremlin Ancestry
Gremlin Ancestry.text:
Gremlins are tiny, fey incarnations of misfortune, curses, mechanical failures, and other acts of mischief and destruction. While many gremlins act as jinxes and saboteurs, others seek a different lot in life in keeping with the stories and expectations surrounding their ancestry. Either way, a gremlin's path can lead to the wandering lifestyle of an adventurer just as easily as it can to growing roots in a settlement or lair.

Whether or not they use their skills to cause malicious malfunctions and pull sadistic pranks, the fact remains that gremlins are incredibly skilled when it comes to understanding objects, magic, or both. Just because that expertise tends to be focused on how things break and fall apart doesn't mean it isn't useful in other ways. For instance, knowing the places where a structure is most likely to break can be crucial in inspecting and maintaining it, even if the more obvious use is sabotage.

Given that they are drawn to destructive mischief by the collective magic that defines their fey essence, even heroic gremlin adventurers often have a wicked sense of humor, a tendency to perform pranks, or a guilty pleasure in engaging in some harmless destruction. Often, they might have all three. Even so, that doesn't stop them from feeling compassion for others or performing selfless acts. Other gremlin adventurers embrace their gremlin powers more fully but find a found family, adventuring party, or other place where they can belong. Such connections can help to temper their most hurtful hijinks, eventually earning them the role of the troublemaker within their group. Even such a role is a form of acceptance, one that subtly subverts the narrative of the malicious outcast and rewrites their possibilities, and thus it's hard to overstate the value that role provides to those gremlins.

If you want to play a tiny trickster known for jinxes and breaking things, you should play a gremlin.

You Might...

  • Enjoy playing pranks and tricks on others.
  • Destroy things as a form of stress relief.
  • Love to hoard items and tinker with things, whether to build nasty traps, to sabotage others, or simply for fun.

Others Probably...

  • Respect your knowledge of curses and crafts.
  • Blame you when things nearby break or go wrong.
  • Believe that you are nothing but bad luck.

Physical Description

Gremlins share very few characteristics across their heritages, each with striking and pronounced features. One common point is that all gremlins are relatively small, at most the size of a halfling or gnome, with many heritages significantly smaller than that. Additionally, many, though not all, heritages have an especially colorful skin tone. Beyond that, gremlin appearances vary as wildly as the many tales and whispered rumors that are told about them among humanoid societies.

Fuaths, aquatic gremlins who love to sabotage ships, constantly drip with water from their yellow skin and seaweed-green fur. Their faces are vaguely reminiscent of seahorses, and their arms end in impressive lobster claws despite their tiny bodies.

Grimples look like tiny mangy opossum people with boar tusks and loose flaps of skin between their limbs that they can use to slow their falls, and potentially to glide or fly.

Hanivers are tiny humanoids with flat bodies covered in yellowy leather skin. They have beady black eyes and rubbery fins that billow out like a ray's and that can eventually act as a combination of fins and wings with enough training and exercise. Jinkins have an appearance that most villagers associate with the word "gremlin." They are tiny with vibrantly colored skin, typically purple but sometimes green or a different color, with wide ears, glowing eyes, relatively small noses, and sharp needle-like teeth.

Mitflits, gremlins who have lost some of their power through adaptation to the Material Plane, are larger than many other gremlins, though still roughly the size of a goblin or halfling. Their appearance shares some similarities with jinkins, but their skin tends to be blue, their heads are larger in comparison to their bodies, their noses are more bulbous, and their ears are a bit flatter rather than pointed upward.

Nuglubs are among the largest of all gremlin heritages, though most nuglubs are still not even three feet tall. However, this measure of their height is partly due to their crouching stature. Their skin is pink with long pointed ears, and they have three glowing blue eyes, the third centered on their forehead. Perhaps their most distinctive feature is their voluminous, oily black hair, which billows around their back like a cloak.

Pugwampi, noted for their bad luck, are tiny gremlins with doglike heads. Their pink skin is covered with patches of brown fur.

Vexgit, or wrecker gremlins, have crustacean-like heads and exoskeletons with tiny insectile bodies. Their face has six red eyes set in a dark exoskeletal shell streaked with orange stripes. A pair of mandibles flank their vicious jaws. From their backs extends a tail tipped with a sharp point. Their exoskeletons are covered with spiky protrusions, especially at the joints, where the protrusions serve a protective purpose.

Gremlin Adventurers

Gremlin adventurers prefer classes that allow them to create mayhem and traps or to tinker with mechanical structures or magic. This includes alchemist, inventor, ranger, rogue, and thaumaturge, though some heritages prefer straightforward destruction with classes like barbarian or sorcerer. Gremlins often have backgrounds tied to mischief or destruction, such as artisan, clockwork researcher, hounded thief, junk collector, junker, magical misfit, mechanic, saboteur, scavenger, tinker, or toymaker.

Society

Surprising other humanoids, many gremlin societies are highly cooperative and relatively egalitarian, although they cooperate toward the goal of sabotage, mischief, and destruction. Gremlins have difficulty understanding why other ancestries place so much emphasis on family, as they share the same ties with their entire community, with little bias toward family members or relatives beyond other community members. Unlike many other ancestries, where older, more established people take leadership positions, the youngest, most energetic, and obnoxious gremlins tend to become the leaders of gremlin communities, at least if left to their own devices. They focus their efforts on bothering and bullying other gremlins until they can take control. For this reason, gremlin communities typically trick or persuade those energetic youngsters to explore the outside world, attempting to aim all that enthusiasm elsewhere. This means that many gremlin adventurers come from the ranks of these potential gremlin leaders, and their zeal makes them especially indomitable in pursuing their various goals.

Beyond these shared societal characteristics, each gremlin's heritage has its own idiosyncrasies and culture. Lastly, gremlins belong to societal groups called crashes, especially those who explore the world outside their home community. Each crash has strong opinions on their role in the world around them, which they share at least in part with gremlins from the same crash, even across gremlin heritages.

The following are descriptions of the eight heritages presented in this volume, followed by the five crashes most common among gremlins.

Heritages

The following eight gremlin heritages aren't the only members of the gremlin family, but they're more likely than most other gremlin heritages to become adventurers.

Fuaths

Fuath gremlins are tied to the strong superstitions of sailors, who put their lives at risk every time they allow a small, fallible ship to be the only protection between themselves and a watery grave beneath the merciless ocean waves. The innumerable whispered tales of aquatic doom passed from shiphand to shiphand imbued fuaths with destructive energy, remaking them into shipwreckers incarnate. Typical fuath mischief includes the destruction of various parts of the ship, navigation equipment, rations, fresh water, and more, performed right under the nose of the crew while they are asleep or otherwise inattentive.

Adventuring fuaths don't limit themselves to ships and ships alone, applying their expertise across a broader array of locations and situations. In order to subsist on land during the day, they expose themselves slowly to surface sunlight, gradually reducing their vulnerability to direct exposure. Even so, they prefer an aquatic adventure or environment if at all possible. Fuath tend to socialize more often with other aquatic creatures (particularly fey and sea hags) than they do with terrestrial gremlin heritages. This is partially due to a language barrier, as fuaths speak Aquan by default rather than Undercommon, but it's also a testament to how deeply they respect other aquatic fey. While destroyers hold only their crueler and more malicious aquatic kin in such high esteem, fuaths of other crashes sometimes respect more benevolent water fey, such as naiads. In fact, fuaths are somewhat more likely to become reckoners who seek vengeance against water pollution and guard pristine natural areas, making them unlikely allies for nymphs with a similar worldview.

While all gremlins are more likely to be evil and least likely to be good, fuaths also tend toward chaos over law. The exceptions are fuath reckoners, who work more methodically and with greater patience and are willing to remain in a location to protect it if necessary or to pursue their own sense of justice to its rightful ends.

Grimples

Grimples often feel that the strictures and trappings of so-called "civilized" society are oppressive and banal. Why should the consensus of others get to decide what is "right" or "proper?" Grimples march to the beat of their own drummer, and that drummer has a quirky and gross sense of humor. They tell jokes and pull pranks about disgusting smells, scatological humor and bodily fluids, and anything else that will shock and revolt their audience and their victims alike. Their targets are typically those who take themselves too seriously, those who push others around and bend them to the will of society, and those who bring social order and conformity where before there were wild places and isolated groups. With their pervasive lice and the ability to regurgitate on demand, it's rarely difficult for grimples to engineer the sort of pranks they love the most, even without any materials beyond those provided by their own body (as well as their parasitic insectile hangers-on).

Adventuring grimples rarely take up quests that force them to labor under society's yoke, but they absolutely love playing the underdog against some sort of oppressive force. They resist this tyranny with disgusting and lurid tactics and a seemingly unbeatable juvenile sense of humor so incessant and incorrigible that it wraps back around to being somewhat admirable, akin to an unyielding determination. A grimple's comrades should make sure they have strong stomachs; even the bestbehaved of grimple adventurers, who only targets their disgusting pranks at their foes, is still reasonably likely to cause occasional intestinal distress to any nearby allies who witness their antics.

Grimples are more likely to be evil than good, like other gremlin heritages, and their disdain for society, rules, and uniformity mean they are much more likely to be chaotic than lawful. In fact, lawful grimples are rarer than good grimples, and most grimples can't stand the few who exist; a jinkins with a grudge against a gang of grimples once achieved the impossible and grossed out the grimples so much they couldn't handle it, all by creating an illusion presenting a neatly dressed, perfectly coiffed and manicured grimple with excellent hygiene and perfect diction. Grimples are often members of the prankster crash, or secondarily the destroyer or reckoner crashes.

Hanivers

The most curious and least malicious gremlin heritage, hanivers feel an intense desire to examine interesting objects around them, ultimately stealing or otherwise acquiring their favorite objects for their own personal trove of treasure. These actions might be self-centered, but they're rarely focused on causing harm or mischief for others. To a haniver, it's all about improving their own collection, and there's no joy in the fact that they're also potentially depriving the previous owner. For this reason, most consider hanivers to be among the most benign gremlins, and when they choose to pursue their interests in less burglarious manners, hanivers are among the only gremlin heritages likely to be openly welcomed by their neighbors.

Hanivers who choose to become adventurers fit right in. One bard quipped "Mischievous gremlins compelled by their nature to obtain whatever shiny loot dangles in front of them by any means necessary? So basically your average adventurer." Motivated by their curiosity and desire to collect more treasures, many hanivers become adventurers. Haniver adventurers are willing to join causes both righteous and wicked as long as there's an enticing enough reward at the other end for them, or something to discover that's interesting enough to pique their curiosity.

Hanivers are far more likely to be chaotic than lawful, but they tend more toward neutrality between good and evil than other gremlins do. They're the most likely among gremlin heritages to be members of the unfettered crash, and when they're not, they're more likely to be pranksters or tinkers than they are to be destroyers or reckoners. They're more curious than they are destructive.

Jinkins

Jinkins claim to be the first gremlins, from which all other gremlin heritages split off as stories varied from place to place. Whether this self-aggrandizing origin story is true or not, it behooves others not to argue it with a jinkin, as they can be viciously prideful on the matter and often take convoluted revenge when they perceive a slight. One piece of evidence that lends credence to the tale is the way that jinkins adhere so strongly to the earliest gremlin folklore, with a strong focus on tinkering, hexing, and sabotaging objects, particularly complex magical or mechanical workings. Jinkins are also extremely fond of traps, particularly incapacitating traps that allow them to taunt and tease those they have captured.

Adventuring jinkins turn their trickery and schemes to their party's advantage, laying traps, snares, and tricks in the path of their foes. A jinkin's comrades often greatly appreciate the advantages they can gain from the gremlin's devious plots. Unfortunately, however, few jinkins fully focus their ingenuity on their opponents, and so their fellow party members might eventually become frustrated with the pranks their erstwhile jinkin ally plays upon them.

Like other gremlins, jinkins are more likely to be evil than good, and their capricious love of pranks and tricks makes them more likely to be chaotic than lawful. Jinkins are often members of the prankster and tinker crashes.

Mitflits

Long ago, mitflits left the realm of the fey and entered the mortal world. Over time, they lost much of their connection to nefarious stories of mayhem and destruction, which weakened their magic powers and left many of them with powerful social anxiety, full of an overwhelming doubt and insecurity they couldn't quite explain. Mitflits often have low self-esteem and a lack of confidence, which can lead others to bully them, building into a vicious cycle. Mitflits yearn for friendship, validation, and acceptance, which they rarely find from other mitflits and especially not from other gremlins. Instead, they befriend insects and other arthropods, who accept them unconditionally.

Mitflits' desire for acceptance can lead to especially happy or sorrowful tales, depending on how their story progresses. Those who find compassionate comrades, such as adventurers who join an understanding party, can form lifelong bonds of friendship, slowly work through their anxiety, and build up their self-esteem. On the other hand, mitflits are especially susceptible to abusive relationships. To maintain the relationship, they would do just about anything for the approval of their abuser, even if they were treated poorly. Mitflits saved from such a relationship can slowly find a way to overcome their trauma, particularly with patient help.

Like other gremlins, mitflits tend toward evil, though much less so than any other gremlin heritage, and they're almost as likely to be neutral between good and evil. Their desire for a social structure to fill in the hole where their missing gremlin stories would go often leads them to a lawful alignment. For better or worse, their alignment can shift quickly toward someone who shows them (real or feigned) compassion and acceptance. Mitflits are the only gremlin heritage that has a high tendency to be unfettered due to their intrinsically weak connection to the gremlin magic of the fey realm.

Gremlin Settlements

Gremlins establish fractious but ultimately close-knit societies, a fact that sometimes surprises other ancestries. Gremlin communities connect via group dynamics, sharing mischief and mayhem to build their bonds, as most gremlins have little or no concept of familial relationships. Gremlin societies are swayed by the community's public opinion rather than any set leadership structure, which allows the loudest and boldest gremlins to build up great influence and take charge of community efforts. Most gremlin settlements are built underground, either in caverns in the wilderness or in sewers, cellars, and undercities beneath other larger settlements.

Nuglubs

Nuglubs love battle as much as other gremlins love pranks and sabotage, giving them a reputation as battle junkies and berserkers among other gremlins. That isn't to say nuglubs dislike traps and trickery, just not to the extent of other gremlins, and they never pick a fair fight if they can avoid it. Stories of ambushes, shocking reversals, and curb-stomp victories over weak foes inspire nugglubs. To a nuglub, the bigger they are, the harder they fall, and once they fall, it's time to hit them while they're down. Nuglubs don't feel the need to take credit for their victories; the visceral fight and the success themselves are their own reward, and most nuglubs prefer it when others "take the blame" for any mayhem.

Unlike other gremlins, nuglubs don't often form large communities and are instead especially used to small groups of approximately the size of an adventuring party. The exception to that tendency are solitary nuglub bosses who autocratically command mixed groups of gremlins and other ancestries. In small groups, however, nuglubs can usually get along without too much quarreling. Even though they have a reputation for being obstreperous, even among other gremlins, nuglubs' zeal for battle, their focus on finishing off weakened opponents, and their tendency to shift the credit and blame for their activities to their allies can make them surprisingly easy to get along with for adventuring groups that prefer violent solutions. Nuglubs are more likely to be evil than good, like the other gremlin heritages, and their fervor for battle leads them to be chaotic more often than lawful. In fact, they're among the least appreciative of the order and structure of all gremlin heritages. Surprisingly, among gremlins, nuglubs are especially unlikely to be members of the prankster or tinker crashes, as they much prefer to be destroyers or reckoners.

Pugwampis

Dog-headed pugwampis are the gremlin heritage most associated with jinxes, bad luck, and misfortune. Infused with the stories that exist across cultures, whispering of charms and activities that curse others with bad luck, pugwampis are surrounded by a palpable sensation of misfortune, almost like an ominous cloud over their heads. The two genres of traditional pugwampi pranks are hands-off and hands-on. In the hands-off approach, a pugwampi hexes their foe or victim with misfortune and then sits back to watch their embarrassing pratfalls and failures when dealing with ordinary threats and challenges. Meanwhile, when a pugwampi chooses to get hands-on, they typically combine puerile humor with traps and misfortune, such as digging a pit and filling it with excrement, spiders, or both. Even among gremlins, creatures of other ancestries (and sometimes other gremlins) particularly dislike pugwampis. For one, people can't help but resent the symbol of misfortune. But beyond that, a typical pugwampi's personality comes across as grating to others. Sadly, this antipathy is even stronger among gnolls, who actively avoid pugwampis at the first opportunity, despite (or perhaps because of) the fact that pugwampis adore gnolls and sycophantically praise them at every opportunity. Pugwampis don't rely on their sense of hearing as much as other ancestries, and it is weaker and less refined as a result.

Pugwampis traveling the world enjoy joining adventuring parties and are glad to do so at the first opportunity, assuming the party is willing to accept a pugwampi. After all, their fellow party members can help protect the pugwampi from bullying or other consequences. The ancestries of the other adventurers usually don't matter much to pugwampis, but of course, if a party with one or more gnolls is actually willing to accept a pugwampi member, nearly any pugwampi adventurer would be over the moon.

Pugwampis tend to be evil more often than good, like other gremlin heritages, and they don't have any particular tendency toward chaos or law. They join the prankster crash more often than any other, though they sometimes become unfettered if pulled out of their normal routine by another adventurer they respect (especially a gnoll).

Vexgits

Insectoid and crustacean-headed vexgits love wrecking and sabotaging the works and creations of other creatures, particularly when they can turn their target's own construction into a clever trap. More so than other gremlins, they love their sabotage to have big, violent, destructive effects over subtlety or a slow approach. These tendencies often lead them to carry heavy tools or big weapons so they can smash apart anything that catches their eye if tampering with their target is impossible or too difficult.

Adventuring vexgits are a massive asset to any party that's willing to think outside the box in terms of their solutions, as they often attempt to lay traps and snares, co-opt their enemy's workings, and turn every advantage against their foes and in favor of their own group. However, parties that rush forward without any preparation can frustrate a vexgit, leading to an increased chance that the vexgit might act up, pull some pranks, or sabotage their own team in order to distress. On their end, vexgit adventurers sometimes struggle to limit their creative ideas for vandalism and disruption to their enemies.

Like the other gremlin heritages, vexgits gravitate toward evil more than good, but their methodical and efficient approach to sabotage means they are more likely to be lawful than chaotic. They tend to prefer the destroyer, prankster, or tinker crash, each of which offers them opportunities to pursue their predilections.

Crashes

The following crashes are the most popular among gremlins, though they're always inventing new ones over time to express their mischievous and ingenious proclivities.

Destroyers

Destroyers are gremlins who love watching things fall apart, explode, or otherwise come undone. Typically, gremlins in the destroyer crash focus on the destruction of objects, but some choose to view the destroyer philosophy in a different way, pursuing the dissolution of their enemies' alliances, schemes, or even beliefs. Due to the crash's name and reputation for wrecking things, outsiders sometimes make the mistake of assuming destroyers aren't particularly smart, wrongly imagining some sort of rampaging brute. In truth, destroyers are extremely clever and creative, employing numerous unexpected methodologies to achieve their desired mayhem and make things break in a way that interests and challenges them. They often see their destruction as a form of art, in which case they are disgusted if others compare their masterpiece to the simple and straightforward rampage of those who destroy things thoughtlessly.

While it's easy to stereotype destroyers as chaotic (due to their desire to see things collapse into a state of entropy) and evil (since the destruction they enjoy is likely to cause the suffering of others), destroyers of other alignments, though rarer, do exist. Non-evil destroyers focus on jobs and roles that allow them to destroy things without causing undue harm. For instance, a destroyer would be the perfect architect for a plan to demolish old, unsafe buildings in order to construct new ones in the same location. Similarly, non-evil destroyers might set smaller controlled fires to prevent a larger wildfire and engage in other acts of helpful destruction. One such possibility is to serve as an adventurer, aiming their destruction squarely at malign organizations and putting their expertise to good use. These adventuring destroyers are gremlins that any party would definitely prefer to have on their side rather than opposing them, as they are likely to spare their comrades from the worst of their destructive tendencies. All of these non-evil applications of destruction tend to be more lawful and methodical as well, though not exclusively. Destroying something on a chaotic whim in a way that avoids harming others requires keen instincts, though if anyone could succeed, it would be a gremlin.

Pranksters

Pranksters are gremlins who absolutely adore pranks, tricks, and humor, often focused on teasing, humiliating, or harming others. Their preferred medium for their schemes varies wildly from prankster to prankster, though the gremlin's tendency to clandestinely sabotage physical objects runs as a common throughline across many a caper. Even so, some pranksters prefer wordplay and rumor-mongering, while others even engage in direct physical comedy, such as throwing a pie filled with itching powder into someone's face. The sky's the limit, and for just about any possible type of practical joke, there's a prankster somewhere who's tried it at least once. These tendencies carry over even to adventuring pranksters, which can be funny when the joke's on the group's enemies but is known to aggravate the prankster's fellow party members when they become the butt of their ally's humor.

Most pranksters prefer mean-spirited mischief, targeting their enemies, those who they feel deserve to be knocked down a peg, or sometimes just innocent victims who were just too juicy a target to resist. These pranksters tend to be evil or, at best, neutral. A few pranksters also employ a mixture of good-natured japes designed to leave everyone laughing, even the target. These are more likely to be neutral or good, though even some evil members of the crash perform less malicious pranks on others they deeply respect. Pranksters are more likely to be chaotic than lawful, though lawful pranksters engage in careful, methodical pranks that require significant planning and precise execution in order to reach an epically humorous conclusion.

Gremlin Superstitions

Mortal superstitions involving them, such as tales of their flaws or effects that agitate them, have a particularly negative impact on gremlins. Chief among these are the tales of gremlin bells to ward gremlins away, which have led humanoids to put up bells, often iron bells, hoping to repel gremlins. While gremlin bells have spread across the land, there are other stories that vary depending on the location. For instance, in some lands, there are rumors that gremlins multiply more rapidly in wet areas or when doused with water. In others, there are tales of gremlins increasing the ferocity of their destructive hijinks if they manage to find a meal after midnight.

Reckoners

Reckoners are gremlins who target their activities against those who transgress, like the shadowy monsters in fairy tales designed to convince children to behave. Their targets vary from the stereotypical misbehaving children to polluters and others who abuse the natural world, people with overinflated egos in need of being knocked down a peg, and even sometimes abusers who victimize the defenseless. Oddly enough, this can cause two groups of reckoners to come into conflict, as those who target misbehaving children might be targeted by other reckoners for going after defenseless youngsters. After all, although gremlins who are members of the same settlement or community typically work together, there's nothing about membership in a gremlin crash that necessarily guarantees an alliance between them. Reckoners are one of the most likely crashes to become adventurers, assuming there's a target in need of a reckoning that's too dangerous to handle on their own. This path is especially likely if they're a loner or otherwise can't convince members of their gremlin community to join them in the reckoning; in that case, adventurers might be their only hope of success.

Reckoners run the gamut on alignment. Some, like a wide swathe of fuath gremlins, indulge their own evil urges against those who have their own sins, which nonetheless makes them no less evil. Others take up the call of the reckoner for more altruistic reasons and have a neutral or even good alignment. While fuath reckoners are still more often chaotic, other reckoners are somewhat more likely to be lawful. This is due to the way they restrict themselves to particular targets, meting out their own take on poetic justice. After all, the principle of "an eye for an eye" is the essence of lawful evil.

Tinkers

Tinkers are gremlins who care more about taking things apart, understanding how they work, and inventing something new than they do about pulling a good prank or destroying things. That's not to say that they don't put their own spin on things, however. Tinkers are more likely to design a new trap, snare, or other instrument of mayhem than they are to create a simple tool that makes daily life more comfortable or convenient for others. Of course, as in all things, there are some tinkers who are exceptions to that trend. When engaging in their preferred areas of research, tinkers are some of the most passionate, energetic, and brilliant minds in all the land. Unfortunately, this sometimes comes with the bad habit of belittling or overlooking anything that doesn't match their focus, which often includes other people and their interests. Ultimately, however, it's rare that a single person, even a genius, can provide everything necessary to perform expensive research or tinkering nonstop. For that reason, many tinkers turn to adventuring as a means to fund their true passion. They prefer to find groups that are interested in letting them test out their tinkering and who work professionally and quickly without too much attachment or melodrama. Once the job is over, most tinkers want to go back to their workshop, not out for drinks at the tavern.

Tinkers care about their work far more than moral concerns, and so they tend to be neutral more often than good or evil, though evil tinkers who perform amoral and harmful experiments come in a close second. Tinkers run the gamut from lawful tinkers, who meticulously document their findings, to chaotic tinkers, who improvise and wildly innovate whatever strikes their fancy.

Unfettered

Unfettered are gremlins who ignore the trappings of any of the usual gremlin stories that otherwise empower and transform their ancestry. Unfettered do what they want, when they want, and their actions rarely relate to traps, tinkering, or sabotage. Unlike the other gremlin crashes, they tend to have classes and backgrounds that defy the expectations of gremlins or even run directly opposed to them. While it might seem like their choices would leave unfettered weaker than other gremlins, unfettered instead draws their strength from the very act of flouting the gremlin stories. After all, stories thrive on counterexamples and conflict, so this defiance is an equally valid source of power. Unfettered are the most common of all gremlin crashes to become adventurers. They sometimes seek a name for themselves to prove they can be whoever they choose, whether they do so simply for their own reasons or specifically to contradict the expectations placed upon them by others in their gremlin community.

Unfettered can be any alignment, though they are often good or neutral rather than evil as part of breaking free from narrative expectations, and they're somewhat more likely to be chaotic due to their free-spirited tendencies. Good unfettered sometimes go so far as to specifically protect others against the same types of actions typical to other gremlin crashes, sometimes even becoming paladins, redeemers, or liberators. Meanwhile, evil unfettered engage in their own favored vices without getting involved with stereotypical gremlin hijinks.

Alignment and Religion

While gremlin alignment varies by heritage and crash, gremlins are more likely to be evil than other alignments overall. That said, the tendency is far less pronounced among gremlin adventurers than it is among gremlin communities at large. Gremlin adventurers instead tend a bit more toward neutrality between good and evil, while retaining any lawful or chaotic leanings from their heritage and crash.

Gremlins often worship powerful fey and trickster deities, as well as deities of tinkers or traps. In Alacar, among the deities of the Balance, Sewa the Rogue has many gremlin adherents. Other deities sometimes speak to certain aspects that gremlins appreciate, such as the call to innovation and change from Lilin the Mother, or Baoyan's dual nature as a deity of fiery destruction and creation.

Gremlin Pranks

Gremlins attempt pranks, tricks, and all other hijinks of all sorts. The following are a few possibilities, which you can use for inspiration for your gremlin's antics.

  • Sabotaging an item or machine to cause it to do something humorous, like belch out smoke, rather than its intended function.
  • Inserting bugs, dead leaves, night soil, or other substances into a place where someone is likely to reach their hand.
  • Setting a tripwire or small pit to cause an amusing pratfall.
  • Using consumables or swiping items and replacing them with something worthless or disgusting.
  • Applying itching powder, foul smelling fruit extract, or other substances to irritate a target's senses.

Names

Gremlins tend to name children in their community based on their own sense of humor, often in a pejorative way. Gremlins then earn a name for themselves once they've accomplished an epic act in the eyes of their community, which they can choose on their own or in concert with their fellow community members. Since they generally deeply dislike their original name given to them in teasing, young gremlins are eager to prove themselves and change their name. Of course, since the new name is chosen by a young gremlin in a coming of age ritual, they tend to be all over the place, ranging from sharing a name with a preferred trap, to pompous, to downright weird.

Sample Names

Calamity, Crankclack, Doooooom, Great Explosion God, Gremergrin, Kablammo, Mogwai, Morbleplix, Spanner, Spear Launcher, Vorpal Executioner, Vizgyr, Zuzuzu.

Gremlin Heritages

Each gremlin heritage is empowered by different variations on the classic gremlin stories, which grants them additional abilities, sharply disparate physical features, and even different sizes. Choose one of the following gremlin heritages at 1st level. If you choose another heritage (such as a versatile heritage), your gremlin is typically Small, though at the GM's discretion, if you're partially descended from a Tiny heritage, you can choose to be Tiny instead.

Fuath

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.HEzZ5vP6Aea5J8ch]{Fuath}

You're a fuath, an amphibious gremlin with big lobster claws. Your size is Tiny. Instead of Undercommon, you automatically speak Aquan. You gain a swim Speed of 15 feet and the amphibious trait. Like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air. You gain a claw unarmed attack, which deals @Damage[1d4[slashing]] damage, has the agile and finesse traits, and is in the brawling weapon group. Despite how they look, your claws are surprisingly prehensile, and you have no trouble using them to perform manipulate actions or actions that require fine dexterity. Lastly, while you've somewhat overcome it, you share your heritage's aversion to lengthy exposure to direct sunlight. After a full hour of direct sunlight, you become @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.4D2KBtexWXa6oUMR]{Drained 1}. Being submerged in more than a foot of water prevents the sunlight from harming you, and getting out of the sunlight for at least a few minutes before gaining the drained condition allows you to recover and reset the count.

Grimple

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.zMZjL36eAS6x49eS]{Grimple}

You're a grimple, and you've learned that @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.zJxUflt9np0q4yML]{Invisible} hands carrying gross objects are perfect for prop comedy. Your size is Tiny. You can cast mage hand as a primal innate spell at will. As normal, the cantrip is heightened to half your level, rounded up.

Haniver

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.VeUxKodFyMiNQFi7]{Haniver}

You're a haniver, and you're easily able to purloin objects you find interesting. Your size is Tiny. You're trained in Thievery (or another skill if you would automatically be trained in Thievery), and you gain the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.feats-srd.Item.jDdOqFmZLwE4dblQ]{Pickpocket} skill feat as a bonus skill feat. If you succeed at stealing from someone, you can also rifle through and rearrange the contents of the target's pockets, pouches, and other containers. The next time the target attempts to draw a weapon or retrieve a worn item, doing so requires two Interact actions instead of one.

Jinkin

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.ITOjzs5qyaUWTULt]{Jinkin}

You're a jinkin, a master of item cursing and sabotage. Your size is Tiny. You gain a +1 circumstance bonus to Crafting checks related to traps, cursed items, and sabotage. If you're a master in Crafting, this circumstance bonus increases to +2.

Mitflit

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.RmjH0iIuUTlXA1e0]{Mitflit}

You're a mitflit, a gremlin disconnected from fey magic, often derided and bullied by other gremlins, and talented in connecting to arthropods. Your size is Small. You gain a special sense: imprecise scent with a range of 30 feet. This means you can use your sense of smell to determine the exact location of a creature. The GM will usually double the range if you're downwind from the creature or halve the range if you're upwind.

Nuglub

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.F4zbEPJ1aAUMNDzY]{Nuglub}

You're a nuglub, a gremlin who enjoys battle more than any other. Your size is Small. You gain a jaws unarmed attack, which deals 1d6 piercing damage, has the finesse trait, and is in the brawling weapon group.

Pugwampi

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.TV44wJ8HSC2GN906]{Pugwampi}

You’re a pugwampi, a gremlin exemplifying curses, jinxes, and unluck. Your size is Tiny. You gain the @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-actions.Item.IYhpgmgSiS7uaJkK]{Pugwampi Jinx} action.

Vexgit

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.QHvt8BOl99YE96pW]{Vexgit}

You're a vexgit, a heritage of wrecker gremlins who typically enjoy sabotaging, smashing, and destroying objects. Your size is Tiny. You gain a mandibles unarmed attack, which deals 1d4 piercing damage, has the agile and finesse traits, and is in the brawling weapon group.

Ancestry Feats

The following feats are available to gremlin characters.

Mimic Ancestry.name: 拟形怪族裔 Mimic Ancestry
Mimic Ancestry.text:
拟形怪是聪明而贪婪的变形者，能够化作一个或多个杂项物体的形态，传统上包括诸如宝箱之类的容器。

拟形怪通常会避开其他拟形怪，同时对类人生物怀有无穷无尽的迷恋。因此，有些拟形怪决定前往更广阔的世界旅行，探索世界所能提供的一切，也就不足为奇了。尽管拟形怪以其黏附特性而闻名，但过于黏稠会让它们极其迟缓；而成为冒险者的拟形怪会花费数年让自身黏液失活，直到它们能以普通拟形怪两倍的速度移动，大致相当于矮人的速度。即使在类人生物的土地上旅行，拟形怪也常常会产生一种难以抗拒的冲动，想要故技重施。话虽如此，在礼貌社会里伏击并吞食他人通常被认为不可接受。这便引出了另一种较不致命的伏击方式，更像是玩闹：目标是让朋友措手不及、被吓一跳，并观察他们的反应。尽管不像那些愿意在同一间地下城房间里一等就是几十年的拟形怪那般极端，冒险拟形怪仍然十分有耐心；它们明白，周密的计划与等待恰当的出手时机，远比鲁莽地冲入混战更可能成功。耐心、狡诈与谨慎筹划，对拟形怪而言皆为美德。

如果你想扮演一个能够变成物体、并让别人对你真正本性大吃一惊的角色，你就该选择拟形怪。

你可能会……

  • 伪装成无生命的物体来吓到并捉弄你的朋友。
  • 保持耐心，并在恰当的时刻出手。
  • 对类人生物及其生活方式抱持深刻好奇。

其他人多半会……

  • 认为你的外貌格外古怪而醒目。
  • 不论你实际能力如何，都期待你是潜入的大师。
  • 担心如果你太饿，可能会把他们吃掉。

外形描述

拟形怪真正的生理本质一直是学者们争论的主题，有些人甚至质疑，把它们的任何一种形态称为“真实”是否公平。拟形怪像躲瘟疫一样避开同类，很少进行此类反思或讨论；但一旦谈起，它们往往会给出鲜明且相互矛盾的观点。学者与拟形怪普遍同意：变形成物体是与生俱来的，幼年拟形怪仅凭模仿周遭所见的物体，就能本能地学会。此外，它们未变形的身体确实柔软、黏糊且呈紫色，不过通常覆盖着一层几丁质来保护拟形怪。当它们长出伪足、腿、舌头之类结构时，或当有人深深凿开它们的伪装、露出内部时，那紫色便是默认色（当然它们也能努力改变该颜色）。物体形态不仅是一种有效的伪装，也能保护拟形怪更柔软的内脏免受伤害。年轻的拟形怪——包括刚开始冒险的那些——通常只有一个特定的物体形态，并在其中度过大部分时间。在这种情况下，经典的宝箱及其他类似容器是它们最常采用的形态，但拟形怪几乎可以伪装成任何物体。

拟形怪冒险者

拟形怪冒险者往往会选择能让它们伏击或戏弄他人、或能利用它们徒手攻击、或与物品有所联结的职业。游荡者、游侠、武僧与奇术师尤其常见。传统的拟形怪背景往往是在幼体时期作为拾荒者，会类似于拾荒者、猎人，或也许是斥候背景。一名在成为冒险者之前与类人生物相处了相当长时间、足以获得更传统背景的拟形怪，几乎可能来自任何背景。即便如此，它们仍擅长那些强调欺骗或隐秘、并能发挥其天生拟形技能的背景。

社会

拟形怪对其他拟形怪怀有敌意，这在其他祖源中几乎闻所未闻。这意味着除了蜂巢统一者外，几乎不存在所谓的拟形怪社会。事实上，大多数拟形怪甚至从未见过自己的父母。拟形怪通过孢子进行无性繁殖，并成长到猫一般大小的幼体形态；幼体会拾取食物，直到体型足够大并达到完全成体。达到成年可能只需几周，也可能长达数年，取决于拟形怪本身以及该地区可获得的食物数量。这个过程也会引入突变、改变与其他不寻常能力，进而形成不同的拟形怪传承。成体拟形怪还会获得采用另一种形态的能力，通常是幼体拾荒时期令它们印象深刻的某个物体。

随着年龄增长与经验累积，拟形怪通常会逐渐能够变形成越来越多的形态，其中包括更精巧、更富创意、并且可能也更大的形态，因为拟形怪本身也可能在体型上成长。

道路

技艺高超的拟形怪往往会追寻若干社会、文化与心理道路之一，成为巢穴大师、多态贤者、体内炼金术士、行旅美食家，或拟形超越者。拟形怪有时也会在选择其他道路的同时成为紫罗兰引导者或蜂巢统一者，不过紫罗兰引导者很少会成为多态贤者、拟形超越者或体内炼金术士。

蜂巢统一者

蜂巢统一者是所有拟形怪中最不寻常的一类，因为它们拥有一种共同体本能，与其他拟形怪的生物冲动完全相反。蜂巢统一者不会避开同类，反而会被其他拟形怪所吸引，试图建立家庭与社会结构。不幸的是，这样的行动十分艰难，因为它们必须对抗几乎所有拟形怪都具备的天性。大多数蜂巢统一者，以及那些愿意接受其讯息的拟形怪，似乎在转化为成体的过程中发展得略有不同，因为它们会主动寻找同类。偶尔，蜂巢统一者甚至能说服某些行为本就异常的冒险拟形怪加入它们的事业。

蜂巢统一者也发现，它们的后代更可能与它们拥有相同的蜂巢统一者心态，可能是继承了父母的一些特征。这导致了一种其他拟形怪从未有过的行为：蜂巢统一者会有意更频繁地进行孢子化，然后留下来在幼体阶段认识自己的后代。如此一来，蜂巢统一者便能形成多世代家庭，逐渐发展成类似复杂社会的结构。

蜂巢统一者比其他拟形怪更偏向守序，不过它们也可能处于任何可能的阵营；邪恶的蜂巢统一者会建立可怖的屠杀邪教与犯罪组织，而善良的蜂巢统一者则会打造幸福的家庭与和平的社会。

体内炼金术士

体内炼金术士专注于理解物质物体的根本原理，甚至会将自己的身体当作坩埚与实验台来进行试验。体内炼金术士并不特别在意它们如何改变形态或为何能改变形态——这些问题才是多态贤者的动力。相反，它们试图更多了解构成周遭世界的物质，把自己当作工具，而非目的本身。举例来说，一名体内炼金术士可能会学习如何变形并合成各种不同材料，专注于成分结构以及这对材料本身所揭示的内容；而多态贤者则更在意形态与外观，以及变形的过程。

体内炼金术士拟形怪常以笔名行事，凭借其独特方法学进行其他科学家无法完成的实验，从而在材料科学领域取得了诸多突破。然而，它们往往有些一根筋，若在关键实验期间被打断，可能会相当暴躁。不出所料，它们常常成为著名的炼金术士或发明家。

行旅美食家

有一件事将每一只拟形怪——从最微小的幼体到最强大的古老拟形怪——紧密联系在一起：它们爱吃。拟形怪食欲惊人，能吞下大量食物。然而，若有必要，它们也能长时间禁食，使其在饮食与进食安排上极具弹性。不过对某些拟形怪来说，进食不仅是生存所需，更是一种生活方式，而食物本身便是一门艺术。

这些便是行旅美食家——周游世界、寻找自己从未吃过的新奇食物的拟形怪，其中包括拥有独特或鲜明食材、处理方式、摆盘、烹饪风格等的食物。对行旅美食家而言，世界就是它们的牡蛎，而它们还带了蚝油，所以开吃吧！

行旅美食家并不一定是把周围一切都吞噬殆尽的饕餮。若说有什么不同，它们反而比其他拟形怪更挑嘴。大多数拟形怪会吃任何最容易找到并获取的食物，而行旅美食家则偏好独特而丰盛的餐饮体验。尽管如此，只要足够新奇，行旅美食家极其容易被食物或其他味觉体验所驱动。作为冒险者，行旅美食家会寻找更可能探索许多遥远国度的冒险队伍，而不是只在单一故乡执行任务的队伍。显然，这种偏好源于它们想要品尝不同文化与地域料理的渴望。

拟形怪巢穴

拟形怪不会形成聚居地，除了一些特别有野心的蜂巢统一者之外。然而，几乎所有拟形怪都喜爱拥有一个属于自己的巢穴，能够加以保护、布置陷阱，并将其打造成自己的安全之所。尽管巢穴大师把这点推向极端，其他大多数拟形怪也仍有这些倾向；它们对巢穴或家园的渴望，甚至超过对陪伴或交谈的渴望。

巢穴大师

与四处游荡的行旅美食家相反，巢穴大师是偏好定居一处的拟形怪。它们最接近古老而强大的经典拟形怪生活方式：选择一个家或巢穴，并在其中布满陷阱、诡计、秘密通道，以及它们变形成物体时可完美藏身的位置。乍看之下这似乎不适合冒险生活，但对拥有据点的冒险队伍而言——尤其是可能需要防守的据点——它反而运作良好。巢穴大师不讲武德，会搞出各式各样的把戏：从经典的宝箱伎俩，到更复杂的阴谋，例如假装自己是一扇门，或是一道必须解开的巨大实体谜题的组成部分，才能继续深入巢穴。

巢穴大师也是最可能雇佣帮手或领导组织的拟形怪——当然，这些组织绝不会由其他拟形怪组成。它们会建立商行、盗贼公会等等。它们的规划倾向与戒律使巢穴大师相较于混乱阵营更偏向守序阵营，不过这种倾向并不强。它们也较不可能是善良的，因为拥有并保护巢穴本质上更被动且以自我为中心，而善良则要求主动帮助他人。话虽如此，善良的巢穴大师确实存在，甚至不止于善良冒险者为据点布设陷阱：还有些善良巢穴大师会利用其癖好设计奇妙的防护措施，为无辜者、孤儿等建立庇护所。对巢穴大师而言，不存在所谓“手段下作”；只要能通向成功，任何手段都行。

多态贤者

多态贤者是对变化之道及其各种形态与可能性充满求知欲的学者。体内炼金术士将变形视为工具来研究物质，而多态贤者则专注于研究变形本身。拟形怪如何进化？它们变形能力的源头是什么？其极限又在何处？为何特别专注于物体？多态学者会将这些问题以及更多问题推向其逻辑极限。在此追求中，它们常常研究魔法，尤其是变化系魔法。在这一过程中，它们试图理解的变化远不止拟形怪变形本身：多态战斗形态、兽化诅咒等等，都是多态贤者研究的素材。多态贤者甚至会与变形怪及其他变形者结盟——这些变形者拥有与它们不同的限制——以便比较、对照，并体验变化所能提供的更广阔多样性。

多态贤者总在追问变形及其形而上实现方式的问题：当变形增大或缩小时，多出来的质量从何而来、又到哪里去？变形如何如此轻易地处理装备，而不是导致装备脱落或被毁？如果生物的心智会被毒品等体内化学物质影响，那么心智为何在大多数变形中仍不受影响？是什么使某些变化魔法可以叠加，而另一些则彼此排斥并替换先前的变化？是什么影响变形后是否能说话、操控工具与武器，或施放法术？当空间不足以变形时，改变体型的魔法如何调整以避免致命情况？多态贤者正在研究这些问题及更多问题背后的解释。

拟形超越者

拟形超越者只有一个目标：它们认为自己当前的拟形怪形态，只是在通往更崭新、更伟大形态之路上、继幼体形态之后的下一步——一种超越其限制的形态——并追求实现该形态。对大多数拟形超越者而言，它们的新形态会是某种类人生物；在某些方面类似变形怪，但不仅能变形成无生命物体，还能变形成任何活生物。基于此，拟形超越者有时会敬仰变形怪与其他变形者，将其视为自己渴望踏上的道路之典范。超越者的目标常使它们与许多其他祖源产生接触、踏上各式各样的冒险，因此超越者是最常见的冒险者之一。

然而，同样的执念也可能很快以悲剧收场：一次失败的超越，可能导致拟形怪将自身心智与身体变形到无法复原的状态，使其成为强大却近乎无心智的杀戮机器。不必多言，这很罕见，并且完全与拟形超越者的目标背道而驰。事实上，其他听闻同伴遭此命运的拟形超越者，往往会压下自己一贯对与其他拟形怪结交的反感，转而去寻找并安乐死那由此产生的憎恶。毕竟，那是对拟形超越者希望达成之事的悲惨嘲弄；它们也知道，如果同样的事发生在自己身上，它们会希望有他人帮助结束折磨。无论该拟形超越者是善是恶、守序或混乱，这种职业礼节都会被贯彻。即便是最自我中心的拟形超越者，也能在那不幸的超越者身上看到自己黑暗的倒影。

紫罗兰引导者

在公众场合最罕见的拟形怪之一便是紫罗兰引导者，也被称为处于自然形态的拟形怪。其原因尚未被很好理解。也许在幼体阶段没有任何物体引起它们的兴趣，或许是随机突变，又或许拟形怪只是想要“表达立场”。不论原因如何，极少数拟形怪几乎把全部时间都花在其自然形态中：一具柔软的紫色身体，外覆几丁质板甲般的甲片，而非变形去模仿某个物体。大多数紫罗兰引导者在首次成年时便做出这一决定，但拟形怪也能在生命周期的任何阶段选择成为紫罗兰引导者。大多数拟形怪在物体形态中更为自在；在自然状态下，它们几乎会觉得自己“赤裸”，因为在几丁质外壳的关节之间有太多脆弱部位暴露在外。而紫罗兰引导者则相反：它们在物体形态中常感到磨得难受，就像类人生物可能会抗拒经常穿着扎人、刺痒或不舒服的礼服一样。归根结底，这取决于哪种形态能让拟形怪更舒适、压力更小；而大多数紫罗兰引导者只是与其他拟形怪有着相反的倾向。

这并不意味着所有紫罗兰引导者都是出于舒适才做出选择。对某些拟形怪而言，这是出于理念：做“自己”，而不是别的什么。有些理念型紫罗兰引导者认为自己才是唯一真正的拟形怪，并觉得其他祖源将拟形怪描绘成宝箱或其他物体形态是一种冒犯。不过，其中大多数也承认拟形怪千姿百态、体型各异、信念多元，因此它们接受其他形态对其他拟形怪来说也很好，即便那不适合自己。这一观点使紫罗兰引导者与试图彻底成为“他者”的拟形超越者直接对立。

拟形怪美学

大多数拟形怪都有非常独特的审美感，尤其体现在其巢穴中家具的摆放与选择上。陈设不仅能成就或毁掉房间的氛围；它们也为拟形怪提供关键的藏身点与伏击位置。从这个意义上说，装饰确实可能关乎生死！

阵营与宗教

尽管许多拟形怪是中立的，只是为自己与自身热情而活、同时寻找食物，但也有许多个体表现出向其他阵营倾斜的趋势。在这些之中，守序与中立同样普遍，但邪恶拟形怪比善良拟形怪稍多一些。即便如此，善良拟形怪也并不罕见到会比“拟形怪冒险者”本身更罕见。

拟形怪往往信奉与自身道路相关的神祇：例如，多态贤者会信奉与魔法知识或变化相关的神祇；巢穴大师会信奉与防御或陷阱相关的神祇；蜂巢统一者会信奉与社群与家庭相关的神祇。另一方面，无论走哪条道路，拟形怪也常常喜爱与食物、狩猎、诡计或惊喜相关的神祇。在阿拉卡尔，过度女王艾布鲁格瑟斯一直是经久不衰的最爱，诡计女神塞瓦也同样受欢迎；后者也被称为“小丑”或“游荡者”。名字 让其他祖源的成员感到意外的是，大多数拟形怪没有名字。鉴于它们的独居天性、以孢子繁殖，以及对其他拟形怪毫无兴趣，这也许并不那么令人惊讶。尽管如此，选择离开地下城、前往外部世界的类人生物社会的拟形怪会为自己取一个名字。由于即便是地下城拟形怪也对外界好奇，并会与探索地下城的人交谈，甚至有些地下城拟形怪也会在这些对话中获得名字。有些拟形怪珍视这个被赋予的名字，尤其当名字来自对其重要的人；而另一些则对其嗤之以鼻，不认为那名字真正属于自己，只把它当作方便之器。后者很容易辨认，因为它们常会说“你可以叫我……”而不是“我的名字是……”或“我是……”。与大多数事情一样，许多拟形怪介于这两种极端之间。

因为是由他人命名，拟形怪名字并无共通性，取决于决定给它们命名者的品味与祖源。它们可能使用该祖源的常见名字、一个普通词汇、对拟形怪偏好物体形态的描述、拟形怪最爱的食物，或任何其他方法。拟形怪通过孢子进行无性繁殖，天生无性别，这使得它们绝大多数为无性别者，并且若不进行更深入研究，未必能理解类人生物关于性别的概念。这类人类学研究正是拟形超越者真心欣赏的活动；在拟形怪之中，它们也最可能探索并表达除无性别以外的性别认同。由于名字由他人赋予，拟形怪有时会拥有在其他文化中常被视为带有性别色彩的名字（例如下方示例名字中的 Violet），但拟形怪并不这么看。名字就是名字。

下方的示例名字仅为方便列出，旨在展示可能性的广泛多样，而非暗示任何趋势。

示例名字

Chester、Erendethil、Mim、Steak、Trick、Violet、Zarathur。

拟形怪传承

拟形怪常在幼体阶段产生突变，与其他拟形怪相比获得不寻常的怪癖。你在1级时从下列拟形怪传承中选择一个。

两栖拟形怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.GEzWOxifT8i7TftT]{两栖拟形怪}

作为幼体时，你必须穿越地下城家园的水下区域来寻找营养，于是你突变获得了有限的适应水环境能力。你获得10尺游泳速度与两栖特征。与所有具有两栖特征的生物一样，你可以同时呼吸水与空气。

猎手拟形怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.FkrKIhF2jExdNRiT]{猎手拟形怪}

大多数拟形怪在幼体时期主要通过拾荒获得食物，而你凭借卓越的嗅觉成功狩猎到大部分食物。你获得一种特殊感官：不精确嗅觉，射程30尺。这意味着你可以用嗅觉来确定一个生物的确切位置。GM通常会在你处于生物下风向时将该射程加倍，而在你处于上风向时将该射程减半。此外，当你尝试定位位于你嗅觉射程内的@Compendium[pf2e.conditionitems.VRSef5y1LmL2Hkjf]{未被侦测}生物时，你的察觉检定获得+2环境加值。

迷你拟形怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.XHZqw4g9tCj3MFox]{迷你拟形怪}

你在摄取远少于正常所需食物的情况下达到了成体形态，因此你比其他拟形怪更小、更灵活。你的体型为小型而非中型。此外，你获得杂技技能的受训（熟练度）等级；若你已受训于杂技，则改为获得你选择的另一项技能的受训（熟练度）等级；并获得@Compendium[pf2e.feats-srd.3G8xUlgCjRmRJNfP]{快速挤身}技能专长。

多重执念拟形怪

Mimics are clever and voracious shapeshifters capable of taking the form of one or more miscellaneous objects, traditionally including containers such as treasure chests.

Mimics typically avoid other mimics while possessing an endless fascination with humanoids. Thus, it's unsurprising that some mimics decide to travel into the wider world and explore all the world has to offer. While mimics are known for their adhesive properties, being so sticky makes them incredibly sluggish, and adventuring mimics spend years denaturing their adhesive to the point where they can move twice as fast as a normal mimic, roughly the speed of a dwarf. Even when traveling in humanoid lands, mimics frequently get an irresistible urge to resort to their old tricks. That said, it's generally considered to be unacceptable to ambush and devour other people in polite society. This leads to a less lethal kind of ambush, more like play, where the goal is to surprise and startle a friend and watch their reaction. While they are less so than mimics who are willing to wait decades in the same dungeon room, adventuring mimics are still patient, and they understand that a careful plan and waiting to strike at the right moment are far more likely to succeed than rushing into the fray recklessly. Patience, cunning, and careful planning are all virtues to a mimic.

If you want to play a character capable of transforming into an object and surprising others as to your true nature, you should play a mimic.

You Might...

  • Disguise as an inanimate object to startle and prank your friends.
  • Remain patient and strike at the right moment.
  • Possess a deep curiosity about humanoids and their way of life.

Others Probably...

  • Consider your appearance especially unusual and distinctive.
  • Expect you to be a master of infiltration, regardless of your actual capabilities.
  • Wonder if you might eat them if you get too hungry.

Physical Description

Mimics' true physical nature is the subject of debate among scholars, some of whom wonder whether it's really fair to call any of their forms "true." Mimics, who avoid their own kind like the plague, rarely engage in such reflection or discussion, but when they do, they often have distinctive and conflicting opinions. Scholars and mimics generally agree that object shapeshifting is innate, something young mimics pick up instinctually purely through imitation of the objects they see around them. It's also true that their un-shapeshifted bodies are soft, squishy, and purple, though usually protected by a layer of chitin to protect the mimic. When they develop pseudopods, legs, tongues, or the like, or when someone carves deep into their disguise to reveal their insides, the purple color is the default (they can still make an effort to change that color as well). An object form is not only an effective disguise; it also helps protect the mimic's softer innards from damage. Young mimics, including those just starting on their adventures, typically have one specific object form in which they spend most of their time. In those cases, the classic treasure chests and other similar containers are by far the most common form for them to take, but mimics can disguise themselves as nearly any object.

Mimic Adventurers

Mimic adventurers tend to choose classes that let them ambush or trick others or make use of their unarmed attacks or connections to items. Rogues, rangers, monks, and thaumaturges are especially common. A traditional mimic background as a scavenger in larval form would always be something akin to the scavenger, hunter, or maybe scout backgrounds. A mimic who spent a substantial amount of time with humanoids before becoming an adventurer, long enough to gain a more traditional background, could be from almost any background. Even so, they excel at deceptive or stealthy backgrounds that make use of their natural mimic skills

Society

Mimics bear an antipathy toward other mimics, which is vanishingly rare across other ancestries. This means that there is nearly no such thing as mimic society, with the exception of hive unifiers. In fact, most mimics never even meet their parents. Mimics reproduce asexually via spores and grow until they reach a larval form the size of a cat which scavenges food until it grows large enough and reaches full adulthood. Achieving adulthood can take anywhere from just a few weeks to several years, depending on the mimic and the amount of food available in the area. This process also introduces mutations, changes, and other unusual abilities in the adult mimic, leading to various mimic heritages. An adult mimic also gains the ability to adopt an alternate form, typically an object that was memorable to them during their larval scavenging.

As they age and grow in experience, mimics usually become capable of transforming into more and more forms, including forms that are more intricate, more creative, and potentially larger as well, as the mimic might grow in size.

Paths

Often an accomplished mimic pursues one of several social, cultural, and psychological paths, becoming a lair master, a polymorphic sage, an internal alchemist, an itinerant gourmand, or a mimic transcendent. Mimics sometimes also become violet ushers and hive unifiers in tandem with one of the other paths, although a violet usher rarely becomes a polymorphic sage, mimic transcendent, or internal alchemist.

Hive Unifiers

Hive unifiers are the most unusual of all mimics in that they possess a communal instinct that runs contrary to all other mimics' biological impulses. Rather than avoiding others of their kind, hive unifiers are drawn to other mimics and seek to form a familial and societal structure. Unfortunately, such activities are challenging, as they work against the natural instincts that nearly all mimics possess. Most hive unifiers and those mimics who are open to their message seemed to have developed slightly differently during their transformation to adulthood as they actively look for others of their kind. Occasionally, hive unifiers even convince other adventuring mimics, already unusual in their behaviors, to join their cause.

Hive unifiers also find that their own offspring are more likely to share their mentality as hive unifiers, possibly due to inheriting some of their parent's traits. This leads to a behavior unknown to other mimics, where hive unifiers intentionally sporulate more often and then stick around to get to know their offspring during their larval states. In this way, hive unifiers can form multigenerational families that come to resemble complex societies of their own.

Hive unifiers tend more towards law than other mimics, though they range through every possible alignment, with evil hive unifiers creating terrifying slaughter cults and crime syndicates while good hive unifiers forge happy families and peaceful societies.

Internal Alchemists

Internal alchemists have a keen focus on understanding the fundamentals of physical objects, even to the extent of using their own bodies as crucibles and testbeds for experimentation. Internal alchemists aren't especially concerned with how they change shapes or why—those questions are what motivate polymorphic sages. Instead, they seek to learn more about the matter that makes up the world around them, using themselves as a tool, not an end in and of itself. For instance, an internal alchemist might learn how to transform into and synthesize all sorts of different materials, focusing on compositional structure and what that reveals about the different materials themselves, whereas a polymorphic sage cares about the form and shape, as well as the process of the transformation.

Internal alchemist mimics, operating under pen names, have discovered numerous breakthroughs in the field of material science thanks to their unique methodologies allowing experiments no other scientist could perform. However, they tend to be somewhat single-minded and can be pretty irritable if interrupted during a crucial experiment. Unsurprisingly, they often tend to become famous alchemists or inventors.

Itinerant Gourmands

One thing unites every mimic, from the tiniest larval form to the most powerful elder mimic: they love to eat. Mimics are ravenous and can consume vast quantities of food. However, they can also fast for long periods if necessary, making them highly flexible with their diet and feeding schedule. However, for some mimics, eating is more than a necessity, it is a way of life, and food is an art form in and of itself.

These are itinerant gourmands, mimics who travel the world looking for new and unusual foods they've never eaten before, including food with unique or distinctive ingredients, preparation styles, presentation, cooking styles, and more. To an itinerant gourmand, the world is their oyster, and they've brought oyster sauce, so it's time to dig in!

Itinerant gourmands are not necessarily gluttons who devour anything and everything around them. If anything, they are more likely than other mimics to be choosier eaters. While most mimics will eat whatever food is easiest to locate and procure, itinerant gourmands prefer unique and rich culinary experiences. Nonetheless, itinerant gourmands are incredibly easy to motivate via food or other gustatory experiences, so long as they are sufficiently novel. As adventurers, itinerant gourmands seek adventuring parties likely to explore many distant lands rather than those with missions in only one hometown. Naturally, this preference comes about due to the desire to sample cuisines from various cultures and locales.

Mimic Lairs

Mimics don't form settlements except for a few particularly ambitious hive unifiers. Still, nearly all mimics love having a lair of their own that they can protect, booby trap, and otherwise make into a safe place for themselves. While lair masters take this to extremes, most other mimics still have these tendencies and yearn for a lair or home even more than companionship or conversation.

Lair Masters

As opposed to wandering itinerant gourmands, lair masters are mimics who prefer to stay in one place. They are most similar to the classic lifestyle of an ancient and powerful mimic in that they choose a home or lair and litter it with traps, tricks, secret passages, and perfect places for them to hide while transformed into an object. While this might seem unsuited for an adventuring lifestyle, it works well for adventuring parties who have a home base, especially one they might need to defend. Lair masters don't play fair, and they engage in all sorts of shenanigans, from the classic treasure chest ploy to more complex schemes like pretending to be a door or a component of a giant physical puzzle that must be solved to continue through the lair.

Lair masters are also the mimics most likely to hire help or lead organizations, though never composed of other mimics, of course. They form businesses, thieves' guilds, and more. Their tendencies towards planning and discipline lead lair masters to tend towards lawful alignments more than chaotic ones, though the tendency is only minor. They are also less likely to be good, as fundamentally owning and protecting a lair is more passive and self-focused, while good requires actively helping others. That said, good lair masters exist even beyond the good adventurers boobytrapping their home base: those other good lair masters use their proclivities to design marvelous protective measures to create sanctuaries for innocents, orphans, and the like. To a lair master, there's no such thing as poor form; anything goes as long as it leads to success.

Polymorphic Sages

Polymorphic sages are inquisitive scholars of transformation in all its various forms and possibilities. While internal alchemists study matter using shapeshifting as a tool, polymorphic sages focus instead on the study of shapeshifting itself. How do mimics evolve? What is the source of their shapeshifting abilities? And where are its limits? Why the focus on objects in particular? Polymorphic scholars test all these questions and more to their logical extremes. In this pursuit, they often study magic, particularly transmutation magic. During this pursuit, they try to understand far more forms of transmutation than just mimic shapeshifting. Polymorph battle forms, the curse of the werecreature, and more are grist for the mill of a polymorphic sage's research. Polymorphic sages even team up with doppelgangers and other shapeshifters who possess different limitations than themselves to compare, contrast, and experience a wider variety of what transformation offers.

Polymorphic sages are always asking questions about transformation and its metaphysical implementation: When a transformation increases or decreases in size, where does the extra mass come from, and where does it go? How do transformations manage to account for equipment so easily, rather than leading to equipment falling off or being destroyed? If a creature's mind can be affected by internal chemicals such as drugs, how does the mind stay unaffected by most transformations? What allows some transformation magic to combine while others are mutually exclusive and replace prior transmutations? What influences whether a transformation can speak, manipulate tools and weapons, or cast spells? How does size-changing magic adjust to prevent deadly situations when there isn't enough space to transform? Polymorphic sages are studying the explanations behind all these questions and more.

Mimic Transcendents

Mimic transcendents have only one goal: they consider their current mimic form to simply be the next one after their larval form on the path to a new and greater form, one that transcends their limitations, and they seek to achieve that form. To most mimic transcendents, their new form would be some kind of humanoid, similar to a doppelganger in some ways but capable of shapeshifting into any living creature in addition to inanimate objects. For this reason, mimic transcendents sometimes revere doppelgangers and other shapeshifters as exemplars of the path they wish to travel. Transcendents' goal often brings them into contact with many other ancestries and onto all sorts of adventures, making transcendents some of the most common adventurers.

However, that same obsession can also quickly end in tragedy, as a failed transcendence can lead a mimic to transform their mind and body into an irrecoverable state, leading them to become a powerful but near- mindless killing machine. Needless to say, this is rare, and it is entirely antithetical to the goal of mimic transcendents. In fact, other mimic transcendents who hear about a fellow who met such a fate often overcome their usual displeasure for camaraderie with other mimics in order to find and euthanize the resulting abomination. After all, it's a tragic fate that spits in the face of what mimic transcendents hoped to accomplish, and they know that if the same happened to them, they would want to have another help to end their torment. This professional courtesy is extended whether the mimic transcendent is good or evil, lawful or chaotic. Even the most self-centered mimic transcendent can see themselves through a dark reflection in the ill-fated transcendent.

Violet Ushers

One of the rarest mimics to see in public is the violet usher, also known as a mimic in its natural form. Why this occurs is not well understood. Perhaps no objects caught their interest during their larval state, or maybe it's a random mutation, or perhaps the mimic simply wishes to "make a statement." Whatever the reason, a very small number of mimics spend nearly all their time in their natural form, a squishy purple body protected by chitinous plates, rather than transform to mimic an object. Most violet ushers make this decision upon first becoming adults, but mimics can choose to become violet ushers at any point during their life cycle. Most mimics feel more comfortable in an object form, feeling almost "naked" in their natural state, with so many vulnerable parts exposed between the joints of the chitinous outer shell. For their part, however, violet ushers often feel a chafing in object form, much as humanoids might balk at wearing itchy, scratchy, or uncomfortable formal wear on a regular basis. Ultimately, it comes down to what brings the mimic greater comfort and less stress, and most violet ushers simply have the reverse tendency to other mimics.

That's not to say that all violet ushers make the choice due to comfort. For some, it's an ideological decision to be "themselves" rather than something else. Some ideological violet ushers consider themselves to be the only true mimics and find depictions of mimics by other ancestries as treasure chests and other object forms, to be offensive. Most of them, however, recognize that mimics come in all shapes, sizes, and beliefs, and thus they accept that other forms are fine for those other mimics, even if it wasn't right for them. This view puts violet ushers directly at odds with mimic transcendents, which seek to fully become the other.

Mimic Aesthetics

Most mimics have a very particular aesthetic sense, especially when it comes to the inclusion of furniture in their lairs. Furnishings don't just make or break a room's ambiance; they also provide critical hiding spots and ambush points for the mimic. In this way, decoration can truly be a matter of life and death!

Alignment and Religion

While many mimics are neutral, simply living life for themselves and their passions while looking for food, there are many who show tendencies toward other alignments. Of those, lawful and neutral are equally prevalent, but evil mimics are somewhat more common than good. Even so, good mimics aren't so unusual as to be more unusual among mimics than mimic adventurers are in the first place.

Mimics tend to believe in deities associated with their path, for instance, deities of magical knowledge or transformation for polymorphic sages, defense or traps for lair masters, and community and family for hive unifiers. On the other hand, no matter the path, mimics are often fond of deities of food, hunting, tricks, or surprises. On Alacar, Ebrugeses the Queen of Excess is a perennial favorite, as is the trickster goddess Sewa, also known as the Fool or the Rogue. Names It surprises members of other ancestries to know that most mimics don't have a name. Due to their solitary nature, spore-based reproduction, and utter disinterest in other mimics, perhaps it shouldn't be so surprising. Nonetheless, mimics who choose to leave their dungeons and head to the humanoid societies in the outside world choose a name for themselves. Since even dungeon mimics are curious about the outside world and hold conversations with dungeon delvers, even some of those dungeon mimics gain names through those conversations. Some mimics cherish this given name, especially if it was given by someone important to them, while others hold it in contempt and don't consider the name to be truly theirs, merely a tool of convenience. Those latter mimics are easy to spot, as they will often say, "You can call me..." rather than "My name is..." or "I am..." As in most things, many mimics lie somewhere between these two extremes.

Because others name them, there's no commonality in mimic names, as it depends on the sensibilities and ancestry of the person who decided to name them. They might use a regular name for their ancestry, a common word, a description of the mimic's favored object form, the mimic's favorite food, or any other method. Mimics reproduce asexually via spores and are inherently sexless, which leads to the vast majority of them being agender and not necessarily understanding the humanoid concept of gender without deeper study. This anthropological research is the type of activity mimic transcendents sincerely appreciate, and they are the most likely among mimics to explore and express a gender identity other than agender. Due to being named by others, mimics sometimes have names that other cultures might often see as gendered (for instance, Violet in the Sample Names below), but mimics don't see it that way. A name is a name.

The Sample Names below are listed for convenience and are meant to show the wide variety of possibilities rather than to be indicative of any trends.

Sample Names

Chester, Erendethil, Mim, Steak, Trick, Violet, Zarathur.

Mimic Heritages

Mimics often develop mutations during their larval stage, which provide unusual quirks compared to other mimics. Choose one of the following mimic heritages at 1st level.

Amphibious Mimic

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.GEzWOxifT8i7TftT]{Amphibious Mimic}

As a larva, you had to traverse underwater portions of your dungeon home to find sustenance, and you mutated to gain a limited ability to handle the water. You gain a swim Speed of 10 feet and the amphibious trait. Like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air.

Hunter Mimic

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.FkrKIhF2jExdNRiT]{Hunter Mimic}

While most mimics get most of their food during their larval form via scavenging, you managed to hunt most of your own food thanks to your exceptional sense of smell. You gain a special sense: imprecise scent with a range of 30 feet. This means you can use your sense of smell to determine the exact location of a creature. The GM will usually double the range if you're downwind from the creature or halve the range if you're upwind. In addition, you gain a +2 circumstance bonus to Perception checks whenever you're trying to locate an @Compendium[pf2e.conditionitems.VRSef5y1LmL2Hkjf]{Undetected} creature that is within the range of your scent.

Mini Mimic

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.XHZqw4g9tCj3MFox]{Mini Mimic}

You managed to reach your adult form while consuming far less food than normal, and you are smaller and more flexible than other mimics as a result. Your size is Small instead of Medium. Additionally, you gain the trained proficiency rank in the Acrobatics skill, or the trained proficiency rank in another skill of your choice if you were already trained in Acrobatics, as well as the @Compendium[pf2e.feats-srd.3G8xUlgCjRmRJNfP]{Quick Squeeze} skill feat.

Multiple Fixation Mimic

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.dmdttubdGuAMjj8p]{Multiple Fixation Mimic}

You have even better control over projecting legs and selectively weakening your adhesive than most adventuring mimics, allowing you to move at a speed that puts sedentary dungeon mimics completely to shame. Your Speed is 25 feet, instead of 20 feet.

Quicksilver Mimic

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.MNJwGzigRXurvAYH]{Quicksilver Mimic}

You have even better control over projecting legs and selectively weakening your adhesive than most adventuring mimics, allowing you to move at a speed that puts sedentary dungeon mimics completely to shame. Your Speed is 25 feet, instead of 20 feet.

Trickster Mimic

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.EAUygBvhN1wqsnFl]{Trickster Mimic}

During your larval form, you mutated to gain basic magical tricks you could use to fool others aurally and synergize with your object form. You can cast @Compendium[pf2e.spells-srd.atlgGNI1E1Ox3O3a]{Ghost Sound} as an innate occult cantrip.

Minotaur Ancestry.name: 牛头人族裔 Minotaur Ancestry
Minotaur Ancestry.text:
牛头人是高大强壮的人型生物，长着牛一般的头部，配有犄角、毛皮与蹄子。他们热爱迷宫、谜题，并喜欢用狡黠的手段解决自己的问题。

世人眼中的牛头人形象，是由善于操弄的恶魔领主巴风特——邪恶牛头人之主——以及人们自身的偏见共同描绘出的残忍而愚钝的猎手。巴风特本身就是天才；他更喜欢让他人低估自己的崇拜者，同时在其追随者之中推动一种会扩散这种误解的文化。然而，尽管其他人型文化带有偏见的怪物志中，对刻板的牛头人描写为愚蠢、放纵且邪恶，那些真正尝试亲自探索牛头人社会的外来者却发现，许多牛头人的生活与外来者所预期的刻板印象相去甚远。牛头人在巴风特将他们纳入麾下之前便早已存在；他们拥有无数不涉及巴风特的创世故事，而嫉妒的恶魔领主竭力压制其中许多故事。他更希望所有人——无论是否为牛头人——都相信他垄断了牛头人的信仰。

由于围绕他们的诸多神话，大多数与巴风特毫无瓜葛的牛头人往往在封闭的社群中和平生活，为自己的食物狩猎或耕作，并自行解决问题。然而，面对巨大危险与超自然威胁时，没有任何族裔能永远独处，牛头人也不例外。席卷世界各地、日益增多的冒险在牛头人的精神深处搅动起某种东西。别人可能在接连不断的灾害重压下惊慌失措或崩溃，牛头人却看见一座不断分岔延展的难题迷宫，唯有以狡黠的解法方能破解。别人可能退缩，牛头人却挺身而出；因此，越来越多的牛头人成为了冒险者，并加入构成周遭世界的众多人型社会。

当然，这一过程并不总是容易。他们踏入的世界里，有许多人习惯于依据巴风特的崇拜者来理解牛头人，因此很可能会引发无数误会。尽管如此，牛头人仍决心在这场如迷宫般复杂的挑战中找到属于自己的道路。有些牛头人很有耐心，努力以温和的方式解释真相，让他人愿意接受而不至于反感并固守己见。另一些则认为，抱持偏见信念的人才应当付出努力，而非牛头人；他们只管按自己选择的方式生活。

如果你想扮演一名高大、牛首的角色，天生擅长理清复杂问题并找到简单解法，那么你就该扮演牛头人。

你可能会……

  • 热爱解决迷宫与谜题，尤其是实体谜题。
  • 享受竞争带来的刺激。
  • 以蛮力与狡黠策略巧妙结合的方式战斗。

其他人多半会……

  • 基于你的动物特征或他们对崇拜巴风特的牛头人的了解，低估你的智力。
  • 因你的身高而请你帮忙拿到高处的东西。
  • 担心你的力量会让你像闯进瓷器店的公牛一样打碎精巧的物体。

生理描述

牛头人高大且肌肉发达，通常身高约10尺；不过某些牛头人传承显著更矮，平均更接近7尺。他们没有那些为了更强力量而向巴风特献祭智力的牛头人那般的触及或肌肉量，但他们以机敏与适应力绰绰有余地弥补。对其他人型而言，牛头人最醒目的特征是他们的牛首，以及华丽的犄角。许多牛头人对自己的角颇为自豪，喜欢加以装饰：在角上戴饰物、涂绘或刻绘如纹身般的艺术，以及无数其他展示方式。相比头与角，牛头人的蹄子就不那么显眼了，但这也使他们在鞋履上需要做出一些调整。

牛头人冒险者

牛头人冒险者偏爱能让他们穿行于实体或隐喻迷宫的职业。这些职业可能包括游侠、游荡者、德鲁伊或野蛮人等探索者，也可能是更奥秘的选项，例如适合谜语者牛头人的调查员，或适合超常穿线者的灵能者与奇术师。牛头人也偏好具有相似主题的背景，例如动物低语者、考古学家、占星师、赏金猎人、信使、侦探、使者、角斗士、隐士、猎人、武学门徒、游牧民、水手、斥候、教师与战士。

社会

牛头人在世界各地拥有种类繁多的社会，每一个都拥有丰厚的历史、文化与民间传说。牛头人社会的一项共同点是：大多数牛头人都爱听好故事、夸张故事或谜团——越是曲折离奇、转折越多越好。

篱群

牛头人有时会把自己分为“篱群”，即一群对迷宫、解题与生活有各自方式的牛头人。有些牛头人聚居地与文化会由一个或少数几个篱群占据强势多数；另一些则由各篱群均衡混合而成。篱群并非排他；因此，特别热衷于解决多种类型迷宫的牛头人，可能同时加入两个或更多篱群。

地平线转角者

对地平线转角者而言，整个世界，甚至整个多元宇宙，都是一座巨大的迷宫；而解开迷宫、找到其真正意义的唯一方式，就是探索每一个角落。该篱群之名源于一种观念：地平线就像迷宫中的转折、弯道或岔口——你无法完全看清另一侧有什么。地平线转角者迫不及待想知道转角处有什么、下一道地平线之外有什么。这使他们相当可能成为冒险者：离开牛头人社群，探索外面勇敢的新世界，并结识其中的人。这也常使地平线转角者与织锦编织者结为盟友；有些牛头人会同时属于这两个篱群。地平线转角者喜欢撰写游记，记录他们众多的旅程与冒险，以广阔世界的可能性激励他人；这也引出了该篱群之名的另一层含义——让他人仅凭翻过一页，便能探索新的地平线。

地平线转角者略更可能偏向混沌而非法度，不过他们也可能是任何阵营，这取决于他们在所探索的土地上采取何种行动。无论走到哪里都寻求帮助有需要之人的通常为善；而掠夺与洗劫者几乎总是邪恶。

断结者

断结者得名于一则传奇：一位古代牛头人被挑战去解开一个难到不可能的绳结，他选择用一把巨斧将其斩断。正如传奇所示，断结者拥有他们自己简单而优雅的策略，用以解开复杂难题、令人苦恼的阴谋与人生的一切谜团：以迅猛的蛮力运用，劈开遮蔽真相的复杂性。断结者偏好锻炼力量并使用简单解法，并不意味着他们不聪明，或无法提出更复杂的方案。断结者也曾深思各种可能，并考量其他篱群；他们只是决定自己更喜欢直接的方式。断结者篱群包含智力跨度很大的牛头人，甚至也有一些来自乍看会令人意外的背景。例如，法师看起来可能不像断结者，毕竟他们爱书与学术；但当牛头人塑能师觉得以爆轰式魔法的直接运用才是解决大多数问题的最佳方式时，他们有时也会倾向成为断结者。

断结者更可能偏向混沌而非法度。这是因为“斩断绳结”的解法往往是跳出框架的方案，要求牛头人越过默认语境与指示，给出出人意料的答案；而法度角色更可能按部就班。虽然人们很容易以为断结者会更暴力、因此更可能邪恶，但直接运用力量并不必然意味着暴力解法；善与中立阵营的断结者也很常见。

心灵潜探者

对心灵潜探者而言，心智是一座我们从未能完全探索或理解的迷宫，总是充满新的未解之谜。对他们来说，迷宫并非真正的实体地点，而更像一种被困住或纠缠在岔路花园中的心境。出于这个原因，心灵潜探者非常重视隐喻，并常将其与观想、自由联想、冥想等技巧一起使用，以便尝试用更具体、也更易理解与导航的方式，解释复杂而朦胧的心理状态。哲学，以及对心智如何扎根于更宏大的本体整体之中的更深理解，也都是心灵潜探者的重要探索领域。

虽然心灵潜探者篱群常与能探索心智的施法者（如灵能者）相关联，但加入该篱群并不保证与魔法技艺有任何联系；确实有许多成员完全无法施展魔法。相反，这些非魔法的心灵潜探者会使用世俗的观察、沉思与理解技巧，纯粹通过心智过程探索自己与周围人的内心。无论是否采用神秘方法论，心灵潜探者都不会止步于发现心智的秘密。他们会将所学用于推进其他目标：无论是提供治疗与咨询、帮助有需要的人，还是操纵他人以服务于心灵潜探者的事业，或无数其他原因。

心灵潜探者不倾向于任何特定阵营，因为对心智的知识既可用于疗愈受损的精神，也可用于将他人扭转向心灵潜探者的意志。正如心境可能状态无穷无尽，心灵潜探者也在整个阵营光谱中表达自我。

牛头人聚居地

牛头人聚居地往往以刻意迷宫化、复杂化的方式来构筑，拥有令人印象深刻的建筑与令人困惑的布局。这通常是有意遮蔽与确实不需要简单、易导航的街道规划两者结合的结果。毕竟，牛头人几乎能轻松导航任何东西。这使牛头人聚居地更易抵御入侵势力，同时也能形成绝美的建筑排列，其景象是大多数族裔从未见过的。然而，对其他来访族裔而言，若无相当程度的困难，几乎不可能在其中找到方向。

超常穿线者

超常穿线者相信，世界上发生的一切都彼此相连，形成一张纠缠的魔法与神秘可能性之网。他们的迷宫最好的表现形式并非代表实体转折的蓝图，而是一块巨大的阴谋板，上面贴着照片、文字剪报，并用红线或丝线将一切连接起来。超常穿线者能以与其他牛头人穿行实体迷宫时同样自然、本能的狡黠，在这些复杂的超常可能性中导航；他们会沿着他人无法建立的联系游走，最终抵达真相。

由于兴趣重叠，超常穿线者也有点可能加入谜语者篱群，或拥有作为谜语者的挚友与盟友。他们也希望与解题者篱群同样紧密相关，因为超常穿线者相信，他们个人那些错综复杂的阴谋与秘密，正是他人也应当共同优先处理的巨大社会问题；但不幸的是，大多数解题者并不这么认为。超常穿线者习惯于比直接、具体的证据领先三步；他人的轻视往往要到为时已晚才停止。正因如此，只要他人愿意给超常穿线者一个机会，倾听他们的解释，并敞开心胸接受“尽管缺乏证据、他们也可能是正确的”这一可能性，就常常很容易与他们成为朋友。

超常穿线者能在他人看不到的地方发现模式；这可能使他们最终走向法度或混沌阵营，取决于他们如何抵达。他们略更可能处于中立或在道德轴上，而不是成为纯粹的善或邪恶。

解题者

解题者希望为他们的家庭、社群、甚至世界所面临的最大问题找到解决方案。他们将自己视为务实的现实主义者：着眼全局，并愿意在他人失败后仍去解决大问题。为此，他们常试图在自己与谜语者之间划清界限——他们觉得谜语者关注的是琐碎的小谜团，常常只是为了“好玩”；他们也与超常穿线者区分开来——他们觉得后者在自身兴趣与专长领域钻得太深，过度聚焦在未必最重要的问题上。这并不是说他们不相信谜语者或超常穿线者所做之事有用，但他们确实认为那不如自己的贡献更具建设性，并且不喜欢被不了解篱群差异的人混为一谈。在那些“迫切的大问题”是某种需要以冒险来解决的威胁的地区，解题者是最可能成为冒险者的牛头人之一。因为他们不只是坐在后室里构思方案、再交给他人执行的思考者；解题者想站在前线，在把想法变为现实所需的两座迷宫之间穿行——复杂规划与临场应变。他们坚定、勤勉，也足够勇敢；即便计划要求他们承受艰辛或危险也在所不惜。解题者很少邪恶，因为大多数邪恶牛头人并不关心解决更大的问题，而是只关注自己；不过，确实存在邪恶的解题者，他们会为帮助自己的家庭而牺牲或毁灭任何事物。目睹他们在自认完全正当的同时，仍能以细致且毫不犹豫的务实去实施暴行，令人不寒而栗。取决于解题者是专精于提前规划，还是擅长随机应变，他们往往倾向法度或混沌阵营；而典型解题者若两者兼采，则会处于法度与混沌之间的中立阵营。

谜语者

谜语者致力于解决谜题、谜语、谜案、密码以及其他类似难题。他们将更宏大的谜团与复杂的解谜追猎视为自己的迷宫，并以机智、狡黠与智力穿行其中，既解开较小的碎片，也解开更大的元谜题。

虽然大多数谜语者专注于解谜，但也有一些喜欢反过来扮演角色：为他人发明谜题与谜案。因此，谜语者篱群的牛头人聚会有时会以“谜语圆桌”的形式出现：一位谜语者提出一个谜题，最先解出的人提出下一个，如此整夜持续，让他们在点心与欢笑中共享新谜题。另一些谜语者则会创造几乎不可能的谜题与谜案，目的是让他人困惑，而不是期待它们被解开。

谜语者与解题者的差异在于：他们追求的是特别棘手的谜团，而不是为现实世界的问题寻找务实解法；而且他们不太在意谜团是虚构还是真实，是琐碎还是重要。尽管如此，有些谜语者会成为侦探或调查员，致力于破解真实案件，并将其视为对自身能力的终极考验：以自己的机智对抗一位以恶毒生计为赌注的犯罪策划者。

谜语者显著更可能偏向法度而非混沌，因为在破译谜团时，有条理且按步骤的方法往往很有帮助；不过，采取狂野、跳出框架方式的谜语者有时也会是混沌。谜语者也略更可能处于善恶之间的中立，因为他们通常对谜语本身更感兴趣，而不太在意解开它们会对他人有益还是有害；不过，解谜本身也很少产生特别恶意的效果。

织锦编织者

织锦编织者认为，人与人之间复杂的关系与联结之网，是史上最狡诈的迷宫。他们认为，当与各种不同族裔共同生活时，这条公理更为准确——这些族裔的世界观惊人地不同。其篱群之名中的“织锦”，是社会丰富织锦的隐喻。他们进一步延伸这一隐喻，解释道：正如织锦唯有将不同颜色的丝线编织在一起才能呈现美丽图画，社会也唯有当我们接纳与自己不同的人、向他们学习并将他们纳入其中时，才能达到真正辉煌的潜力。因此，与其他篱群的牛头人相比，织锦编织者更可能成为前往其他族裔的使者，或欢迎其他族裔进入他们的家园。反过来，这也使他们更可能成为冒险者：他们努力探索外部世界以及生活于其间的众多族裔。织锦编织者通过识别有毒的关系与世界观、聚拢并组织亲友与盟友等方式在社会中导航，进而创造他们希望见到的社群与世界。他们也更可能加入其他篱群，尤其是解题者。

由于妖魔化“他者”是邪恶的典型工具，织锦编织者远更可能为善，或至少中立，而非邪恶；并且他们略更可能偏向法度而非混沌。然而，罕见的邪恶织锦编织者也可能令人胆寒：他们能够动员多元的联盟投入其邪恶旗帜之下，完成的成就甚至超过多数排外的邪恶霸主单凭自身所能梦想的极限。

阵营与宗教

拒绝巴风特恶魔道路的牛头人很少拥有混沌邪恶阵营，而更倾向于善与中立阵营。有些牛头人偏向混沌，喜欢以个人化、非正统且富适应性的方式应对难题；另一些则偏向法度，更喜欢有纪律、按部就班的方法。

牛头人常崇拜那些与他们一样享受困难谜题与解题的神祇。在阿拉卡尔，库尤尤其受欢迎；赫斯特、德蕾米拉以及许多其他神祇也各自拥有牛头人追随者，还有一些人追随艾德。

牛头人艺术

牛头人的艺术作品、建筑与服饰在设计上往往融入大量转折与回环，有时甚至会直接把迷宫或曲径迷宫纳入图案之中。这种审美选择的起源在于：牛头人会在无意识层面觉得这类图案令人安定，因此他们觉得融入这些图案的环境平和而友善。于是，他们会尽可能将这类图案包含其中。

更妙的是，如果牛头人心不在焉或感到压力，而附近又有带有真实迷宫的艺术作品，他们可以有意识地解迷宫来尝试集中注意或平复心情；以稳定节奏反复完成这类任务，能对他们的精神产生聚焦或安抚的效果。其他族裔的成员通常不会对牛头人艺术中的迷宫产生同样感受，但那些扭转、蜿蜒的线条依然具有某种审美上的愉悦。

名字

牛头人社群遍布世界各地，他们的名字也如其故乡一般多样。大多数牛头人倾向于只使用一个名字，但会在其名字之后加上“之子”“之女”或“之子嗣”，再接父母的名字，以示与父母的联系。以下是来自不同牛头人社群的一些名字。

示例名字

Akabeko, Apis, Asterion, Audumbla, Buchis, Camahueto, Donn, Finnbhennach, Gavaevodata, Gugalanna, Hathor, Hesat, Io Daughter of Melia, Jason, Kamadhenu, Kao, Kusarikku, Kuyūthā, Mithras, Nandi, Nemwer, Pasiphaë.

牛头人传承

在1级时从以下牛头人传承中选择一个。牛头人传承在体型上有所差异：有些牛头人位于中型体型的最上沿，而另一些则进入大型体型。按常规，大型牛头人不会因其为大型而获得任何其他数据调整，例如触及；他们只是大型。拥有多用传承的牛头人一般为中型（与大多数中型牛头人传承一样处于上沿），因为该牛头人的传承复杂性趋向平均值。在GM的裁量下，若多用传承涉及某种也常常极其巨大的生物，例如龙裔后嗣，则可能改为大型牛头人。
