Minotaurs are large muscular humanoids with bovine heads, complete with horns, fur, and hooves. They love mazes, puzzles, and exercising cunning solutions to their problems.

The world sees a picture of minotaurs as cruel and unintelligent hunters painted for them by both the manipulative demon lord Baphomet, lord of wicked minotaurs, and their own prejudices. Baphomet, himself a genius, prefers for others to underestimate his worshipers while at the same time promoting a culture among his followers that spreads this misunderstanding. However, while the stereotypical minotaur found in the biased bestiaries of other humanoid cultures is described as unintelligent, wanton, and evil, those outsiders who seek to actually explore minotaur societies for themselves find that many other minotaurs live their lives far removed from the stereotypes the outsiders expected. Minotaurs existed long before Baphomet co- opted them, and they have countless other creation stories that don't involve Baphomet at all, many of which the jealous demon lord has done his best to quash, preferring everyone, minotaur or not, to believe that he has a monopoly on their faith.

Due to the many myths surrounding them, most minotaurs who want nothing to do with Baphomet tend to live peacefully in insular communities, hunting or farming for their own food and solving their problems on their own. However, no ancestry can remain alone in the face of great danger and supernatural threats, and minotaurs are no different. The growing number of adventures swirling around all over the world have stirred something within minotaurs' spirits. Where others might panic or crack under the weight of disaster after disaster, minotaurs see an ever- branching labyrinth of conundrums and troubles, one that can be solved only with cunning solutions. Where others might step down, minotaurs step up, and thus a growing number of minotaurs have become adventurers and joined the many other humanoid societies that make up the world around them.

Of course, this process hasn't always been easy. They are entering a world with many people used to understanding minotaurs based on the worshipers of Baphomet, and thus likely to engage in numerous misunderstandings. Nonetheless, minotaurs are determined to find their own path through this maze-like and complex challenge as well. Some minotaurs are patient, working to explain the truth gently in a way that others will be willing to accept without bristling and entrenching their opinions. Others feel that those with bigoted beliefs should be the ones who have to make the effort, not minotaurs, and simply live how they choose.

If you want to play a big bovine- headed character with a natural penchant for untangling complex problems and finding simple solutions, you should play a minotaur.

You Might...

  • Love solving mazes and puzzles, especially physical puzzles.
  • Enjoy the thrill of competition.
  • Fight using a clever combination of raw power and cunning strategies.

Others Probably...

  • Underestimate your intelligence based on your animal features or what they know of Baphomet-worshiping minotaurs.
  • Ask you for help reaching things that are high up due to your stature.
  • Worry that your strength might cause you to break delicate objects, like a bull in a porcelain shop.

Physical Description

Minotaurs are incredibly tall and muscular, typically standing around 10 feet tall, though some minotaur heritages are notably smaller, averaging closer to 7 feet tall instead. They don't have quite the reach or musculature of those minotaurs who sacrificed their intellect to Baphomet for greater power, but they more than make up for it in cleverness and adaptability. A minotaur's most salient feature to other humanoids is their bovine head, replete with magnificent horns. Many minotaurs are quite proud of their horns and take to decorations, wearing ornaments on them, painting or inscribing them with art like a tattoo, and countless other ways of showing them off. Somewhat less noticeable than their heads and horns are minotaurs' hooves, which lead to some adjustments in their footwear.

Minotaur Adventurers

Minotaur adventurers enjoy classes that let them traverse the physical or metaphorical mazes they prefer. These could include explorers like rangers, rogues, druids, or barbarians, or more esoteric options like investigators for riddler minotaurs or psychics and thaumaturges for paranormal threaders. Minotaurs prefer backgrounds with similar themes, such as animal whisperer, archaeologist, astrologer, bounty hunter, courier, detective, emissary, gladiator, hermit, hunter, martial disciple, nomad, sailor, scout, teacher, and warrior.

Society

Minotaurs have a wide variety of societies across the world, each with rich histories, cultures, and folklore. One commonality across minotaur societies is that most minotaurs love a good story, tall tale, or mystery, the more tangled with twists and turns, the better.

Hedges

Minotaurs sometimes divide themselves into hedges, groups of minotaurs who have their own approaches to labyrinths, problem-solving, and life. Some minotaur communities and cultures have strong majorities drawn from one or a few hedges, while others have minotaurs from an even mix of all hedges. Hedges aren't exclusive, and thus, minotaurs who are especially excited about solving multiple types of mazes might become a part of two or more hedges.

Horizon Turners

To horizon turners, the entire world, or even the entire multiverse, is one big labyrinth, and the only way to solve the maze and find its true meaning is to explore every corner. The hedge takes its name from the concept that the horizon is like a twist, bend, or intersection in a maze, where you can't quite see what's on the other side. Horizon turners can't wait to see what's around that corner, what's beyond the next horizon. This makes them fairly likely to become adventurers, leaving minotaur communities, exploring the brave new world outside, and meeting the people in it. This often makes horizon turners allies with tapestry weavers, and some minotaurs belong to both of those hedges at once. Horizon turners enjoy writing travelogues about their many journeys and adventures to excite others with the possibilities of the wide world, which leads to another sense of the hedge's name, allowing others to explore new horizons all with the simple turn of a page.

Horizon turners are slightly more likely to be chaotic than lawful, though all alignments are possible depending on what sorts of actions the horizon turner takes among the lands they're exploring. Those who seek to help those in need wherever they go are usually good, while those who pillage and plunder are almost always evil.

Knot Slashers

Knot slashers are named after a legend in which an ancient minotaur was challenged to solve an impossibly difficult knot and chose to do so by slashing the knot with a huge axe. Just like in the legend, knot slashers have their own simple and elegant strategy for unraveling complicated conundrums, bedeviling conspiracies, and all of life's mysteries: they cut through the obfuscating complexity with a swift application of brute force. Just because knot slashers prefer to exercise strength and use simple solutions, it doesn't mean they aren't intelligent or otherwise aren't capable of coming up with a more complicated solution instead. Knot slashers have pondered on the possible options and considered the other hedges, and they decided that they simply prefer the direct approach. The knot slashers hedge includes minotaurs of a wide range of intellect, and even some from backgrounds that might seem surprising at first. For instance, wizards might not seem like knot slashers at first glance, given their love of books and scholarship, but minotaur evokers sometimes gravitate toward being knot slashers when they feel that direct applications of blasty magic is the best way to solve most problems.

Knot slashers are more likely to be chaotic than lawful. This is because the "knot slashing" solution is often an outside-the-box solution that requires the minotaur to look past the assumed context and instructions and come up with an unexpected answer, and lawful characters are more likely to follow along instead. While it's easy to assume knot slashers would be violent and thus more likely to be evil, a direct application of force doesn't have to mean a violent solution, and knot slashers of good and neutral alignments are quite common as well.

Mind Delvers

To mind delvers, the mind is a maze that we've never been able to fully explore or understand, always full of new unsolved mysteries. To mind delvers, a labyrinth isn't really a physical place but more so a mindset of being trapped or wrapped up in a garden of forking paths. For this reason, mind delvers are big on metaphors, and they often use them, along with techniques such as visualization, free association, and meditation, in order to try to explain complex nebulous mental states in a way that's more concrete and easier to comprehend and navigate. Philosophy and deeper understanding of the way the mind is grounded in the greater ontological whole are also important areas for mind delvers to explore.

While the mind delver hedge is associated with mages who can explore the mind, like psychics, joining the hedge doesn't guarantee any connection to the magical arts, and indeed, many members are completely unable to practice magic. Instead, these non-magical mind delvers use mundane techniques of observation, contemplation, and understanding to explore their minds and the ones of those around them purely through mental processes. Regardless of whether or not they employ mystical methodologies, mind delvers don't stop at simply discovering the secrets of the mind. Mind delvers use what they learn to further their other goals, whether that's to provide therapy and counseling and help others in need, to manipulate others to the mind delver's cause, or for any of countless other reasons.

Mind delvers don't tend to any particular alignment, as knowledge of the mind can be just as easily used to heal a damaged psyche as it can be used to twist someone to the mind delver's will. Just as there are an infinite number of possible states of mind, so too do mind delvers express themselves across the entire alignment spectrum.

Minotaur Settlements

Minotaur settlements tend to be struclured in a way that makes them intentionally labyrinthine and complex, with impressive architecture and confusing layouts. This tends to result from a combination of intentional obfuscation and the genuine lack of need for a simple and easy-to-navigate street plan. After all, minotaurs can navigate just about anything with ease. This makes minotaur settlements easier to defend against an invading force while also allowing for strikingly beautiful arrangements of buildings the likes of which most ancestries have never seen. However, it's nearly impossible for other visiting ancestries to find their way around without significant difficulty.

Paranormal Threaders

Paranormal threaders believe that everything that happens in the world is connected, forming a tangled web of magical and mysterious possibilities. Their labyrinths are best represented not by a blueprint representing physical twists and turns but instead by a huge conspiracy board with pictures, clippings of text, and red strings or threads connecting them all. Paranormal threaders can navigate these complex paranormal possibilities with the same natural instinctive cunning that other minotaurs use to move through a physical maze, wandering through connections others wouldn't be able to make in order to finally arrive at the truth.

Due to their overlapping interests, paranormal threaders are somewhat likely to also join the riddler hedge or to have close friends and allies who are riddlers. They'd like to be just as closely associated with the problem solver hedge since paranormal threaders believe that their personal convoluted plots and secrets are the kind of huge societal problems others should share as priorities, but unfortunately, most problem solvers don't tend to agree. Paranormal threaders are so used to being three steps ahead of the direct concrete evidence, with others dismissing their concerns until it's too late, that it's often quite easy for other people to befriend them if they'll just give the paranormal threader a chance, listen to their explanations, and open their mind to the possibility that they might be accurate, despite the lack of evidence.

Paranormal threaders find patterns where other people don't, which can wind up leading them to either a lawful alignment or a chaotic one depending on how they get there. They are slightly more likely to be neutral or on the moral axis than they are to be good or evil.

Problem Solvers

Problem solvers want to find solutions to the biggest problems facing their family, their community, or even the world. They think of themselves as practical realists who look at the big picture and are willing to tackle big problems even after others have failed to do so. In so doing, they often attempt to draw a sharp distinction between themselves and riddlers, who they feel focus on minor trivial mysteries, often for "fun," and paranormal threaders, who they feel have gone too far down a rabbit hole in the area of their own interests and expertise, hyperfocusing on issues that might not be the most important. That's not to say they don't believe that what riddlers or paranormal threaders do is useful, but they do consider it less constructive than their own contributions, and they don't like being lumped in together by people unfamiliar with the differences between the hedges. Problem solvers are among the most likely minotaurs to become adventurers in regions where the big pressing problem is some kind of threat that requires adventure as a solution. This is because they aren't just backroom thinkers who dream up solutions and leave them for others to implement; problem solvers want to be on the front lines, navigating the twin mazes of complex planning and on-the-spot improvisation required for turning an idea into reality. They are determined, sedulous, and brave enough to do so even when their plan requires hardship or danger on their part. Problem solvers are rarely evil, as most evil minotaurs don't really care about solving larger problems and instead focus on themselves; that said, evil problem solvers who will sacrifice or destroy anything to help their own family do exist, and it is terrifying to behold the meticulous and unhesitating practicality of the atrocities they are capable of committing all while feeling themselves totally justified. Depending on whether a problem solver specializes in advanced planning or rolling with the punches, they tend towards either a lawful or chaotic alignment, with an alignment neutral between law and chaos for the typical problem solver who prefers a little bit of both.

Riddlers

Riddlers work to solve puzzles, riddles, mysteries, ciphers, and other similar conundrums. They find larger enigmas and complex puzzle hunts to be their labyrinths, and they navigate their way through using their wits, cunning, and intellect to solve smaller pieces and larger metapuzzles alike.

While most riddlers are focused on solving puzzles, some also enjoy taking the reverse role and inventing puzzles and mysteries for others to solve. Thus, a gathering of minotaurs from the riddler hedge sometimes takes the form of a riddle round table, where one riddler proposes a puzzle, and the first one to solve it proposes the next, continuing onward the whole night long, allowing them to share new puzzles together alongside refreshments and merriment. Other riddlers create nearly impossible puzzles and mysteries with the intent to befuddle others, rather than the intent to be solved.

Riddlers tend to differ from problem solvers in that they are looking to solve especially tricky mysteries, as opposed to finding practical solutions for real-world problems, and they don't much care whether their mystery is hypothetical or actual, trivial or important. That said, some riddlers become detectives or investigators and work to solve real cases, considering it the ultimate test of their abilities to test their wits against a criminal mastermind whose diabolical livelihood is on the line.

Riddlers are significantly more likely to be lawful than chaotic, as a regimented and methodical approach is often helpful in deciphering mysteries, but riddlers who take a wild outside-the-box approach are sometimes chaotic. Riddlers are somewhat more likely to be neutral between good and evil since they are usually more interested in the riddles themselves than in whether solving them would be beneficial or harmful to others, but solving riddles is also rarely especially malign in effect.

Tapestry Weavers

Tapestry weavers consider the complex web of relationships and connections between people to be the most devious labyrinth ever devised. They consider that axiom even more accurate when it comes to living together with various different ancestries, all of whom have shockingly different worldviews. The tapestry in the name of their hedge is a metaphor for the rich tapestry of society. They extend the metaphor to explain that just as tapestries can only have beautiful artwork by weaving together threads of various colors, so too can society only reach its true glorious potential when we accept others who are different, learn from them, and include them. Thus, tapestry weavers are much more likely than minotaurs of other hedges to become emissaries to other ancestries or welcome other ancestries into their homes. This, in turn, makes them more likely to become adventurers as they strive to explore the outside world and the many ancestries who live there. Tapestry weavers navigate society by identifying toxic relationships and worldviews, gathering and organizing kith, kin and allies, and otherwise creating the community and world they wish to see. They're a bit more likely to become a member of other hedges as well, particularly problem solvers.

Since vilifying the other is a typical tool of evil, tapestry weavers are much more likely to be good, or at least neutral, than they are to be evil, and they are slightly more likely to be lawful than chaotic. However, the rare evil tapestry weavers can be terrifying, as they can manage to rally a diverse coalition to their evil banners and accomplish more than most xenophobic evil overlords could dream of doing on their own.

Alignment and Religion

Minotaurs who reject the demonic path of Baphomet rarely have chaotic evil alignments, tending instead more toward good and neutral alignments. Some minotaurs are chaotic, enjoying individual unorthodox and adaptive approaches to difficult problems, while others are lawful, preferring a regimented and methodical approach.

Minotaurs often worship deities who enjoy difficult puzzles and problem-solving as much as they do. On Alacar, Kuu is especially popular, though Herst, Dremira, and many others have their share of minotaur followers as well, and some follow the Eld.

Minotaur Art

Minotaur artwork, architecture, and clothing tend to incorporate many twists and turns in their designs, sometimes including mazes or labyrinths among their patterns outright. This aesthetic choice owes its origin to the fact that minotaurs find such patterns unconsciously soothing, and so they find environments incorporating them to be peaceful and welcoming. Thus, they choose to include such patterns when they can.

Even better, if a minotaur's mind is wandering or they're feeling stressed, and there's art with actual mazes nearby, they can solve the mazes consciously to try to focus or calm down; repeating such tasks at a steady pace can have a focusing or calming effect on their psyche. Members of other ancestries don't usually feel the same way about the mazes in minotaur art, but there's still something aesthetically pleasing about the twisting, sinuous lines.

Names

Minotaur communities are spread far and wide throughout the world, and their names are as varied as their homelands. Most minotaurs tend to use a single name but indicate a connection to their parents by adding after their given name, "son of," "daughter of," or "child of," and then their parents' name. What follows are a few names from various minotaur communities.

Sample Names

Akabeko, Apis, Asterion, Audumbla, Buchis, Camahueto, Donn, Finnbhennach, Gavaevodata, Gugalanna, Hathor, Hesat, Io Daughter of Melia, Jason, Kamadhenu, Kao, Kusarikku, Kuyūthā, Mithras, Nandi, Nemwer, Pasiphaë.

Minotaur Heritages

Choose one of the following minotaur heritages at 1st level. Minotaur heritages vary in size, from the very upper edges of size Medium for some minotaurs, into size Large for others. As normal, large minotaurs don't have any other statistical adjustments for being Large, such as reach; they are simply Large. A minotaur with a versatile heritage is generally Medium (at the upper edge like most Medium minotaur heritages), as the complexity of the minotaur's heritage tends toward the mean. At the GM's discretion, a versatile heritage involving a creature that is also often enormous, such as draconic scion, might lead to a Large minotaur instead.

Nymph Ancestry.name: 宁芙族裔 Nymph Ancestry
Nymph Ancestry.text:
Nymphs are living manifestations of the natural world's beauty. Each nymph's life force is deeply tied to a ward, a place of awe-inspiring natural beauty. Nymph and ward protect each other, growing metaphysically together in a symbiotic relationship. However, this bond restricts a nymph to a small area around its ward.

Rarely, for one of any number of reasons, a nymph's bond with their ward becomes broken, allowing the nymph to travel across the world untethered. Nymphs can normally shift their bond through a gradual process when necessary, but the process of fracturing a nymph's bond is much more permanent. Whether the nymph severed the bond intentionally or was forced to destroy the bond to save themselves or their ward from danger, breaking the bond in such a way alters or shatters part of the nymph's essence that normally connects to the bond, rendering the nymph incapable of forming a new bond in exactly the same way, while simultaneously removing their dependency on a bond to survive. In place of the energy from their bond, they replenish themselves by eating and drinking just like most other fey.

Nymphs without a ward or any physical need to stay in the same location are sometimes known as wanderers due to their tendency to exercise their newfound freedom. Nymph wanderers are especially likely to become adventurers, as their travels carry them into interesting and dangerous situations. Even though wanderers no longer possess a bond to a ward, their instincts as guardians are rarely completely suppressed, causing them to instinctively take action to protect people, creatures, and places that can't defend themselves. Wherever they go, nymph wanderers turn heads, especially in settlements full of ancestries ill- accustomed to looking upon a nymph's supernatural beauty.

Nymphs without a bond might not be restricted to a particular location any longer, but they still possess a strong fondness for the types of natural locations that resemble their old wards. A wanderer's mystical connection to nature as a whole and areas like their ward is still powerful, and they are capable of achieving a state of natural symbiosis with such locations to help sustain themselves.

If you want to play a beautiful fey with a powerful connection to a particular part of nature, you should play a nymph.

You Might...

  • Find yourself most at ease in natural places, especially those that match your old ward.
  • Not realize how much of a stir you make in town due to your uncanny beauty.
  • Have a fondness for beautiful art and music.

Others Probably...

  • Idealize you and hold unrealistic standards for your every action due to your appearance.
  • Treat you favorably at first and seek friendships but react poorly to being ignored or rejected.
  • Have difficulty understanding that you don't look at the world the same way that they do.

Physical Description

Nymphs appear as avatars of nature's most beautiful places. Each nymph has a gorgeous appearance, though no two are alike. While many nymphs are female and nymphs of other genders tend toward an androgynous aesthetic, nymphs of every gender and all kinds of body shapes exist. Like many other types of fey, nymphs normally have long ears and a vaguely humanoid appearance. However, each nymph heritage has major differences in their appearance, corresponding to the natural phenomena present in that heritage's typical ward.

Asteriai, or star nymphs, have skin as dark as the night sky, speckled with silvery-blue stars across their body, with silvery-blue hair and eyes that sparkle with twinkling motes of stardust.

Aurai, or breeze nymphs, have breezy, airy features, windswept hair, and typically pale coloration. Especially powerful aurai have wings growing from their backs, allowing them to soar through the very winds they embody.

Dryads, or tree nymphs, have bark-colored skin with tree-like features such as branches, leaves, and flowers growing from their bodies. For this reason, green is the most common dryad hair color, and their hair often changes colors during autumn and falls out during the winter like leaves. Their eyes often match the color of flowers growing on their body.

Epimelides, nymphs of meadows and orchards, are among the most humanlooking nymphs, appearing almost as shepherds with more typical humanoid skin tones, except where grass, mud, and soil grow from their bodies. Their hair and eye colors are usually typical for humanoid residents of the region where they first established a ward. Otherwise, they often have hair and eyes with grass green or rich soil brown hues.

Hesperides, or sunset nymphs, have hair in one or more of the colors of a sunset, in the yellow, orange, and red range. Even their body's coloration typically swirls with sunset light, especially approaching their hands and feet. Their eyes glow with light of similar colors. A particular hesperid often displays the full range of sunset colors between their hair, eyes, and body.

Lampads, or cavern nymphs, have rich light gray stony skin, a hint of blue, darker gray rock formations, and colorful crystals growing from their body. Their hair is typically white, or if not white then in pastel colors. One of their most distinctive features is their solid white eyes, which typically drip with thick tears of sorrow.

Maenads, nymphs who embody the wild uninhibited nature of wine as well as the poisonous aspects of nature, have a wild feral beauty with sharp teeth and nails long enough to serve as claws. Veins of reddish-purple wine trace across their body, with grapevines wound through their hair, and they're rarely without various containers of alcohol stored on their person.

Naiads, or freshwater nymphs, have skin, bodies, and even eyes seemingly made of water, typically a rich, vibrant blue color, though with variation based on the color of the water from their original ward. Their bodies are often adorned with lily pads, reeds, and other features found in lakes, ponds, and other freshwater bodies. A naiad's hair sometimes has a watery appearance and coloration, but otherwise, it stands out as looking more like a humanoid's hair than the rest of a naiad's body.

Nymph Adventurers

Nymph adventurers are most likely to choose classes that either cast primal magic or become attuned with nature, such as druid, ranger, sorcerer, summoner, or witch, though some heritages have further tendencies, such as hesperides, whose attachment to treasures make them more likely than other nymphs to become thaumaturges. Nymphs often have backgrounds involving beauty or nature, such as animal whisperer, artisan, artist, astrologer, entertainer, herbalist, musical prodigy, plant whisperer, or trailblazer.

Society

Nymphs often live on their own, protecting their ward without needing multiple nymphs in the same location. However, sometimes a nymph queen arises to preside over an especially large area of natural beauty. In such a monarch's expansive domain, other nymphs of the same kind often congregate, each protecting one small part of the whole but occasionally coming together for a convocation or ball at the behest of their queen. Nymph wanderers travel across the land, easily entering and exiting communities full of people bedazzled by the nymph's beauty but never truly accepted as one of the community's own.

Heritages

Each nymph heritage has different ways of relating to the world around them, and these tendencies apply to wanderers just as much as to other nymphs. The following eight heritages are more likely to become wanderers than most.

Asteriai

Asteriai are nymphs of the stars and constellations. Normally, an asteria bonds to a particular constellation of stars as their ward, choosing a location for their home with a clear view of that constellation all throughout the year, or traveling on a migrating path to be sure they can see the constellation each night. Asteriai protect those under the cover of darkness, like the twinkling stars within the blanket of night. Given that their ward is a visual manifestation of a set of stars each bigger than an entire planet and astronomically far away, most asteriai have developed a sense of scale and distance that are very different than other creatures, even other nymphs and fey. The mightiest mountain or most ancient forest are mere specks compared to the vastness of space. While some creatures despair in the face of the vastness of the universe, asteriai smile unblinkingly at that truth; it only motivates them more to do their part. Asteriai love mysteries, and they often attempt to find solutions for hidden or otherwise unsolved problems affecting other creatures around them. This makes them natural foils for mysterious villains and beings that promote unfathomable conspiracies, such as the deities known as Enigmas in the world of Alacar. But it's not only terrestrial mysteries that catch an asteria's attention. With their eyes ever upon the firmament above, asteriai are well-poised to protect their home world from threats arriving from other worlds. Asterias tend to be mysterious in the way they communicate, but in a way that alluringly draws the listener into the asteria's mysteries rather than in a confusing way that offputs a listener and keeps them at a distance. This makes it easier for asteriai to gather allies when needed to protect the night from danger or unravel a particularly tangled conspiracy, but it can occasionally cause other creatures to become obsessed beyond the asteria's original intent.

Asteriai usually become wanderers by choice, when a particular mystery or adversary forces them to break their link to their constellation to follow a lead or to protect someone. However, they're also sometimes forcibly broken from their wards as a result of meddling against powerful and malevolent forces beyond mortal comprehension. These adversaries are capable of the kind of backlash that can sever a nymph's bond to their ward with ease. Combining these two sources make asteriai especially likely to become wanderers overall, since most other nymphs tend to become wanderers in one way or the other. Of the five glades, asteriai wanderers often become wardens, or else slightly unusual cosmopolitans who interact with mortal societies in equal part to learn about mortal cultures and to gather clues about a mystery or adversary. Asteriai are usually too focused on the bigger picture at a cosmic scale to care about something as trivial to them as the bickering between law and chaos, so they're more likely to be neutral between those two extremes. Since the ultimate goal of their investigations and protection are usually to help people, they're often good-aligned but distant, but an almost equal number are neutral between good and evil despite their benevolent goals.

Aurai

Aurai embody the breezes, winds, and the air itself, bonding to beautiful natural locations where the winds blow clearly as wards, such as clifftops on the windward side of mountains. Aurai tend to be flighty, capricious, and tempestuous in their whims and feelings, though their overall personalities and worldviews vary depending on the sort of winds they embody. For instance, aurai manifesting boreal arctic easterlies tend to be colder and harsher in their attitudes, whereas trade wind aurai are warmer, friendly, and slightly less unpredictable than their kin, while jet stream aurai are forceful, exuberant, and excitable. The ability to grow wings and fly that powerful aurai develop often serves as a distraction from any other daily activities and obligations, as an aura with that capability might spend hours flying through the air with the wind in their face and the breeze through their hair.

Aurai are among the most likely nymphs to become wanderers by choice, yearning to follow the winds wherever they blow rather than sticking to just one location. Of the five glades, aurai wanderers often become explorers, as they find travel and exploring new places to be especially exciting. Aurai tend to be chaotic, and even though their alignment varies based on which type of wind they embody, they are almost never lawful.

Dryads

Dryads embody trees and the other flora of forests, jungles, and other such verdant regions, and they bond to specific trees as wards, typically oaks. Dryads tend to have a quiet, harmonious, and peaceful approach to life, preferring non-confrontational methods of resolving conflicts and building consensus with allies rather than simply solving problems on their own. Dryads of other types of trees exist, such as kraneai, or cherry tree dryads, and meliai, or ash tree dryads. Each has their own personality tendencies and preferences, though they all share the same mystical connection to their trees. For instance, kraneiai can be wistfully sentimental, embodying the evanescence of beauty and life. On the other hand, meliai are sometimes overprotective, often "adopting" those who catch their interest and spoiling them with attention and assistance, even if they don't need it

While some dryads choose to be wanderers, they are more likely to become wanderers after something terrible happens to their tree. Dryads wanderers are likely to associate with any of the five glades, though they are somewhat more likely to be naturalists. Like other dryads, they tend toward chaotic and good alignments, though depending on what happened to their tree, they can be of any alignment, even evil, and defy others' expectations for how a dryad usually acts.

Nymph Settlements

Nymphs who retain their connection to their ward are often either solitary or congregate in larger natural regions ruled by a more powerful nymph queen. Due to the fact that becoming a nymph wanderer is either a personal choice or an unintentional occurrence, there aren't many nymph wanderers overall, and thus there are very few settlements consisting mainly or entirely of nymph wanderers. Rumor has it that there is a village hidden in the pristine wilderness somewhere that serves as a sanctuary for any nymph wanderer who seeks respite.

Epimelides

Epimelides embody meadows and orchards and bond to such bucolic and pastoral regions as wards. Among all the nymph heritages, epimelides are one of the most likely to spend significant amounts of time interacting with humanoids, even if they aren't wanderers. That's because other nymphs tend to live in remote regions far from where humanoids ever tread, but epimelides specifically flourish in locations where rural humanoids live in harmony with the world around them. The existence of epimelides serves as an incentive for shepherds and orchard keepers to revere nature, as the presence of an epimelid not only guarantees them a benevolent protector but also serves as an enormous boon due to the increased health of flocks and fruit trees near the epimelid's domain. This close proximity to humanoids means that epimelides understand humanoid culture better than other nymphs. However, they are still ultimately alien embodiments of nature, leading to moments of severe miscommunication over time. Epimelides are naturally nurturing, friendly, and gregarious, as well as excellent hosts to any who come to visit them, though they have their own fey perspective on the nature of friendships and time.

Epimelid wanderers arise relatively equally from intent and misfortune, though once they become wanderers, they often travel through various humanoid settlements, learning more about their mortal neighbors. This makes them among the most likely heritages of nymph to become cosmopolitans, though they also ascribe to the explorer and warden glades with relative frequency as well. Epimelides tend to be good, as well as neutral between law and chaos, though wanderers often balk these trends.

Hesperides

Hesperides embody the light of sunset, typically choosing wards on the coast where they can watch the light of the setting sun sink beneath the horizon. Hesperides are as reliable as the sun's rising and setting and appreciate a daily routine, making their nature somewhat unlike the wilder and more carefree feelings of many other nymph heritages. They often protect not only their ward but also physical treasures, transforming them into golden apples to keep them under close watch. Just because hesperides are more serious and duty-bound than other nymphs, that doesn't mean they can't be as exuberant and joyous as other nymphs. Many hesperides love to dance in the sunlight, twining light into beautiful ribbons of color in a form of natural art. Hesperides often like to team up with other solitary guardians, notably including lawful dragons, particularly when it's necessary to protect an especially powerful treasure. Dragons, for their part, are happy to agree to the arrangement, though they secretly (or sometimes not so secretly) consider the shared treasure to be a part of their hoard.

Hesperid wanderers tend to arise due to the theft or loss of something important the hesperid was sworn to protect. This overall origin can arise with varying specifics, whether the metaphysical backlash from the missing treasure severed the hesperid's bond or they were forced to take matters into their own hands so they could pursue the thief across the world far from their ward. Hesperides' alignment tends toward lawful, unlike many other nymphs, though hesperid wanderers who intentionally abandoned their duties could be of any alignment.

Lampads

Lampads embody the secrets found in the darkest, most hidden places beneath the earth. They typically choose wards such as beautiful subterranean grottos. Lampads live in lands where friendship is hard to come by, as most other inhabitants underground are foes. This, combined with their mercurial emotions, makes them incredibly lonely and eager for companionship. Lampads are often unpredictable and moody, overwhelmed by their powerful emotions and likely to weep uncontrollably for the catharsis it provides them. While lampads aren't saints, they are rarely actively malicious. Instead, their reactions are like mirrors of the way they are treated. When attacked, most lampads use their namesake lights to retaliate and lead their foes astray, whereas they offer guidance to lost innocents who respect nature.

Lampad wanderers are more likely to sever their bonds due to intense loneliness or other powerful emotions. They come to belong to a glade depending on their most fervent wish from the time before they were wanderers. For instance, those lampads who wish to connect with others might become cosmopolitans, whereas those who wish to seek beauty in all its forms might become philokalists. They tend to be wildly chaotic, and due to their overwhelming emotions, they are the only nymph heritage with a stronger chaotic tendency than aurai.

Maenads

Nature is endlessly beautiful, but that doesn't mean it's gentle. Nature is also deadly and merciless, and often the most beautiful and colorful things in nature turn out to be poisonous. Such is the case with maenads, nymphs embodying the reckless abandon of wine-soaked festivities as well as both the benefits and drawbacks of nature's poisons. In civilization, poison tends to have a bad reputation, associated with assassinations and conspiracies. But in nature, poison is just another defense mechanism, often clearly signposted with unusual coloration in order to protect both the poisonous creature and any potential predators. Not only that, some poisons, like alcohol, have effects that society might even consider beneficial. Of course, they usually are treated differently than other poisons in that case. To a maenad, it's all the same. Maenads embody that over-the-top and obvious natural poison, rather than a clandestine vial added to a drink. They tend to be extremely up-front and honest about the danger of imbibing alongside them, but in a way that does nothing to reduce the allure of the bacchanalia. A typical maenad believes that the thrill of festivity more than makes up for any consequences experienced once the party is over, so long as the reveler understood the risks and consented despite them.

Most maenads live lives of reckless abandon, but they're still (wanderers excepted) guardians of nature with responsibilities to their ward. Their physical wards vary depending on their overall personality. They more sociable maenads tend to have vineyards, wineries, or other places alcohol is made as their wards, whereas wilder maenads form a bond with isolated areas rife with natural venoms and poisons of other kinds. These more secluded maenads often make friends with the poisonous and venomous local wildlife, adoring venomous snakes and colorful poisonous frogs as irresistibly cute.

Unfortunately for those who frequent maenad bacchanalias, even the most polite and well-meaning maenads can become dangerous in the throes of their wine-soaked debauchery. Other creatures quickly learn that a maenad's revels aren't for the fragile or faint of heart. Those who can and do brave a maenad's parties earn the maenad's respect and friendly affection. A maenad would go a long way for such a drinking buddy, a connection they consider the deepest in the world, far more intimate than lovers. Even in their most unrestrained moments they would never mean to hurt their drinking buddy... but accidents happen, so they tend to choose sturdy drinking buddies who can survive a wild night with no permanent harm done. Maenads are one of the only heritages of nymph to have a sizable number of wanderers who legitimately have no idea what happened to their bond with their ward. These maenad wanderers typically woke up one day after a particularly wild party with blurry vision, a bit of a headache, a feeling of satisfaction... and without the bond with their ward. Other maenad wanderers sometimes sever the bond intentionally so they can experience excessive revelry across the world, or locate a particular natural poison they've always wanted to find but that's endemic to an isolated region. Of the five glades of nymphs, maenad wanderers most often become cosmopolitans, explorers, or philokalists, but with their own uniquely maenad twist on the three groves. Cosmopolitan maenads engage in society's wildest parties, whether with nobles at their galas, with other tourists at cultural festivals touting local specialties, or with academy students partying on break. Explorer maenads seek out new poisons and festivities across the world. Philokalist maenads continue to seek out beauty, but they want to see wild and passionate art, rather than the sort of tame and elegant work that might be displayed in most galleries. There are very few maenads who aren't chaotic, though anything is possible with wanderers. Maenads are usually neutral between good and evil, and some are goodaligned but maeands are one of the few nymph heritages who have a significant number of evil members among them. Very few of these evil maenads are actually malicious and sadistic, causing harm to others out of a perverse joy or amusement. Instead, they're simply egocentric or negligent, selfishly pursuing their own pleasures without regard for the consequences those have on all the other people around them.

Naiads

Naiads embody bodies of fresh water such as ponds, lakes, and springs, and they typically choose such places as their wards. Even if they aren't wanderers, Naiads have a looser connection to their bonded ward, allowing them to travel to other places as long as they don't get particularly hurt. This flexible bond, flowing like water, means they can travel safely to humanoid settlements, making them, along with epimelides, one of the two heritages most likely to interact with humanoids. Naiads are often bubbly, kind, and excellent at making and keeping friends. Thus, they also use this looser leash to build alliances and friendships and visit all sorts of non-humanoid allies, including naiads and other nymphs.

Naiads usually don't choose to become wanderers but are instead forced into that state due to circumstances outside their control. This distinction is mostly due to the fact that a typical naiad's bond to their ward is already flexible enough to allow them to become an adventurer without severing the bond in the first place. For this reason, naiads occasionally become adventurers even without completely removing their bond, instead adjusting the bond to make it slightly more flexible while leaving it intact. Naiad wanderers tend to be both chaotic and good, though individual naiad wanderers can be of any alignment.

Glades

Nymph wanderers have identified disparate schools of thought they call "glades." Each nymph glade has its own unique perspective on what it means to be a nymph wanderer, whether through sharing parallel origins or coming to understand similar truths about the world around them. The following are descriptions of the five glades that are most common among nymph wanderers.

Cosmopolitans

Cosmopolitans are nymph wanderers who enjoy interacting with humanoid and other nonfey cultures and learning about how they interact. Among all the nymph glades, cosmopolitans are the ones most likely to learn enough about different cultures to truly understand the differences between their worldviews and therefore find a way to fit in seamlessly into settlements of other ancestries. In fact, cosmopolitans can potentially even become a part of large urban communities rather than the smaller rural villages other nymphs usually prefer. Even so, such places are often rife with pollution and disrespect for the natural world, so it would be quite an unusual city, or an unusual cosmopolitan nymph wanderer, who could live in harmony with each other. Cosmopolitans often adventure to engage with and understand new cultures and communities, making them one of the only glades that don't have a notable preference toward wilderness adventures. They tend to reach a more profound understanding with their adventuring companions beyond simply knowing and accepting their differences and truly delving into why each of them thinks the way they do.

Cosmopolitans don't have any particular alignment tendencies as an entire glade, but where they manage to assimilate into multicultural settlements with various other predominant ancestries, they are somewhat more likely to flirt with the alignment tendencies of those other ancestries, in addition to, or contrast to, those possessed by other nymphs of the same heritage.

Nymph Beauty

Nymphs are incarnations of nature's beauty, and so many facets of their appearance are in the eye of the beholder. For this reason, nymphs will often be depicted by a given ancestry in a way that matches that ancestry's traditional definition of beauty, but only because that's what the artist creating the depiction imagines. The only absolute truth of a nymph's beauty is that nearly any onlooker finds the nymph beautiful. Nymphs of all shapes and sizes exist, and no two people see a nymph in quite the same way. The same isn't true of a nymph queen or nymph wanderer's alternate form using Change Shape, however. A subjective appearance would give a nymph's true form away, so the alternate form is typically determined by averaging out the aesthetic preferences of the most populous humanoid ancestry living nearby. In a humanocentric society, that means the nymph often looks like a human, but in a world with more varied ancestries, the possibilities are endless!

Explorers

Explorers are nymph wanderers who genuinely embody the name "wanderer," exuberantly traveling across the world and seeking out the beauty of its hidden places. Wherever there is a secret place of beauty or forgotten vista, explorers seek them out. Some explorers broke their bonds to their ward specifically to sate their wanderlust, chafing at the thought of needing to remain in one place for long. On the flip side, many explorers use their world-traveling voyages to help them soothe their shattered psyche after being forcibly ripped from their beloved ward forever, replacing the hole in their heart with something new. Explorers are among the most common glades to become adventurers, and they often join up with parties who travel from place to place, seeking adventure across the lands, rather than those who operate from a single home base. Aurai are especially likely to become explorers, following the trails traced through the skies by the winds and breezes. Explorers are more likely to be chaotic than lawful and tend to be neutrally aligned between good and evil. The few evil explorers tend to be possessive of the beautiful locations they find, eliminating native creatures or any others who were aware of the secret places they traverse and claiming that they "discovered" the location first.

Naturalists

Naturalists are nymph wanderers who devote their lives to understanding and attuning with nature in all of its countless possibilities and wonders. While nearly all nymphs, wanderers or otherwise, share a deep affinity for nature, naturalists take that bond even further. They might not have a connection to a ward any longer, but their feelings toward nature as a whole are just as strong as those nymphs who do, perhaps even stronger. Most naturalists had no choice when it came to becoming a wanderer, whether their bond was forcibly severed or broken as a last resort to save their ward or life. Those who did choose to dissolve their connection intentionally usually did so not because they no longer desired the bond with their ward. Rather, they made their choice because they felt that what they were doing wasn't simply breaking the connection but expanding that connection to apply across all of nature, rather than simply to one location. Naturalists prefer to join adventuring parties that respect nature and who adventure in the wilderness rather than groups that deal with cities, their pollution, and politics.

Naturalists are the most likely groves to retain the alignment tendencies of a nymph's original heritage. While becoming a wanderer can always be the catalyst for a small or major shift in alignment, naturalists are the least likely to experience such a shift and the most likely to relate to nature and the world around them in similar ways to nymphs of their heritage who are still bonded to their ward.

Philokalists

Philokalists, from the word "kalos" meaning beauty, are a glade of nymph wanderers who are lovers and seekers of beauty everywhere it appears in the world around them. They appreciate beauty in the natural world, though they don't exclusively seek it out in the same way as explorers or naturalists. Instead, they admire aesthetically pleasing works of art, architecture, creatures, people, and everything else in the world around them. While some philokalists' predilections are only skin deep, most philokalists' definition of beauty isn't necessarily limited to traditional standards of beauty defined by humanoids and involving physical features like symmetry. Instead, they are capable of seeing the beauty that comes from within. Philokalists are one of the less likely glades of nymph wanderers to become adventurers, but that's only because so many of the other glades find the urge to adventure so common. Even so, the pursuit of lost artwork and treasure, ancient ruins, and other mysterious sources of beauty motivates philokalists to join adventuring parties and explore the world, doubly so if they find their fellow travelers to be beautiful.

Philokalists generally split into two camps. Most philokalists, who understand the true meaning of beauty as coming from within, tend to be good. The others, who embrace a shallow standard of beauty, are usually neutral between good and evil. Chaotic philokalists tend to be more individualistic regarding their interpretations of beauty, while lawful philokalists apply their standards to others' work as consummate art critics, starting aesthetic movements. Meanwhile, evil philokalists jealously hoard beautiful things for themselves or vainly seek to be the fairest of them all, destroying anything else that challenges their position.

Wardens

Wardens are nymph wanderers who most closely embody the protective nature of nymphs. While wardens might have lost their connection to their ward, they still yearn for something to protect. Among all the glades, even compared to naturalists, wardens rarely voluntarily severed their connection to their ward. If they did, it was only as an absolute last resort, or it was something they did in their past that they have come to deeply regret over time. To a warden, after all, the duty to protect is such a deep part of their identity that they wouldn't abide by any decision to abandon that duty for a flippant reason.

For this reason, some wardens look down upon nymph wanderers who intentionally severed their bond with their ward to pursue their own desires, as they see these actions as selfish signs of severe character flaws. If they are a warden who made such a decision and only came to this realization later in life, then so be it; that means they were being selfish too back then, and they've finally learned what really matters. Those wardens work even harder to try to overcome what they feel to be their flaws and to make up for what they see as their past mistakes. Wardens who are guarding an area or something immobile tend to stay in the same place. This could even potentially be their old ward if they can psychologically bear to stay there after losing their mystical bond. However, many other wardens choose to guard something or someone that could move from place to place, and they live an itinerant lifestyle following whoever or whatever they swore to protect. Some wardens act as mercenaries, protecting something for a set time based on their contract before moving on to the next job, but many prefer to dedicate themselves to one charge. Wardens who become adventurers often either choose quests that are in service of protecting their charge or have chosen a fellow adventurer in their party as their charge.

Wardens' dedication and consistency mean they are significantly more likely to be lawful than chaotic, and they tend to be neutral, or potentially good, more often than evil. Evil wardens might choose charges to guard that initially belonged to another, violently or burglariously obtaining them first in order to keep them safe.

Nature's Vengeance

While evil nymphs are typically still protectors of nature, they act as merciless avengers for the natural world, a cause that can end up uniting them with unusual allies, such as gremlin reckoners.

When the balance of nature is under threat, evil nymphs feel nothing but satisfaction when wiping out the mortals they consider a blight to the pristine tranquility of their sylvan sanctums. As opposed to working one by one through direft confrontation, they prefer using magical schemes such as unleashing a spore that transforms an entire settlement into peaceful non-sapient trees.

Alignment and Religion

Nymph wanderers can have any alignment, and their alignment varies based on their heritage and grove. However, they are much more likely to be good or neutral than evil. Additionally, most heritages, with hesperides as a notable exception, are more likely to be chaotic or neutral than they are to be lawful. Even so, a nymph's understanding of morality can differ from a humanoid perspective. For instance, a good nymph is typically willing to make sacrifices to protect innocents, but they might consider helpless plants and animals and defenseless humans to be equally worthy of that protection. However, that doesn't mean that nymphs don't understand the way that the cycle of nature requires carnivores to eat meat and herbivores to eat plants in order to survive.

Nymphs often worship vitae, incarnations of nature's life force sometimes called "nature spirits," or belong to natural and elemental faiths. In Alacar, this includes the Eld, and some nymphs worship ancient eldamon with an element roughly matching the nymph's heritage (air for aurai, space for asteriai, wood for dryads, earth or wood for epimelides, light for hesperids, earth for lampads, poison for maenads, or water for naiads). Nymphs who worship more traditional deities tend to prefer fey deities or deities of nature, such as Than Dat, the goddess of earth and plants or Chira, the goddess of spring in Alacar, or specific deities matching their heritage, like Thongfa, the goddess of air and storms for aurai.

Names

Nymphs tend to have names that end with either an a or a long ‘e' sound, though many nymphs have names that don't follow that trend. This personal name, however, is usually something more private or intimate to them, and so they don't always share it with new people until they feel closer. Until then, they often rely on descriptive names that include a descriptive word and a term from nature connecting to their heritage, like "Ethereal Zephyr" for an aura or "Green Willow" for a dryad.

Sample Names

Amphitrite, Daphne, Dione, Erythea, Ethereal Zephyr, Green Willow, Minthe, Pherousa, Radiant Twilight, Thenoe, Thetis.

Nymph Heritages

Each nymph heritage has a connection to a different aspect of nature, which grants distinct benefits in addition to the differences in physical appearance and society. Choose one of the following nymph heritages at 1st level.

Asteria

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.p73LPppXBQ4Tw8SY]{Asteria}

You're an asteria, a nymph of stars and constellations, and you can create a guiding star to lead your allies to success. You gain the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.spells-srd.Item.izcxFQFwf3woCnFs]{Guidance} cantrip is a primal innate cantrip. As normal, a cantrip is heightened to half your level, rounded up. As a creature connected to the space element, you gain the teleportation trait.

Aura

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.E9voFXkVTXbbWiGi]{Aura}

You're an aura, a nymph of breezes and winds, and you can control the power of wind to slow your fall and protect yourself from landings. You take no damage from falling, regardless of the distance you fall. You gain the air trait.

Dryad

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.pzds658huL55GjaH]{Dryad}

You're a dryad, a nymph of trees (typically oak trees), and you can magically entangle foes with plants. You gain the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.spells-srd.Item.J6vNvrUT3b1hx2iA]{Entangling Flora} cantrip is a primal innate cantrip. As normal, a cantrip is heightened to half your level, rounded up. You gain the plant trait.

Epimelid

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.IOZoB03kYPJGZHnV]{Epimelid}

You're an epimelid, a nymph of meadows, pastures, and orchards. You can produce a magic fruit imbued with primal magic. Once per day, you can produce a fruit as a single action. If a living creature that can derive sustenance from fruit consumes it as an Interact action within the next hour, they regain 1d8 Hit Points, plus an additional 1d8 Hit Points for every 2 of your levels beyond 1st. The fruit's effect has the healing, necromancy, positive, and primal traits. You gain the earth and plant traits.

Hesperid

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.LhZXryHKnw1TEqLq]{Hesperid}

You're a hesperid, a nymph of sunset light. You can transform a large object into a golden apple for safekeeping, using the @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-actions.Item.JfYyuKoVJLjruKS4]{Create Golden Apple} activity. You gain the light trait.

Lampad

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.BFb6MgT8hC8hhBdA]{Lampad}

You're a lampad, a cavern nymph with mercurial emotions and the ability to create magical lanterns in order to guide the lost through underground passageways. You gain@UUID[Compendium.pf2e.spells-srd.Item.WBmvzNDfpwka3qT4]{Light} as a primal innate cantrip. As normal, a cantrip is heightened to half your level, rounded up. You gain the earth trait.

Maenad

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.Xs78L2trnlRA1MXX]{Maenad}

You're a maenad, a nymph of alcohol and the beauty of nature's pristine poisons and venoms, as well as the flora and fauna that possess them. You gain poison resistance equal to half your level (minimum 1), and each of your successful saving throws against a poison affliction reduces its stage by 2, or by 1 for a virulent poison. Each critical success against an ongoing poison reduces its stage by 3, or by 2 for a virulent poison. You gain the poison trait.

Naiad

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.GbfZjPA39EF0lmMa]{Naiad}

You're a naiad, a nymph of fresh water bodies, such as lakes, springs, and ponds. You gain a swim Speed of 10 feet and the amphibious and water traits. Like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air.
