Sidhe Ancestry.name: 希德族裔 Sidhe Ancestry
Sidhe Ancestry.text:
Sidhe are the "fair folk" that are the origin behind countless fairy tales warning about trespassing, hospitality, and strange rules of courtesy. They are the otherworldly nobility whose beauty and alien morals entrance and terrify mortals and who follow the logic passed down through oral tradition. But to sidhe, they are just people, and mortalkind are equally confusing and perplexing.

Ultimately, the first thing that sets sidhe apart is the fact that they choose to set themselves apart from other ancestries physically and spatially. Their ancestry's name derives from the word for their faerie halls, palaces, and otherworlds, often accessed through hills, mounds, or other similar entryways. In fact, the sidhe penchant for creating otherworldly spaces via subterranean entrances or in places with great natural or magical power has led some scholars to believe that sidhe might have been responsible for creating the first intelligent dungeons.

By creating their own realms and faerie courts, sidhe began the process of weaving their own rules, etiquette, and stories. Sidhe have lifespans long enough to make elves seem like mere children, and over the course of time, these rules and stories became real for sidhe, a distorted reality that they can force others to share under certain conditions. Thus, folklore spread among other ancestries on how to avoid the dangerous actions of malevolent sidhe: don't eat the faerie food, don't offer insult or trespass, and always carry cold iron. Parents whisper these and other tales to their frightened children, hoping that sidhe won't take the children for their own purposes and replace them with something else from their own realms.

Only a small minority of sidhe seek to cause such mischief and mayhem, but they are the sidhe most likely to interact with mortals in the first place. Most other sidhe isolate themselves in their own realms, where they can pursue their passions uninterrupted by any but other sidhe or allied fey. Adventuring sidhe, known as "sidhe heroes" with a bit of playful derision by other sidhe, are the exception among exceptions. They seek the outside world for a variety of reasons, rarely related to playing tricks on mortals or maliciously punishing those who fail to show them the appropriate respect. They might adventure to learn more about the mortal world, to enjoy the thrill of it, to partake in a grand quest for honor, to curate their own narrative and avoid a prediction of calamity, or for countless other reasons still.

If you want to play an enigmatic fey from an aloof, beautiful, and menacing ancestry famed for their connection to folklore and fairy tales, you should play a sidhe.

You Might...

  • Follow your own eclectic set of rules, compulsions, and narratives.
  • Consider yourself superior to mortals due to your faerie heritage.
  • Have difficulty understanding exactly why your mortal companions make certain decisions and distinctions that don't seem obvious to you.

Others Probably...

  • Assume that you are ancient, powerful, and wise, knowing and understanding far more than you actually do.
  • Worry that you might curse them for the pettiest slight or insult, or replace their children with a faerie changeling.
  • Find your worldview and morals difficult to understand.

Physical Description

Sidhe appearance varies somewhat based on their heritage, but most sidhe have features that could be described as reminiscent of elves but more so, albeit without the distinctive elven eyes. Sidhe have long ears, fine features, and tend to be taller and more slender compared to elves, in much the same way that elves appear when compared to other humanoids, notably humans. Sidhe reach adulthood physically slightly more slowly than humans do, but they are generally considered adults by sidhe society based on the needs of their story, which can wildly vary, The sidhe heritages each have their own differences, though they all fit this general description.

Aes sidhe tend to have colorful but light and pastel hair, skin, and eye colors. Their otherworldly features are impossibly fine, soft, and flowing. Their appearance gives an amaranthine impression of ancient and unfading beauty with an air of ethereal fragility.

Baobhan sidhe have impossibly graceful features befitting dancers, with incredibly pale skin and hair and eyes that tend to reds and crimsons matching the color of the blood they drink, and a tendency to prefer gothic clothing and appearances. Their appearance is especially captivating, but part of that is the captivation of prey when facing a predator. These predatory features mean they are the only sidhe who often appear actively threatening or malevolent at a glance, even when they are kind-hearted.

Cait sidhe have features that seem vaguely feline, especially their eyes, which look just like those of a cat. Their hair color matches their fur in their cat form. The stereotypical hair color of most cait sidhe is usually black, though sometimes with a white streak matching the white spot that appears on the chests of many cait sidhes' cat form. That said, they can appear as all different breeds of cat as well. A few cait sidhe sport cat ears or other minor feline features even when they aren't in their cat form, though it isn't common.

Cu sidhe, on the other hand, have slightly canine features, and they tend to have hair colors and styles that match their dog form. The stereotypical hair color and style for most cu sidhe is typically shaggy and dark green, though any other breed of dog is also possible. A small number of cu sidhe display dog ears or other minor canine features even when they aren't in their dog form, though most don't.

Daoine sidhe have bolder, darker, richer hair, eye, and skin colors than aes sidhe. Their features also tend to be stronger and less delicate than aes sidhe, but still give that same feeling of alien beauty. They lend an impression of impossible strength, courage, and invincibility to a daoine sidhe's appearance.

Leanan sidhe tend to look a bit more like mortals than the other types of sidhe, with appearances that could be drawn directly from the impressions of mortal artistic inspiration and the definitions of aesthetic beauty. By smoothing out the surreal perfection of other sidhe, they appear more approachable, and even more bewitching, which allows them to approach and inspire mortals more easily, while ultimately draining their life force if they so choose.

Sidhe Adventurers

Sidhe adventurers often choose classes that grant them arcane or occult spellcasting or give them social tricks, such as bard, rogue, swashbuckler, sorcerer, witch, or wizard. But no class is more common for sidhe than thaumaturge, since sidhe see Fate as a metanarrative and follow inscrutable rules only they understand. Sidhe often have backgrounds allowing them to express their majesty, cunning, or intellect, such as academy dropout, archaeologist, artisan, artist, astrologer, bookkeeper, charlatan, emissary, entertainer, magical merchant, magical misfit, merchant, musical prodigy, noble, occult librarian, scholar, spell seeker, student of magic, teacher, tinker, or toymaker. Sidhes' tendency to be involved in strange and complex narrative situations mean that they are also more likely than most ancestries to have uncommon or rare backgrounds..

Society

Sidhe society in their own faerie realms is a complex web of interconnected interactions, hierarchies, etiquette, hospitality, faerie honor, ritualized expressions, and story-like narratives. In any given situation, a sidhe living among other sidhe knows their place in the pecking order, what words to say, how to dance the dances and play their part. But just as a mortal falling into sidhe society can be overwhelmed in these intricacies, sidhe heroes who head to the mortal world have their own trouble fitting in. Even so, some sidhe heroes enjoy this liberating feeling of escaping from their assigned part, while others feel lost without this guidance.

Heritages

Each of the sidhe heritages has their own traditional societal roles and tendencies, though these are far less likely to be shared by sidhe heroes than they are by other sidhe of that heritage. Even among those living in faerie realms and walking faerie paths, every individual sidhe spins out the tales embodied by their heritage in unique and distinct ways. Sidhe who believe in the Fate of their roles in the story find this thought both comfort and tragedy, favoring one or the other perspective depending on how well their feelings correspond to the path they feel the metanarrative story of Fate has chosen for them.

Aes Sidhe

Aes sidhe consider themselves to be the eldest of the sidhe heritages, the "first" sidhe from times so ancient that they make the turning of the ages seem paltry. Researchers, scholars, and extraordinary mages, aes sidhe claim that they were the ones who built the faerie realms, otherworlds, and extraplanar settlements where daoine sidhe set up their courts. While daoine sidhe stand on the ceremony of nobility more often, it's a mistake to assume that aes sidhe are any humbler or more down to earth. Aes sidhe simply have their own brand of finicky courtesy, narrative, and rules of interaction, a brand that even some of the other sidhe find archaic and abstruse. Theirs is the social dance of respect for one's elders, or for only achieving greatness by standing on the shoulders of giants. To insult an aes sidhe's ingenuity or depth of scholarship is a major faux pas, which makes it difficult to bring up other intellectual points of view in a scholarly discussion, lest you imply the aes sidhe had been so thoughtless so as not to consider it. Thus, formal debates among aes sidhe have so many different rules of engagement that they're almost impossible for a mortal to penetrate. The best strategy tends to be to take the role of a supplicant with an inferior understanding and ask the aes sidhe to explain it, which avoids the ritual engagement as intellectual equals or rivals. For instance, instead of asking if an aes sidhe has taken a certain theorem into account, a mortal could ask the aes sidhe to explain to the mortal how the aes sidhe's proof accounts for that theorem.

Aes sidhe heroes tend to be builders or watchers, as both paradigms suit them, particularly builders. They tend to neutral alignments far more than extreme alignments.

Baobhan Sidhe

Baobhan sidhe, the blood drinkers of the sidhe ancestry, have some of the most interactions with mortals (along with their cousins leanan sidhe), although unfortunately many of those interactions are fatal for the mortal in question. While their name includes a word for woman and comes from the fact that the first generation of baobhan sidhe were all female, baobhan sidhe of all genders are possible. Male baobhan sidhe sometimes call the heritage fola sidhe instead, though that alternate name is fairly obscure among mortal scholars, some of whom don't realize the feminine implications in the name baobhan sidhe in the first place. Baobhan sidhe possess a narrative of a beautiful and mesmerizing predator, like a snake dances to mesmerize its prey before it strikes. Interacting with a baobhan sidhe "properly" to show the appropriate respect tends to be either the most or least difficult among all sidhe. Some baobhan sidhe seek to give their potential prey an out by holding up standards of discourse and politeness to the highest possible level, killing those who make the slightest mistake. If a given baobhan sidhe is hungry, however, and has determined to kill a conversation partner regardless, then it can be easy to talk to them, as baobhan sidhe tend to be more lenient with decorum when they're talking to a meal who doesn't have long left to live.

Baobhan sidhe heroes tend to be among the baobhan sidhe who prefer not to kill mortals to get the blood they require for sustenance. Nonetheless, drinking from animals is still something that runs against a baobhan sidhe's inherent narrative and strikes them as uncultured, jarring, and fundamentally wrong on a level they can't explain. They can do so and survive, but nearly all would sooner die. Instead, these non-evil baobhan sidhe seek to sustainably drink blood from mortal volunteers who have enough blood to give, never taking so much that it risks the mortals' health. Baobhan sidhe heroes tend to be authors or knaves, outcast from baobhan sidhe society due to unusual beliefs on the idea of killing mortals for blood and sport, or sometimes watchers. While baobhan sidhe in general tend heavily to chaotic evil, since most baobhan sidhe heroes are authors or knaves and those two paradigms tend to mismatch their heritage's alignment tendencies, baobhan sidhe heroes usually aren't chaotic evil.

Sidhe Settlements

Sidhe build magnificent magical settlements in pocket dimensions, Otherworlds, and alternate realms deep in the hidden places of the world. While many of the largest sidhe cities were built by aes sidhe millennia ago, modern sidhe create smaller homes even today, sometimes only large enough for a single manse or workshop with expansive grounds. Their settlements in the broader faerie realms tend to be more famous, however, as they are easier to access for other fey, or even enterprising mortals. Wherever sidhe congregate, they are the undisputed rulers of their domain, enforcing their unfathomable rules of etiquette and courtesy upon all those who venture within. Where daoine sidhe set up their courts, settlements often either exist in pairs or otherwise split down the middle by the rulership and influence of the two dichotomies the courts represent.

Cait Sidhe

Cait sidhe, or cat sidhe, are sidhe who also take the form of cats, typically black cats, and who are associated with narratives of bad luck, mischief, and aloofness. They have their own hierarchies distinct from other sidhe, with their own King, Queen, or Monarch of the Cats ruling over cait sidhe in a particular faerie domain. Cait sidhe are among the easiest sidhe for mortals to avoid offending because the fickle feline fey are likely to avoid conversations, simply going about their way for their own mysterious purposes, or occasionally—when one of the notoriously independent cait sidhe serves a more powerful figure—for those whose agendas they serve. Cait sidhe move swiftly, silently, and virtually unnoticed in communities with many cats, especially if they hide their telltale mismatched fur on their chest (typically white). That's not to say it's impossible to offend a cait sidhe by violating its sense of the proper decorum, though. Tales abound of those cursed with bad luck for such an offense, though in truth cait sidhe enjoy such curses so much that it causes others to wonder whether they don't set the bar higher than necessary specifically to find someone who transgresses in order to "punish" them. One of the most common cait sidhe rituals is to curse those who don't leave them a saucer of milk on important holidays (which includes nearly everyone if the locals aren't in the know) while blessing those few who do. Then again, cait sidhe are so curious that mortals have found their curiosity is a good way to distract them away from curses or other activities a mortal would prefer to avoid.

Cait sidhe heroes are able to overcome their independence and work alongside groups to achieve a goal, though they often maintain the bristly personalities for which other cait sidhe are known. Their favorite paradigm by far is watcher, though they are occasionally knaves, banished from the hierarchy of other cait sidhe for eschewing traditional cait sidhe standards or simply for attempting and failing to seize power from a local King or Queen of the Cats. They are more likely to be chaotic than lawful, and they rarely care enough about issues of morality to be good.

Cu Sidhe

Cu sidhe are sidhe who take the form of hounds, typically shaggy green dogs, and they often take the role of heralds, announcers, or messengers for other sidhe due to their incredibly loud barks that can be heard at a great distance. Cu sidhe appreciate their role and their connection to other sidhe heritages, though other sidhe vary in the way they treat their cu sidhe heralds. While some share the cu sidhe's opinions and treat them as trusted friends and allies, others see them as little more than hounds or lapdogs to be used as needed, showing just enough affection to maintain the cu sidhe's undying loyalty. Mortals can usually avoid offending a cu sidhe by recognizing that a cu sidhe's interactions with them tend to be in the form of dire warnings and pronouncements to make way for another sidhe. Those who stand aside and seek safety after hearing a cu sidhe's bark have earned the cu sidhe's respect for their consideration, and cu sidhe rarely pursue them further. Those who don't heed the bark's warning and continue to stand in the way of the cu sidhe or their noble patron quickly earns a cu sidhe's wrath, however, and such folk are rarely long for this world.

Cu sidhe heroes display the same loyalty to their adventuring companions that other sidhe show toward their lord or patron. They come from all paradigms, though they are less likely to be builders than any of the other four paradigms. They are more likely to be lawful than chaotic, and they have no tendencies toward good, evil, or neutrality between the two extremes.

Sidhe or Sith?

In Pathfinder monster lore, several sidhe heritages exist, though not always under the sidhe name. They bear the names leanan sidhe, baobhan sith, and cat sith, with those spellings. The main difference between those spellings is that sidhe is usually used for the Irish spelling (sometimes also spelled sí) and sith is typically used for the Scottish spelling. When consolidating into a single ancestry, I chose to use the Irish spelling, sidhe, because writing "You can now play a sith in 5e and Pathfinder!" is likely to give you a lot of incorrect ideas based on the bad guys in a popular science fantasy franchise. The sidhe ancestry is a mix of the Irish and Scottish legends, the pre-existing monsters, and some new ideas to help make an ancestry known especially for being "Other" in all their usual fairy tale appearances into playable characters, combined with attention to be respectful to all the source material. If you're interested in more information on sidhe from a primary source, the beliefs and traditions of the Fairy Faith are actively practiced today in Ireland, Scotland, and beyond!

Daoine Sidhe

Daoine sidhe are the most stereotypical sidhe in mortals' imaginations, the quintessential otherworldly nobles with dangerous rules of politeness and hospitality within their faerie realms and courts. Arrogant and bold, daoine sidhe play games of politics from within their fey enclaves, jockeying for power, prestige, and position while playing by their courts' byzantine rules of etiquette and protocol. Daoine sidhe appear to others on the surface as beautiful nobles or knights in shining armor, infinitely wise and kind, but in reality, they are unimaginably cruel to those who step out of line. They might be all smiles, but only on the surface, stretching out a hand to shake while hiding a knife behind their back.

Daoine sidhe courts tend to be focused around dichotomies, embodying two opposing concepts into rival courts allowing them to choose their side in the conflict. Tensions run high between opposing courts, and daoine sidhe tend to identify strongly with their court… at least until or unless they come across a twist in their personal narrative where they switch sides, denying everything for which they used to stand and embodying all that they once despised. Daoine sidhe heroes are often either knights errant or knaves, though they're sometimes authors if they seek a way to escape the harsh strictures of daoine sidhe society. They are more likely to be lawful than chaotic, and they have no particular draw toward good or evil.

Leanan Sidhe

Leanan sidhe are muses, lovers, confidantes, patrons, and inspirations to mortal artists. However, an artist with a leanan sidhe for a muse tends to die young, as the inspiration they grant comes at the cost of the mortal's life force, and this is how a leanan sidhe feeds. This makes them similar to their cousins, baobhan sidhe, though they don't physically drink blood from mortals, and unlike baobhan sidhe they do offer something in return. Leanan sidhe tend to be friendly and considerate, albeit regal and haughty, making interactions with leanan sidhe the absolute easiest of all sidhe for mortals to handle. Some mortals find it odd that leanan sidhe would feed on their lives while being so genuinely kind and polite. Is it just a facade or veneer to hide ill intent? But in truth, leanan sidhe are beings of contradictions. They care for others, and do so most deeply for the mortal artists they sponsor, and sometimes even love them. They feel honest tragedy and sorrow when their artists die, and in fact experiencing a deeper grief is considered a mark of pride by leanan sidhe. The catharsis of their terrible grief provides a strange high, leading some leanan sidhe to taste deep grief as a chaser to a cocktail of love and affection. Other leanan sidhe are tired of the cycle of passion and grief and seek to find another way to live. Leanan sidhe legend tells that the first sidhe queens and muses, who share similar abilities, were once leanan sidhe who found ways to escape their fates by changing into something different.

Leanan sidhe heroes come to their status through a variety of paths, and each of them comes with their own paradigms. Those who seek a new path in life are overwhelmingly authors, but more traditional leanan sidhe who become heroes are often watchers or knaves. They tend to chaotic alignments over lawful, and while most leanan sidhe are rarely good, leanan sidhe heroes seeking to change their fate can be of any alignment.

Sidhe, Fate, and Metagaming

Many sidhe believe in Fate as a quasi sentient metanarrative that rules over the universe, and especially over sidhe, enforcing narrative tropes common in stories. While this can be a unique and interesting perspective to take with a character's belief systems and lead to fascinating roleplay, it also has an unparalleled risk that it might lead to the kind of metagaming that would pull some people out of the story. Some metagaming is inevitable, and much is harmless or even beneficial. However, that line varies from group to group, and the fact of the matter is, in our world there really does exist a sentient arbiter of fate plotting out narrative arcs for the sidhe player character: the Game Master. If your group finds the sidhe idea of Fate too distracting, you might need to downplay it, or choose to play a sidhe who doesn't put as much stock in that belief. However, for groups who really enjoy this kind of philosophical and literary challenge, it can add depth and excitement to the way you run the game and tell stories. Plus, it provides a convenient answer to questions of the medium like "Why do we always run into so many random encounters and find so much treasure? At this rate, travelers would die before reaching the next town or become set for life after one trip." The answer is that most other travelers don't have Fate offering them risk and reward hand in hand with a sly smile.

Paradigms

Sidhe heroes themselves have also come up with five of the most common paradigms in their narratives to describe the way they interact with the mortal world, though many more have been chronicled across the ages by the nigh deathless sidhe, who live for over 1,000 years unless slain by violence, illness, or another outside force.

The following five paradigms were determined by sidhe heroes of ages past.

Authors

Authors are sidhe who are convinced that the narrative Fate has spun out for them is wrong or unfair. They want to change their role, and so they've resorted to extreme measures, becoming sidhe heroes in order to seize their own stories and write the next chapters themselves. Most authors' obsession with restoring agency by reclaiming control over their own stories leads them to act in ways that other sidhe find troubling and unnatural; even knaves are far easier to understand. That's because authors are intentionally attempting to subvert the narratives Fate spins out for them by breaking many of the fundamental rules of sidhe interactions, or observing them based on the role they hope to achieve rather than the role that the story had previously assigned them. But despite their fervent wish, this process is more likely to catastrophically blow up in their face than it is to succeed in rewriting their story into something new. There's a reason why sidhe act the way they do, following the paths of the narrative, the ritual expressions, and the complex web of rules. Fate pushes back hardest on those who go against the grain, and would-be authors can find the blowback far harsher than whatever they had experienced in the first place that set them on their rebellious path.

However, that's not to say they are doomed to failure. Every once in a while, Fate smiles upon their struggle, or perhaps, as authors describe it, Fate is always truly on the side of an author willing to stake everything on a desperate bid to seize the narrative. After all, it makes for a more interesting story. Not only that, overcoming great hardship in order to achieve a deep desire serves to give the accomplishment greater narrative weight, making it more likely to be a permanent and fulfilling shift. Those who achieve things too easily can find their victory hollow.

Authors' individualistic desire to buck the trends makes them somewhat more likely to be chaotic, though they also often have alignments significantly different from the norm for their heritage. For example, baobhan sidhe in general are usually chaotic evil, so a baobhan sidhe hero who was an author might be most likely to be chaotic good, but also fairly likely to be any other alignment far from chaotic evil.

Builders

Builders have a simple goal: they want to build something lasting, something permanent, and in so doing forever leave a mark on the world around them. Most typically, they seek to build a new realm for sidhe habitation, whether it's as small as a tiny Otherworld or demiplane for a solitary work space or large enough to hold multiple sidhe settlements in its space. Stereotypical builders who follow the paradigm to the letter are common among aes sidhe heroes, and they make up a high percentage of those willing to challenge the outside world among the notoriously reclusive aes sidhe. Other builders might seek to create other notable achievements, such as a giant monument to proclaim their skill, a powerful barrier to protect what they cherish, or a tenacious gateway to connect two places together. Builders usually become sidhe heroes not out of an ideological or emotional attachment to the hero lifestyle but out of sheer practicality: doing so is by far the best way to be exposed to the new ideas, rare materials, and trying challenges that will all be necessary for them to achieve their true goal.

Builders' desire to leave something lasting is relatable to sidhe of all alignments, depending on the nature of what they wish to build. Lawful good builders might want to make a place that could become a utopian community and lift others up, while chaotic evil builders might instead exploit labor and seize riches to construct a monument to their own might and greatness paid for in blood, sweat, and tears.

Knaves

Knaves are sidhe who became sidhe heroes not by choice but because they were banished from polite sidhe society. What few knaves made the decision themselves were retroactively pronounced knaves by their sidhe associates, which ultimately comes down to a similar narrative paradigm as far as Fate is concerned. The reasons for being abjured forever from the society of fellow sidhe vary wildly from knave to knave. Usually it's something weightier than stealing tarts baked by someone of higher social status, and some knaves have committed some truly heinous acts that would make other sidhe blanche.

However, once again, mortals have difficulty understanding sidhe morality, as the proximal cause for being declared a knave can sometimes be an action, or series of actions, that sound admirable or righteous to mortal minds. For instance, a sidhe whose liege was victorious over a rival in one of the few open wars between sidhe might be ordered to exterminate the noble's relatives and retainers. To mortals, this request would be evil, even to those who argued it was a necessary evil, but to sidhe, refusal to fulfill that order is a major failing, even if it was to save an innocent infant who knew nothing of the original plot and had no reason to seek revenge. After all, how many stories exist of the sole orphaned survivor discovering their true heritage and growing up to finish off their parents' killer? To sidhe, who expect to live for millennia, any such vulnerability in the story that gives Fate an excuse to kill them is one too many, and a significant threat to their life expectancy. Thus, such mercy would be worthy of banishment.

Knaves' actions after being banished vary wildly but they tend to be extreme. Some become the exact opposite of what they once were, embracing their status as knaves, switching from hero to villain or vice versa. Others double down on their original role as if to show Fate that they were the ones who stayed true to their role, or perhaps in an attempt to desperately win back to the approval of other sidhe, in the slim hope of having their exile revoked. Finally, some knaves decide that if their home rejects them, they'll just make their own home instead, either becoming builders as well or starting a more mortal community.

Like authors, knaves tend to have a more chaotic alignment than lawful, but they also often have an alignment that doesn't match perfectly with their heritage's expectations. That said, these tendencies are weaker than they are among authors, as authors necessarily break the mold but knaves sometimes gain their exile from a single lapse in judgment. It's worth noting that sometimes knaves become authors in response to being declared knaves, deciding that if they can't have their old path in life any more, then they will be the ones to decide where their story goes next.

Sidhe Narratives

Thanks to the whims of Fate, sidhe can become involved in various narratives common among mortal cultures. The following are examples of narratives that might influence a sidhe. Consider looking for other narrative tropes by reading literary or mythological analysis or online on sites like tvtropes.org.

  • The only one who can defeat the lord of evil is the hero, who rises from obscurity empowered by three special artifacts.
  • The last heir to a family line thought to be extinct grows up to topple the villainous lord who slew their kin.
  • Despite many unforgivable acts, the villain has a change of heart, but they can only prove their redemption and earn forgiveness by sacrificing themselves heroically.
  • The hero's child, raised far from their side, seeks to challenge their famous parent to a duel anonymously, only to be slain in the battle.

Knights Errant

Knights in shining armor. Noble heroes who would die before dishonoring themselves, who truly embody the meaning of the word "noble." These things rarely exist in mortal life, instead formed by stories that serve as little more than romanticized propaganda, blended together with the accounts of the few real champions among a mainly corrupt lot. To sidhe, however, those romanticized propaganda stories have become a narrative, and that narrative can become their truth, making story into reality.

To be sure, even for sidhe, it's usually something of a facade, a role in the story that is likely to provide them great reward if they simply play the part to the end. When two daoine sidhe courts set themselves up as foils, in part this allows the more "heroic" of the two courts to receive the narrative advantages common in heroic literature, even when neither court operates with anything approaching mortal morality. But sharing mortal morality is not required to worship at the altar of honor and nobility, and those sidhe who truly embody these stories often become sidhe heroes, feeling that questing throughout the world is a far more fitting use of their skills than politicking and backstabbing in faerie courts with other knights and nobles. So rather than pledge their lives to such things, these knights errant wander the lands in search of quests to seek, dangers to overcome, and heroic tales to embody.

Knights errant might be much more freewheeling and whimsical than a knight remaining rigidly in service to a local lord, but that only goes so far; they're still far more likely to be lawful than chaotic. They are also more likely to be good or neutral than evil, due to the stories they embody, though the fact that the "heroic" knights errant can be evil at all, without much difficulty, is a stern reminder to mortals not to let the labels sidhe use distract them from the fact that sidhe operate under completely different standards of morality. To them, one can be an honorable hero while still being evil by mortal reckoning. There are also knights errant that are better considered as villains than heroes, wandering the land and explicitly performing mustache-twirling over the top acts of cliche villainy that would make even mortal villains blanche.

Nearly all of these villainous knights errant are evil. Oddly enough, though, a few villainous knights are neutral, or even good-aligned. Non-evil villainous knights engage in villainy so intentionally convoluted that they know it doesn't stand a chance of succeeding, while serving as an obvious enemy to unite people and help them put aside their differences, reducing strife, building camaraderie, and allowing heroes to be born. Thus, they act as designated villains fulfilling their role out of obligation.

Watchers

Watchers are a paradigm of eccentric sidhe heroes who have one major distinction from all the others. Other sidhe heroes deal with mortals as a necessity of their role living and questing in the outside world among mortals. To many, they might need mortals for certain story roles (for instance as victims or innocents for knights errant to save), but those mortals are means to an end. If there was a way to quest in the mortal world without the whole mortal part, they might not mind it. To watchers, though, that would be missing the point. They became sidhe heroes and went out into the mortal world specifically because they wanted to research and observe mortals in their natural habitat. More fervent watchers believe that sidhe have much to learn from mortals, but even more moderate watchers still find them endlessly fascinating. When other sidhe deride such pursuits, watchers point out that mortals are much more populous than sidhe and love to tell stories, so observing, or even influencing, the way these stories spread and change might allow sidhe to have advanced knowledge of the metanarrative trends Fate might one day enforce upon all sidhe. Other sidhe refuse to acknowledge the accuracy of such claims, certain that the only stories that truly matter are the ones that sidhe formulate and tell among themselves. Whatever the case, watchers delight in observing the actions, decisions, and beliefs of mortals, which are bizarre and alien to them as sidhe are to mortals. Watchers consider themselves above those sidhe who mock them, since watchers serve as an audience to life's narrative, and ultimately, the audience decides whether a performance is successful.

Watchers are more likely to observe and research than to take action, causing them to be slightly more likely to have neutral alignments than alignments in the extremes.

Metanarrative Masterminds

Sidhe often find themselves attached to common narratives that exist in mortal cultures, which means that no matter how much sidhe hole themselves up in their own realms away from mortalkind, there's still an undeniable connection between them. While most sidhe would scoff to hear this spoken aloud, some of the most devious sidhe masterminds not only embrace this truth but actively manipulate it to their own advantage. These schemers have discovered that it's possible for a long- lived sidhe to sow the seeds of new kinds of stories among various mortal cultures in order to reap the benefits later when those stories become ubiquitous enough to influence Fate. For instance, a cunning swordswoman with a sorcerous rival who defends their tower well might take advantage of the metanarrative by spreading tales of the heroic warrior defeating the wicked sorcerer in their tower.

Alignment and Religion

Sidhe can have any alignment, though they are more likely to be neutral or evil than good. This is in large part because of the fact that their own morality is alien to that of humanoids and their gods, and they see the multiverse's manifestations of alignment as simplistic and trite. Why do "good" humanoids slaughter countless animals wholesale, sometimes raising them especially brutally to make the meat taste better, whereas doing so to just a few humanoids makes you "evil?" They find the very distinctions that define good and evil to be inherently biased, and thus meaningless compared to their own narrative definitions of heroes and villains, honor and dishonor. Sidhe readily acknowledge that the multiverse has been arranged according to these alignments; they don't deny that alignments exist. They simply believe that the multiversal arrangement around alignment is due to bias in the multiverse's composition on the part of deities. Their own realms, some sidhe insist, possess no such bias, and thus are superior.

Whatever their alignment, these feelings that the deities themselves were biased in arranging the multiverse around mortal morality causes many sidhe to feel distant to most deities worshiped by mortals. They usually stick to fey divinities, including powerful ancient kings and queens of sidhe legend who have since been deified in sidhe society. Another faith common among sidhe is the belief in a cosmic force that serves as a metanarrative that controls the fate of the world like a story playing out in real time. Crucially, this worship of Fate directly, as a separate entity and metanarrative, is distinct from any of the usual mortal deities of fate, and many such deities take great offense at the very concept.

Names

Sidhe names tend to be lyrical and challenging for those who don't speak Sylvan to pronounce, with novel vowel and consonant combinations. These names they give to others are merely their public names, however. They keep their private names secret to only themselves and others who need to know, since those might give a clue about the sidhe's true name. Their true name, of course, remains a secret they guard with their life, as it allows others to influence them (Pathfinder Secrets of Magic). Sidhe often take epithets, kennings, or titles based on their roles or accomplishments, which they sometimes use as part or all of their public name. Sometimes sidhe have multiple public names, and they might pretend one of them is their private name.

Sample Names

Ailil, Aoife, Auberon, Diana, Knight of Roses, Medb, Morgan, Pied Piper, Scáthach, Tamlin, Uonaidh.

Sidhe Heritages

Each sidhe heritage has unique characteristics, magic, and stories, setting them apart from the others. Choose one of the following sidhe heritages at 1st level.

Aes Sidhe

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.JQFDxsBN7Jcbt1qE]{Aes Sidhe}

You're an aes sidhe, from a line of enigmatic scholars who understand the mysteries of ancient magic used to build the sidhe realms, granting you additional spellcasting. Choose a common cantrip from the arcane or occult spell list, or another cantrip from the list you chose to which you have access. You can cast this cantrip as an innate spell of the chosen tradition at will (heightened to half your level as normal).

Baobhan Sidhe

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.09KkEPhpdBiAxO99]{Baobhan Sidhe}

You're a baobhan sidhe, from a line of captivating performers who drink the blood of bleeding foes. You gain a claw unarmed attack, which deals 1d4 slashing damage and has the agile and finesse traits. Your claw is in the brawling group, but it's razor sharp and prone to causing bleeding, so it uses the critical specialization effect of the knife weapon group instead.

Cait Sidhe

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.tRtfw0StihZXTeqe]{Cait Sidhe}

You're a cait sidhe, from a line of tricksters who can switch to cat form at will. As a single action, which has the concentration, occult, polymorph, and transmutation traits, you can switch back and forth between your humanoid form and your cat form, a specific Tiny cat (typically black) with a spot of wildly different color on your chest (typically white). While in cat form, you can't use weapons, shields, or other held items of any sort, and you are limited in what actions you can take that have the manipulate trait. The only manipulate actions you can take are to Cast a Spell with somatic components or simple Interact actions such as opening an unlocked door. Your cat form can't perform actions that require fingers or significant manual dexterity, including any action that would require a check to accomplish. The GM might determine other manipulate actions are appropriate for your cat form. While in cat form you can speak, and the only unarmed attacks you can make are jaws unarmed attacks that deal 1d4 piercing damage, have the agile and finesse traits, and are in the brawling weapon group. You can remain in cat form indefinitely until you choose to switch back.

Cu Sidhe

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.5LL04KyeSKlD6lWM]{Cu Sidhe}

You're a cu sidhe, from a line of heralds and messengers who can switch to dog form at will. As a single action, which has the concentration, occult, polymorph, and transmutation traits, you can switch back and forth between your humanoid form and your dog form, a specific Small dog (typically shaggy and moss green). While in dog form, you can't use weapons, shields, or other held items of any sort, and you are limited in what actions you can take that have the manipulate trait. The only manipulate actions you can take are to Cast a Spell with somatic components or simple Interact actions such as opening an unlocked door. Your dog form can't perform actions that require fingers or significant manual dexterity, including any action that would require a check to accomplish. The GM might determine other manipulate actions are appropriate for your dog form. While in dog form you can speak, and the only unarmed attacks you can make are jaws unarmed attacks that deal 1d6 piercing damage and are in the brawling weapon group. You can remain in dog form indefinitely until you choose to switch back.

Daoine Sidhe

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.dUiBl3eeFOhISz0s]{Daoine Sidhe}

You're a daoine sidhe, from a line of nobles divided into opposing courts. You gain the trained proficiency rank in Society and the @Compendium[pf2e.feats-srd.JtjnFsOToBLnSRO9]{Courtly Graces} skill feat. If you were already trained in Society, you become trained in a different skill of your choice, and if you already had Courtly Graces, you gain a different 1st-level skill feat of your choice for which you qualify.

Leanan Sidhe

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.ermc38rJtI9iGAu8]{Leanan Sidhe}

You're a leanan sidhe, from a line of muses who inspire mortal artists to greatness but, in so doing, slowly drain their life force. You can choose to grant a willing creature your assistance over the course of an intimate conversation or other interaction taking at least 10 minutes, giving them the benefit of a successful Aid on all their Craft and Performance checks, but also giving them a –2 circumstance penalty to saving throws against effects you create. This lasts until you choose a different creature to assist or decide to revoke your assistance. Over time, leaving the effect on the same creature might inspire them to incredible heights and cut their life short, but this normally takes years, which is longer than the course of a typical campaign. In long-term campaigns with a lot of downtime, the GM determines whether this happens and exactly how long it takes.

Slime Ancestry.name: 黏液怪族裔 Slime Ancestry
Slime Ancestry.text:
黏液怪是具有能够发展出特殊而独特能力的核的软泥怪，这些能力因黏液怪而异。当黏液怪的核赋予它们智慧时，起初这看起来可能不像治疗或火球那样令人印象深刻，但它最终会带来适应性，使它们成为所有黏液怪中最强大的。

黏液怪冒险者出自这些有智慧的黏液怪之中，被赋予思考、学习与成长的力量。尽管大多数黏液怪是自身进化的结果而自发获得智慧，但也有一些是实验或其他有意的定向筛选过程的产物。不久之后，大多数有智慧的黏液怪会意识到自己在智力上与无心智的同类几乎没有共同之处，于是会去寻求其他祖裔成员的陪伴，把他们当作交谈伙伴、盟友与朋友。它们喜欢冒险、谜题以及其他迫使它们动用智力的挑战，这能帮助它们把核的能力进化到新的高度。由于每个有智慧的黏液怪所走的道路都独一无二，它们在脾性与能力上也存在鲜明差异。然而，很少有黏液怪会继续只是躺在地城里，吞噬任何闯入者。相反，这些黏液怪会找到能让它们更上一层楼的目标。

如果你想扮演一团能走出自我道路、并进化出更强能力的可适应软泥怪，你就该选择黏液怪。

你可能会……

  • 挑战自己的极限，看看自己是否会进化与适应。
  • 不管别人做事的方式如何，都要开辟属于自己古怪的未来之路。
  • 寻求各个祖裔的伙伴进行交谈并充实自己。

其他人可能会……

  • 乍一看把你误认为是无心智的软泥怪。
  • 好奇你的生理结构究竟如何运作。
  • 觉得你的黏液怪特殊能力既令人惊讶又令人赞叹。

外形描述

从表面上看，有智慧的黏液怪与无心智的同类十分相似：色彩鲜艳、形状大致如水洼的软泥怪，在中央有一枚显眼的核，使它们与其他软泥怪区分开来。这枚核既是它们最强的优势，也是唯一的弱点：它给予它们凌驾于其他软泥怪之上的力量，但同时也是其身体中可被精明敌人利用的脆弱要害。由于核的能量会渗入黏液怪的流体身躯，黏液怪的颜色有时会透露其核的内在潜能线索。这是双向的——意味着有时当核进化出新力量时，黏液怪的颜色也会随之剧烈改变。

与其他黏液怪不同，有智慧的黏液怪常会改变其无定形的身体外观，让自己呈现出对其他祖裔成员而言不那么异样的模样，通常是模糊的人形。归根结底，这是为了促进交流：人形能让它们通过面部表情与手势来表达情绪，从而使沟通更容易。

黏液怪冒险者

黏液怪冒险者往往偏好那些能让它们将黏液怪能力与兴趣发挥到极致的职业。尽管因黏液怪而异，但常见的偏好包括炼金术士、魔战士、武僧、游荡者与剑客。黏液怪起初往往拥有较为独行的背景，例如隐士、猎人、游牧民或拾荒者，但在成为冒险者之前，它们有时会在其他祖裔之间工作足够久，从而为自己确立另一种背景。这些背景往往与它们的个人兴趣相关，或让它们能更多了解其他祖裔，可能包括动物密语者、理发师、酒保、簿记员、厨师、信使、使者、草药师或学者。

社会

每个有智慧的黏液怪的个体境遇，使它们在通常无心智的物种中成为罕见的例外。黏液怪通过无性繁殖的有丝分裂过程繁衍：形成新的核，然后黏液怪一分为二。黏液怪在适应性上的一个显著优势在于，新形成的核通常不会被预先注定拥有父代的能力，而是包含让新黏液怪获得与父代完全不同能力的潜力。然而，这一点对有智慧的黏液怪而言无疑是不幸的，因为它们的后代往往不会分享它们的智力。

因此，除了一些基于其新心智具体运作方式的显著例外之外，有智慧的黏液怪往往都是独自觉醒自我意识。于是，黏液怪在世界各地几乎没有什么社会也就不足为奇了。世界上仅有的零散黏液怪聚居地分为两类：要么是让四面八方的有智慧黏液怪前来与同类同住的圣地；要么是由那些特殊境遇使许多有智慧黏液怪得以共同出现的黏液怪所组成的聚居地。

群系

有智慧的黏液怪对陪伴、交谈与智力刺激怀有近乎难以餍足的渴望，这促使它们与其他有智慧祖裔的成员互动。在自身经历与收养的家园背景下，它们发展出各自的角色与社交癖好。尽管各不相同，但仍会出现一些相似性，从而形成若干显著的黏液怪互动群系。然而，这些仅是宽泛的模式，并不能涵盖黏液怪体验的全部。有些黏液怪会同时表现出多个群系的特质，或在同一时间呈现群系的混合。

阿帕莱特

有些黏液怪试图伪装混入人形社会，假装自己根本不是黏液怪，这类黏液怪有时被称为“阿帕莱特”，得名于它们的行为与一种名为阿帕莱特的软泥怪相似。尽管大多数有智慧的黏液怪以自身为傲，但也有一些拒绝自己作为黏液怪的本性，或希望摆脱它，于是转而试图把自己伪装成人形。它们的理由与方法一样多种多样，包括对黏液怪本性的厌恶、对人形的嫉妒与崇拜，甚至包括原始阿帕莱特那种对自身软泥怪本性的典型无知。对那些完全将自己认同为被消化人形的融合体而言，最后一种可能性更是大幅增加。阿帕莱特会谨慎练习替代性的色彩变化，以塑造一种并非显而易见只是“黏液怪换了个形状”的人形外貌。它们近乎虔诚地研究人形社会的方式，以便尽可能无缝融入其中。有些阿帕莱特甚至相信，自己对“人性”的追求已超越了人形本身。它们的理由是：它们每一步都必须努力争取，而大多数人形只是天生如此却将其挥霍殆尽，几乎不理解自己的特权与天赋，反而选择以不人道的方式行事。阿帕莱特可以是任何阵营而无明显倾向，尽管那些不只是想生活在人形社会中、而是要消灭并取代某个特定人形来过其人生的阿帕莱特几乎总是邪恶；若这些邪恶的阿帕莱特与变形怪的篡位者相遇，它们会发现彼此有许多共同点——前提是任何一方愿意向对方承认自己是个冒名顶替者。

纽带锻造者

由于有智慧的黏液怪数量稀少，且绝大多数无法将智慧传给后代，许多黏液怪便伸出拟足以示团结，锻造出由自己选择的家庭、友谊与社群。持此理念的黏液怪被称为纽带锻造者，是有智慧黏液怪中最受欢迎的群系之一。它们不只专注于自身的关系与友谊，还会把各类祖裔的人聚拢在一起，甚至包括那些平时不会合作、或可能被其他祖裔成员视为“怪物”的存在。纽带锻造者不只是在比喻意义上搭桥，它们也努力在字面意义上建造聚居地——连桥梁本身也建起来。它们充当把这些社群粘合在一起的黏性胶水，往往凭借其直率的热情之力，但它们也会确保自己所建之物能在其直接影响之外依然长久。

纽带锻造者往往从小处做起，先组建亲密的小团体或同盟（例如冒险队），以共同目标为方向。然而，从那之后，无论是出于意图与规划还是一连串境遇，它们常会把团体发展为更大且更包容的社群，通常以最初的同盟作为更广泛组织的核心。纽带锻造者常把这个核心称为“核”，以此将整个社群比作一具黏液怪身体。纽带锻造者更多时候趋向善而非恶，尽管邪恶的纽带锻造者会建立错综复杂的犯罪集团，或组建强大而多样的毁灭性掠袭大军。它们比起混沌更可能守序，而混沌的纽带锻造者往往把日常琐事与苦役留给其核中的其他成员，自己则专注于结交并激励他人。

吞噬者

吞噬者是那些想要尽情品尝世界所提供的一切的黏液怪：无论是吞食最精致的美食、尽可能获取更多知识、品尝每一种魔法，还是让自己的感官饱览自然世界的所有景致。尽管该群系的黏液怪常自称“吞噬者”，这并不一定意味着它们会吃下遇到的任何东西。吞噬者只会品尝令它们感兴趣之物，然后继续前行。确实，有些邪恶的吞噬者会吞尽一切、使周遭化为废墟，甚至在学会某个知识后毁掉知识源头、在品尝某种魔法后摧毁其根源、或在享受某种感官体验后消灭它。但总体而言，吞噬者满足于体验本身，并不需要将其垄断。吞噬者专注于个人的感受与经历，因此它们更可能混沌而非守序，且通常并不善良，尽管大多在善恶之间保持中立。然而，那些肆意甚至恶意吞噬的吞噬者，则强烈趋向邪恶。

黏液怪聚居地

尽管黏液怪聚居地很少，但现存的往往与纽带锻造者、分形体或继承者有关，而每一种情况都会导向截然不同的聚居地。纽带锻造者建立聚居地时，他们很可能是那里唯一的黏液怪，尽管却是聚居地建立中最重要的人物之一。其中一个聚居地“多尔”，其统治议会包含一名黏液怪、一条永恒龙、一位树精宁芙、一名恶鬼，以及一名小妖精。分形体聚居地的居民全部或几乎全部都是同一只黏液怪的后代，并共享记忆与人格。例如“克莱姆”村完全由名为克莱姆的黏液怪居住。最后，继承者会建立由各式黏液怪组成的聚居地，其中许多可能出自同一只黏液怪的后代，但每一只都是完全不同的个体。其中一个家园“黏乐园”，隐藏在一处富含魔法水晶与矿物的洞窟中，并拥有天然温泉。

分形体

分形体或许是黏液怪中最不寻常的群系——即使在一个对人形来说本就异样的祖裔中也是如此。分形体的核能够将自己的智慧传给任何黏液怪后代。此特征使它们即便在有智慧的黏液怪中也极其稀有，与继承者共享这一点，但其过程与继承者大不相同。分形体的子代出生时便拥有几乎是父代黏液怪复制品的心智，尤其在记忆与人格方面。尽管如此，它们并不完全互为克隆体。父代与子代之间通常仍有一些细微差异，同时子代的实用技能会显著减弱，并带有某种不成熟。例如，若父代黏液怪是强大的战士，子代将拥有几乎相同的记忆、人格与倾向，但会缺少战斗技能，直到接受足够的训练与实战经验。能否继续将智慧传给后代的能力，并不总是从原始分形体传递到第一代子代；但若能传递，那么这一过程就会代代延续。

这使得分形体成为少数能够相对轻易形成黏液怪社群的群系之一，尽管这些社群会让大多数其他祖裔成员感到格外不适。尽管子代黏液怪并非完全是父代的克隆体，并会因各自经历而在一生中进一步分化，但它们仍然极其相似，以至于在他人眼中显得诡异；它们使用共享的习语、几乎相同的语调与手势也会令人毛骨悚然。尽管如此，分形体社群也欢迎愿意学习理解它们、并尊重其独特而令人震撼的一致性文化的外来者。分形体可以是任何阵营，但几乎总与其父代阵营相同，或至少在阵营上与父代相差不超过一步。例外通常来自那些经历了极端创伤或极度福祉，从而永远改变其人生与视角的个体。

继承者

继承者是智慧可遗传的黏液怪，其智慧会传递到后代的核中。每一个新的继承者都拥有智慧，但其心智可塑且如白纸一般，使它们能发展出各自鲜明的人格。继承者传递智慧的能力在某种程度上让人联想到分形体，包括这种能力能否代代延续这一点既零星又稳定。然而，与出生时就拥有大体成形的心智与人格的分形体不同，继承者在繁育后代的运作方式上更类似于大多数其他祖裔，因此其他祖裔成员会觉得它们没那么异样。

继承者几乎同样乐于像分形体那样组成有智慧的黏液怪社群，并在其中彼此视为家人——而这往往在事实层面也确实如此，因为若将谱系追溯得足够久，同一社群中的所有继承者通常都出自同一只具有智慧的黏液怪。其他祖裔认为继承者聚居地比起分形体聚居地更亲近、也更不令人不安，因为多样的观点与意见更像他们自己的聚居地，尤其当继承者提供人形村庄或城镇里通常能找到的货物与服务时。例如，许多黏液怪喜欢按摩、桑拿与温泉，这使其他祖裔成员感到共鸣。另一方面，黏液怪有时也喜欢另一种按摩形式：它们被缓慢压实进一处狭小空间，让身体在周围没有多余空隙的情况下被重塑；这甚至可能作为集体活动，与其他黏液怪一起被压进同一空间。其他祖裔往往至多只能把这种做法视为幽闭恐惧。

继承者没有阵营倾向，可以是任何阵营，就像任何其他祖裔一样；不过，那些努力与其他继承者形成家族社群的个体更常趋向守序。

融合体

尽管大多数黏液怪通过其核的潜能发展出独立的智慧，但也有一些获得了某种间接赋予它们智慧的能力。常被称为“融合体”的黏液怪群系便属其中。融合体并非直接获得智慧，而是获得了吸收能力，使它们能够把进食对象的一些要素纳入自身。最终，这导致黏液怪吞噬了一只具有智慧的生物，并在过程中获得智慧。然而，它们因此吸收了餐食的智慧，这意味着融合体黏液怪要么拥有两份意识与人格——一份属于新生的黏液怪，另一份属于被吸收的受害者；要么只拥有受害者单一的意识。在后一种情况下，被吸收的受害者可能会感到一种异常的意识连续性，几乎就像自己死去后转生为黏液怪。

由于其不寻常的起源，融合体的优先事项与其他有智慧的黏液怪以及大多数其他群系显著不同。然而在某些情况下，尤其当它们只拥有受害者的意识时，它们也可能是阿帕莱特，或很难与阿帕莱特区分。拥有两份意识的融合体有时会发现彼此协作很容易，从而作为一种新存在和谐生活。另一方面，它们也同样可能为主导权甚至生存而挣扎，因为其中一份人格威胁要遮蔽或吞噬另一份。最终，大多数融合体会找到适应处境的方法并尽力为之：要么接纳如今的自己，要么建立停战与协议，将精力用于共同利益而非自我破坏。融合体的阵营会受其体内另一份意识影响，不过它们也可能在与另一个自我相处的经历中学会冷酷或仁慈。

秘谜者

秘谜者是专注于解谜、发掘秘密或揭开黏液怪一族隐秘奥秘的黏液怪。该群系的黏液怪永远好奇，通常对重大或重要之事如此，但有时也会对爱好、琐事与细枝末节如此。所有秘谜者唯一的共同点是：它们对实验与揭示真相的冲动无法餍足。绝大多数秘谜者希望揭露并发现此前无人能发掘的秘密。若它们也专注于学习各类事实并让自己在知识上膨胀，它们也可能同时是吞噬者。另一方面，传统的秘谜者会通过实验、哲学式思维练习、演绎、归纳、溯因，以及愈发复杂的心智体操，来破解最令它们着迷的谜团。

在秘谜者想要彻底解开的秘密之中，没有哪一个能像“黏液怪整体的智慧”那样令如此多的秘谜者着迷：它最初如何、又在何时出现？为何发生率一直在增加？这是黏液怪核力量的趋同进化还是同源进化？这些问题与更多疑问激发着全世界秘谜者的想象。尽管有智慧的黏液怪本就稀少，秘谜者更是罕见，那些有兴趣解开此谜的个体仍会在跨越国度与大陆的范围内形成稀疏的通信网络，彼此分享研究成果，并极偶尔组织研讨会展示发现，他们称之为“宏大秘谜”。这种活动是一个独特的机会，让你能与来自世界各地的秘谜者相会，并看看这些黏液怪社群中的伟大心智最近发现了什么。秘谜者往往更关注自己喜爱的难题而非道德议题，因此可为任何阵营，但整体趋向中立。

核的进化

有智慧的黏液怪与学者都无法确定，究竟哪些标准或刺激最可能促使黏液怪的核进化出新的、不同寻常的能力。诸多理论声称，时间、绝境与生命精华等截然不同的因素都可能发挥作用。有一点似乎很明确：冒险的黏液怪比起过着久坐生活方式的黏液怪，进化速度要快得多。大多数无心智的黏液怪一生至多获得一次进化，但正因如此，有智慧的黏液怪更有可能再次进化，获得新的强大能力。

阵营与宗教

尽管有智慧的黏液怪可以是任何阵营，但它们的阵营会因其觉醒智慧的具体情况而大不相同。即便如此，它们比起极端阵营仍略更可能保持中立，因为对黏液怪而言重要的事物往往与传统的道德与伦理讨论正交。这并不是说它们不理解阵营，也不是说大多数黏液怪不关心善恶或秩序与混沌；只是有足够多的黏液怪把注意力放在其他事情上，使它们略微倾向中立。

有智慧的黏液怪会崇拜各式各样的神祇，常常会投入它们最先接触到的宗教，尽管偏好也取决于其群系。例如，秘谜者崇拜创新与知识的神祇，纽带锻造者崇拜社群的神祇，而吞噬者崇拜食物或饥饿的神祇。在阿拉卡尔，这些包括：纽带锻造者崇拜维拉与塞梅莱；秘谜者崇拜库乌与时间的三姐妹；吞噬者崇拜埃布鲁格西斯与阿蒙。

名字

除分形体与继承者之外的有智慧黏液怪往往都是独自觉醒智慧、并经历独特进化，因此它们的名字通常缺乏规律，彼此之间也毫不相像。它们要么自选名字，要么由他人赋名；名字常取自喜爱的人、物体、概念、自然之力或类似事物。

在分形体之中，名字往往强烈趋向延续性，部分原因在于子代拥有许多曾被父代之名呼唤的记忆。它们有时采用的一种方式是让父子之间的名字产生轻微变体，从而导致整座聚居地的黏液怪彼此极其相似，连名字也惊人地相近，令来访者抓狂。奇怪的是，那些子代坚持与父代拥有完全相同名字的分形体聚居地反而更容易让外人应付。这是因为尽管从技术上讲这是个人人同名的聚居地，但这些分形体总会采用一个昵称或额外名字，让所有人将其作为事实上的名字使用，而这些昵称往往更具辨识度，同时也让其他祖裔联想到“姓氏与名字”的概念。

示例名字

克洛索、法尔法、莉莉、莱姆、火箭、露璃、史莱姆、溶液、穗、暴风。

黏液怪传承

每个有智慧的黏液怪的进化都独一无二，因此有智慧的黏液怪在觉醒时拥有独特能力也不足为奇。

敏捷黏液怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.xb23IB5tBqVNeSDY]{Agile Slime}

尽管黏液怪已经比某些其他软泥怪更快，但你的动作即使与其他黏液怪相比也更迅捷。你的速度为25尺，而非20尺。

两栖黏液怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.weUIYKEwVRMMj1l4]{Amphibious Slime}

你曾生活在一个同时包含水域与陆地部分的环境中，并已习惯在水中移动。你获得10尺游泳速度与两栖特征。与所有具有两栖特征的生物一样，你既能在水中呼吸也能在空气中呼吸。

分形黏液怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.oqVHRZmPpNVMWOKN]{Fractal Slime}

你是另一只能够与后代分享部分记忆与人格的有智慧黏液怪之子，因此你天生带有他者的知识。你在一项自选技能中成为受训（熟练度），该技能应对应你父代尤为擅长的一项技能。5级时，你在所选技能中成为专家。

超小型黏液怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.OuaqeAvuI434IzYv]{Tiny Slime}

你是一只极其微小的黏液怪，为了在获得智慧之前以这种体型生存，你常常不得不悄无声息地潜行以避免被注意。你的体型为超小型，而非小型或中型。此外，你获得隐秘（技能）的受训（熟练度）等级；若你已在隐秘（技能）中受训，则改为获得一项自选技能的受训（熟练度）等级；并获得@Compendium[pf2e.feats-srd.beyw5bdA5hkQbmaG]{Terrain Stalker}技能专长。

暮光黏液怪

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.fmPJypvzfJcNN9if]{Twilight Slime}

你不仅能够看见（不同于大多数软泥怪），而且你发展出的视觉优于其他有智慧的黏液怪，使你能在昏暗光照条件下看得很清楚。你获得昏暗视觉。

Slimes are oozes with a nucleus capable of developing special and unique abilities, which vary from slime to slime. When a slime’s nucleus grants them sapience, at first that might seem like a less impressive power than healing or fireballs, but it ultimately leads to adaptability that makes them the most powerful slimes of all.

Slime adventurers come from among the ranks of these sapient slimes, granted the power to think, learn, and grow. While most slimes come to sapience spontaneously as a result of their own evolution, some are the results of experimentation or other intentional selective processes. Before long, most sapient slimes realize that they have little in common intellectually with their mindless brethren and seek out the companionship of members of other ancestries as conversation partners, allies, and friends. They enjoy adventures, puzzles, and other challenges that force them to exercise their intellect, which helps them to evolve their nucleus’s abilities to new heights. Because each sapient slime’s path is unique, they bear stark differences in temperament and capabilities. However, few continue to simply lie around in a dungeon and devour anything that comes their way. Instead, these slimes find goals that elevate them.

If you want to play an adaptable ooze who finds its own path and evolves to greater capabilities, you should play a slime.

You Might...

  • Challenge yourself to your limits to see if you evolve and adapt.
  • Blaze your own quirky path to the future, regardless of the way others do things.
  • Seek out others of all ancestries to engage in conversation and enrich yourself.

Others Probably...

  • Mistake you for a mindless ooze at first glance.
  • Wonder exactly how your biology works.
  • Find your slime special abilities surprising and impressive.

Physical Description

On a surface level, sapient slimes appear much like their mindless brethren: colorful, roughly puddle-shaped oozes with a distinctive nucleus in the center that sets them apart from other oozes. This nucleus is both their greatest strength and their one weakness, as it offers them the power that elevates them from other oozes, but it’s also a vulnerable point in their body for a savvy foe to exploit. Because a nucleus’s energy seeps out into the slime’s fluid body, a slime’s coloration sometimes gives clues about the inner potential of its nucleus. This goes both ways, meaning that sometimes a slime’s color will change dramatically as its nucleus evolves new powers.

Unlike other slimes, sapient slimes often alter the shape of their amorphous bodies to give themselves an appearance that seems less alien to members of other ancestries, typically a vaguely humanoid shape. Ultimately, this is to facilitate communication: a humanoid shape allows them to do things like express their emotions via facial expressions and gestures, which makes communication easier.

Slime Adventurers

Slime adventurers tend toward classes that allow them to use their slime abilities and interests to their full potential. While this varies from slime to slime, some favorites include alchemists, magi, monks, rogues, and swashbucklers. Slimes tend to have solitary background at first, such as hermit, hunter, nomad, or scavenger, but before becoming adventurers they sometimes work among other ancestries long enough to establish another background for themselves. These tend to be associated with their individual interests or to allow them to learn more about other ancestries, potentially including animal whisperer, barber, barkeep, bookkeeper, cook, courier, emissary, herbalist, or scholar.

Society

The individual circumstances of each sapient slime make each of them a rare exception among a species that is typically mindless. Slimes reproduce asexually by a mitotic process in which a new nucleus forms, and then the slime splits in two. One of slimes' distinct advantages in adaptability is that a newly formed nucleus doesn't usually come preordained with the parent's capabilities but instead contains the potential for the new slime to gain completely different abilities than the parent. However, this fact is certainly unfortunate for sapient slimes, in that their offspring do not often share their intellect.

Thus, with a few notable exceptions based on the specific workings of their new minds, sapient slimes awaken to their consciousness alone. It should come as no surprise, then, that slimes have very few societies throughout the world. What few scattered slime settlements exist come in two categories: either meccas for sapient slimes far and wide to come and live among their fellow slimes, or settlements made up of slimes whose specific circumstances allowed many sapient slimes to arise together.

Clusters

Sapient slimes have an almost insatiable desire for companionship, conversation, and intellectual stimulation, which causes them to engage with members of other sapient ancestries. Within the context of their experiences and adopted homes, they develop their own roles and social quirks. While each is distinct, some similarities arise, leading to several noticeable clusters of slime interactions. These are only broad patterns, however, and they don't speak to the totality of slime experience. Some slimes express the qualities of more than one of these clusters, or a blend of clusters, at the same time.

Apallites

Slimes who seek to disguise their way into humanoid society, pretending not to be slimes at all, are sometimes called apallites, after the way their behavior mirrors those of the oozes known as apallites. While most sapient slimes are proud of who they are, others reject their nature as slimes, or wish to escape it, and they instead seek to pass themselves off as humanoids. Their rationales are as varied as their methodologies, but they include disgust with their slime nature, envy and admiration of humanoids, and even the original apallites’ characteristic ignorance of their ooze nature. This last possibility is made all the more likely for melded who identify entirely as the digested humanoid. Apallites carefully practice alternate colorations in order to create a humanoid form that isn’t obviously just a slime taking a different shape. They study the ways of humanoid society religiously so they can fit in as seamlessly as possible. Some apallites even come to believe that they have perfected the pursuit of humanity beyond even humanoids themselves. Their rationale is that they had to work for it every step of the way, whereas most humanoids are simply born to it and squander it away, barely understanding their privilege and gifts and choosing to act in inhumane ways. Apallites can be any alignment without any particular tendencies, although those who wish not just to live in humanoid society but to eliminate and replace a particular humanoid and live their life are nearly always evil; these nefarious apallites would find much common ground with doppelganger usurpers, if either would ever willingly admit to the other that they were an impostor.

Bondforgers

Due to how few sapient slimes exist and the fact that the vast majority of them can’t pass their sapience on to their offspring, many slimes reach out a pseudopod in solidarity, forging families, friendships, and communities of their own choosing. Slimes of this school of thought are bondforgers, one of the most popular clusters among sapient slimes. Rather than focusing only on their own relationships and friendships, they bring together people of all sorts of ancestries, even those that don’t normally work together or might be considered "monsters" by members of other ancestries. Bondforgers don’t just build bridges metaphorically, they also work to literally build settlements, bridges and all. They serve as the slimy glue holding such communities together, often through the force of their forthright enthusiasm, but they also work to make sure that what they’re building can last beyond their own direct influence.

Bondforgers often start small, forming an intimate group or fellowship, such as an adventuring party, in order to work toward a common purpose. However, from there, either through intent and planning or a series of circumstances, they often grow their group into a larger and more inclusive community, typically with the original fellowship as the core for the wider company. Bondforgers often call this core the "nucleus," metaphorically comparing the entire community to a single slime body. Bondforgers tend to be good much more often than evil, though evil bondforgers create intricate crime syndicates or powerful and diverse marauding armies of destruction. They are more likely to be lawful than chaotic, and chaotic bondforgers often leave the day to day details and drudgeries to other members of their nucleus, focusing instead on befriending and inspiring others.

Devourers

Devourers are slimes who want to partake in everything the world has to offer, whether it’s devouring the finest foods, obtaining as much knowledge as possible, tasting every kind of magic, or feasting their senses on all the vistas of the natural world. While slimes in the cluster often call themselves "devourers," that doesn’t necessarily mean they eat anything and everything they come across. Devourers only partake in that which interests them, and then they move on. It’s certainly true that some evil devourers lay waste to their surroundings consuming all, even going so far as to destroy sources of knowledge once they’ve learned it, magic once they’ve tasted it, or sensory experiences once they’ve enjoyed them. But for the most part, devourers are satisfied with the experience itself and feel no need to monopolize it. Devourers are focused on their personal sensations and experiences, and thus they are more likely to be chaotic than lawful and are generally not good, though most are neutral between good and evil. However, those who devour heedlessly, or even maliciously, strongly trend toward evil.

Slime Settlements

While few slime settlements exist, those that do tend to be associated with bondforgers, fractals, or inheritors, and each of those cases leads to a very different settlement. When a bondforger starts a settlement, they may well be the only slime living there, albeit one of the most important figures in the settlement’s founding. One such settlement, Dole, has a ruling council including a slime, an aeternal dragon, a dryad nymph, an oni, and a sprite. Fractal settlements have inhabitants who are all or nearly all descendants of the same slime, with shared memories and personalities. For instance the village of Clime is entirely inhabited by slimes named Clime. Finally, inheritors build settlements with all sorts of slimes, many of which might be descendents of the same slime, but which are each completely distinct individuals. One such home, Gootopia, is hidden within a cavern rich with magic crystals and minerals and possessed of natural hot springs.

Fractals

Fractals are perhaps the most unusual cluster of slimes, even among an ancestry that is already alien to humanoids. A fractal’s nucleus is capable of passing on their sapience to any of their slime offspring. This trait makes them extremely rare even among sapient slimes, shared with inheritors, but the process is much different than it is for inheritors. A fractal’s offspring is born with a mind that is nearly a duplicate of the parent slime, especially with respect to memories and personality. That doesn’t quite make them clones of each other, however. There are usually some minor differences between the parent and child, along with significantly lessened practical skills and a certain lack of maturity. For instance, if the parent slime was a powerful fighter, the children would have nearly the same memories, personality, and tendencies but lack the fighting skills until they receive an appropriate amount of training and practical experience. The ability to continue passing on sapience to offspring doesn’t always pass from the original fractal to the first generation of offspring, but when it does, the process continues from generation to generation.

This leads to fractals being one of the few clusters capable of forming slime communities with relative ease, though those communities feel especially off-putting to members of most other ancestries. While the child slimes aren’t entirely clones of the parent and diverge even further over the course of their lives due to their differing experiences, they are nonetheless so similar that it can appear eerie to others, and the use of shared idioms with almost identical intonations and gestures can be uncanny. Nonetheless, fractal communities are welcoming to others who are willing to learn to understand them and respect their unique and startlingly uniform culture for what it is. Fractals can have any alignment, but they almost always have the same alignment as their parent, or at least an alignment within one alignment step from their parent’s. The exceptions tend to be those with extremely traumatic or beatific experiences that change their lives and perspectives forever.

Inheritors

Inheritors are slimes whose sapience is heritable, passing on to their offspring’s nuclei in turn. Each new inheritor is sapient, but their mind is plastic and a blank slate, allowing them to develop their own distinctive personalities. Inheritors’ ability to pass their sapience on is somewhat reminiscent of fractals, including the fact that the ability to continue doing so from generation to generation is sporadic and consistent. However, unlike fractals, which are born with mostly-developed minds and personalities, inheritors function in a way that’s more similar to how most other ancestries with respect to producing offspring, and so members of those other ancestries consider them less alien.

Inheritors enjoy forming communities of sapient slimes almost as much as fractals do, and they consider each other family there, a fact which is often factually accurate, as all inheritors in a given community tend to have descended from the same sapient slime if you trace their lineage back far enough. Other ancestries consider inheritor settlements much more approachable and less disconcerting to visit than fractal settlements because the diverse outlooks and opinions remind them more of their own settlements, especially when inheritors provide goods and services that are usually found in humanoid villages or towns. For instance, many slimes enjoy massages, saunas, and hot springs, which members of other ancestries find relatable. On the other hand, however, slimes also sometimes enjoy a different form of massage in which they are slowly compacted into a small space that reshapes their body with no extra space around them, potentially performed as a group activity with other slimes compacted into the same space together. Other ancestries tend to find this practice claustrophobic at best.

Inheritors have no alignment tendencies and can be of any alignment, just like any other ancestry, though those who strive to form family communities with other inheritors tend more often to be lawful.

Melded

While most slimes develop independent sapience via their nucleus’s potential, some instead gained an ability that indirectly granted them sapience. The slime cluster often known as "melded" are among that number. Instead of gaining sapience, melded gained an absorptive capability, which allowed them to incorporate some elements of their meals into themselves. This eventually resulted in the slime devouring a sapient creature and becoming sapient in the process. However, in so doing, they absorbed the sapience of their meal, meaning that the melded slime either possesses two consciousnesses and personalities, one each for the nascent slime and the absorbed victim, or simply a single consciousness for the victim alone. In this latter case, the absorbed victim might feel an unusual continuity of consciousness, almost as if they’ve died and been reincarnated as a slime.

Melded have significantly different priorities than other sapient slimes and most other clusters, owing to their unusual origin. However in some cases, especially when they possess only the victim’s consciousness, they can also be apallites, or else hard to distinguish from apallites. Melded with two consciousnesses might sometimes find it easy to work in harmony with each other in order to live as a new being. On the other hand, they are just as likely to struggle for dominance or even survival, as one personality threatens to eclipse or devour the other. Ultimately most melded find a way to acclimate to their situation and make the best of it, whether it’s by accepting who they are now or establishing a detente and agreement to use their energy for mutual benefit rather than self sabotage. Melded have alignments influenced by the other consciousness within them, though they might learn either ruthlessness or mercy from their experience dealing with their other self.

Mysteria

Mysteria are slimes focused around solving puzzles, unearthing secrets, or uncovering the hidden mysteries of slimekind. Slimes in this cluster are endlessly inquisitive, typically about weighty or significant matters, but also sometimes about hobbies, trivialities, and minutiae. The one thing all mysterias have in common is that they find the urge to experiment and uncover the truth insatiable. The vast majority of mysteria wish to expose and discover secrets no one else has ever managed to unearth before. If they are also focused on learning all sorts of facts and engorging themselves on knowledge, they might be devourers as well. On the other hand, a traditional mysterium engages in experimentation, philosophical thought exercises, deduction, induction, abduction, and ever more complicated mental gymnastics in order to solve the mysteries that fascinate them most.

Of the secrets that mysteria wish to conclusively unravel, none offers as much fascination for as many mysteria as that of the sapience of slimes as a whole. How and when did it first come about? Why has the incidence rate been increasing? Is this a case of analogous or homologous evolution in the power of slime nuclei? All these questions and more stimulate the imaginations of mysteria the world over. While there are few enough sapient slimes to begin with, and even fewer mysteria, those interested in unraveling this particular riddle form a sparse network of correspondence across nations and continents, sharing the results of their research with each other and very occasionally organizing symposia to present their findings, which they call a "Grand Mysterium." Such an event is a unique opportunity to meet with various mysteria from across the world and see what these great minds among the slime community have discovered recently. Mysteria tend to be focused more on their favorite conundrums than on matters of morality, and thus can be of any alignment, while they tend toward neutrality.

Nucleus Evolutions

Sapient slimes and scholars alike aren't sure exactly what criteria or stimuli are most likely to lead to the evolution of a slime nucleus to gain new and unusual abilities. Theories abound claiming that factors as disparate as time, desperation, and life essence play a role. One thing that seems clear is that adventuring slimes are able to evolve at a much faster rate than slimes living sedentary lifestyles. Most mindless slimes gain at most one evolution during their lifetime, but for this reason, sapient slimes are much more likely to evolve again, gaining new and powerful abilities.

Alignment and Religion

While sapient slimes can be of any alignment, their alignment varies greatly depending on the specifics of their awakening to sapience. That said, they are slightly more likely to be neutral than they are to have an extreme alignment, as the things that matter to slimes tend to be orthogonal to traditional discussions of morality and ethics. That’s not to say they don’t understand alignment, or that most slimes aren’t concerned with good and evil, or law and chaos, but simply that enough slimes focus on other matters that they have a mild tendency to be neutral.

Sapient slimes worship all sorts of deities, often taking to whichever religion they learn about first, though preferences also depend on their cluster. For instance, mysteria worship deities of innovation and knowledge, bondforgers worship deities of community, and devourers worship deities of food or hunger. On Alacar, these include Wera and Semelleh for bondforgers, Kuu and the three sisters of time for mysteria, and Ebrugeses and Amon for devourers.

Names

Sapient slimes other than fractals and inheritors awaken to their sapience alone, with a unique evolution, and so their names tend to lack patterns and bear no resemblance to each other. They either choose names for themselves or receive them from others, often named after favorite people, objects, concepts, forces of nature, or the like.

Among fractals, names tend to have strong trends toward continuity, partially because of the fact that children each have numerous memories of being called by their parents' name. One option they sometimes use is to make a slight variation between names from parent to child, leading to entire settlements of slimes who are very similar to each other and have incredibly similar names as well, bedeviling visitors. Oddly, it's those fractal settlements where children insist on possessing the exact same name as their parent that are easier for outsiders to handle. This is because even though it's technically a settlement where everyone has the same name, these fractals always adopt a nickname or additional name that everyone uses for them as a de facto name, and these nicknames tend to be more distinctive, while also reminding other ancestries of the idea of having a family name and personal name.

Sample Names

Clotho, Falfa, Lily, Lime, Rocket, Ruri, Slime, Solution, Sui, Tempest.

Slime Heritages

Each sapient slime’s evolution is unique, and so it’s no surprise that sapient slimes awaken with distinctive abilities.

Agile Slime

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.xb23IB5tBqVNeSDY]{Agile Slime}

While slimes are already faster than some other oozes, your movements are quick even compared to other slimes. Your Speed is 25 feet, instead of 20 feet.

Amphibious Slime

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.weUIYKEwVRMMj1l4]{Amphibious Slime}

You lived in an environment with both aquatic and terrestrial sections, and you've grown accustomed to moving through water. You gain a swim Speed of 10 feet and the amphibious trait. Like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air.

Fractal Slime

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.oqVHRZmPpNVMWOKN]{Fractal Slime}

You are the child of another sapient slime capable of sharing some of their memories and personality with their offspring, and so you were born with another's knowledge. You become trained in a skill of your choice, which should correspond to a skill in which your parent was especially talented. At 5th level, you become an expert in the chosen skill.

Tiny Slime

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.OuaqeAvuI434IzYv]{Tiny Slime}

You are an incredibly tiny slime, and in order to survive at your size before you gained sapience, you often had to sneak around unnoticed. Your size is Tiny instead of Small or Medium. Additionally you gain the trained proficiency rank in the Stealth skill, or in another skill of your choice if you were already trained in Stealth, as well as the @Compendium[pf2e.feats-srd.beyw5bdA5hkQbmaG]{Terrain Stalker} skill feat.

Twilight Slime

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.fmPJypvzfJcNN9if]{Twilight Slime}

Not only are you able to see, unlike most oozes, but the vision you've developed is superior to other sapient slimes, allowing you to see well in dim light conditions. You gain low-light vision.
