Stheno Ancestry.name: 斯忒诺族裔 Stheno Ancestry
Stheno Ancestry.text:
Sthenos are an incredibly young ancestry, just barely over a century old, of medusa kin, snake-haired humanoids with a curious and passionate outlook on life.

Sthenos aren't the only medusa kin in the world. The powerful euryale are snake-tailed medusa kin cursed by an evil deity with great and poisonous power at the expense of their minds being consumed by an ever-increasing corruption of depravity and nightmares. One such euryale, named Stheno, sought to escape this fate. She spurned the worship of the deity empowering other euryale and began to pray to a new goddess. The story of which goddess she chose depends on who you ask and where you travel. In the world of Alacar, her new patron was either Chira, goddess of spring or Lilin goddess of art and change, or perhaps both working in tandem. In other lands and on other worlds, her patron might have been a different deity of love, beauty, or the arts, especially one who believes in redemption and new beginnings. Furious at Stheno's defiance, the euryales' wicked tormentor struck her down in a fit of divine pique, but Stheno's faith was too strong to be destroyed that easily. With the last of her life force and will, she imbued each of the hundred snakes that made up her hair, and they went on to become the first stheno, growing into forms that resembled medusa. At that moment, a new ancestry was born. This isn't just a legend lost to the distant past or the mists of time. There are people alive from long-lived ancestries who were alive before the stheno ancestry was born, and there might even be some of the original hundred stheno alive today.

While those first stheno arose from a single woman, they possessed a startling variety of appearance, physical features, personalities, and more. Perhaps a gift of Chira or Lilin to help them grow, as ancestries with too little genetic diversity might have trouble continuing. Another such gift was the ability to procreate with humans and other similar ancestries, producing a stheno about half the time and a member of the other ancestry the other half. This allowed sthenos to slowly expand their numbers beyond the original hundred and avoid the dangers of a small, insular population. In fact, after they were born, the original stheno split up, with dozens of different ideas for what to do next, and began to slowly spread out and explore the world. Unfortunately, sthenos look so much like medusas that many people are suspicious or outright hostile, expecting them to be malevolent or to possess a petrifying gaze. For those who take the chance to get to know them, however, most sthenos are kind and caring neighbors and exemplary community members. When welcomed in a community, they tend to establish large friend circles and to have big families, bustling and full of warmth.

Stheno tend to be extremely fond of snakes and consider snakes of all kinds, not just the ones that make up their own hair, to be cute. They often keep pet snakes both out of their general love of ophidians and to serve as friends to their hair snakes. Unlike medusas and euryales, whose hair snakes have extremely toxic bites, most sthenos' hair snakes are non-venomous in nature and harmless to anything much larger than an insect. A stheno's hair snakes are no mere appendages; they are actual snakes, rather than snake-shaped tendrils that the stheno controls. As such, they operate of their own accord, though they try to be helpful in their own way. Even so, they share the stheno's body and are affected by the stheno's changing moods. They become nervous and twitchy when the stheno is frightened, hiss threateningly when the stheno is enraged, cuddle when the stheno is in love, and so on.

If you want to play a snake-haired medusa-kin character whose entire ancestry is so new that they're still figuring out their culture and place in the world, you should play a stheno.

You Might...

  • Form friendships easily with those who accept you and show you kindness.
  • Love snakes and consider them exceptionally cute.
  • Have a passion for some kind of art form, even if others might not consider it "art."

Others Probably...

  • Think you are a medusa the first time they see you and fear or mistrust you.
  • Consider you a valued and loyal friend and companion.
  • Wonder what it feels like to have snakes for hair.

Physical Description

Sthenos look mostly like humans with snakes for hair. However, there's a great deal of physical diversity in their other features, including skin tone, snake coloration, eye color, body shape, and all sorts of other such factors. Their height, weight, and aging process are all similar to humans as well. The one exception is that they lack hair, instead possessing snakes on their heads instead, as well as soft dark scale patterns over their eyes that resemble eyebrows enough to be mistaken as such. Sthenos who descend from a human (or other human- like ancestry) and a stheno are fully sthenos, but they possess some physical characteristics of their other parent. Some sthenos have additional unusual features that are far outside the norm for others of their ancestry. For example, sthenos with the euryale atavism heritage have snake tails, like euryales do.

Stheno Adventurers

Stheno adventurers come from every possible class, but they especially prefer classes that allow them to use their passion for art and creation, such as bards, inventors, psychics, and thaumaturges. Flower bearers and nightmare dreamers are often clerics or champions (especially redeemers for flower bearers). Meanwhile, gorgoneions sometimes become oracles or rogues, following in the footsteps of medusas, and ophidiacs are often druids or rangers. Sthenos live in all sorts of societies and come from every possible background. However, their absolute favorites include acolyte, animal whisperer artisan, artist, astrologer, barkeep, cook, entertainer, fortune teller, musical prodigy, raised by belief, tinker, and toymaker.

Society

As sthenos have traveled throughout the land, they have established tight-knit but open and welcoming societies, social groups, and families wherever they go. Since their ancestry began with one hundred individuals only a little over a century ago, there are few majority-stheno settlements yet and no real stheno homeland. Those stheno settlements that exist are small. Instead, they usually live in larger settlements, either cosmopolitan or with the majority of some other ancestry. They might gather together in a few neighborhoods or spread throughout the settlement. Either way, they benefit from choosing homes with other stheno, or from having a history of recognizing and welcoming sthenos, so that they are less likely to be mistaken for medusas.

Sthenos have rapidly developed multiple styles of stheno fine arts and culture over the past century and change. While you might expect a new ancestry, where many older members knew one or more of the first generation, to have a more consistent culture, the reverse is true. When the ancestry was suddenly born fully formed, each group that set out into the world began to develop a distinct culture, and thanks to their love of art, they created a variety of new art forms as well. These inevitably borrowed, synthesized, and blended with the local cultures around them, leading to markedly different results wherever they traveled. Locals tend to find the mix of familiar and new refreshing and exciting, and where sthenos are welcome, their neighbors celebrate stheno cultural innovation and arts

Outlooks

Unsurprisingly, stheno have branched out into a variety of different outlooks on life and the future, some of which tend to vary by region, with sthenos who live in a particular area sharing similar outlooks, and some of which are sprinkled across sthenos everywhere with little tendency to cluster together. Some sthenos have multiple outlooks at once, even those that are seemingly incompatible, having found a unique way to look at the world that combines them. Others change their outlook as they grow, age, and experience major turning points.

Aesthetes

To aesthetes, art is life, and life is art. Sthenos with this outlook are creative, inspired, and extremely artistic, often creating entirely new art movements that fuse newly-developed stheno ideas with the motifs common to the cultures in their area. Aesthetes work in all forms of artistic media, including the visual arts, music, literature, theater, and more. One notable area that can sometimes be contentious for them is hyper- realistic stone sculpture. Some aesthetes seek to avoid emphasizing artwork in that medium due to the strong associations with medusa petrification, which can still be an issue for sthenos in societies that know about medusas but not sthenos. Other aesthetes, however, feel like such a decision is artistic cowardice, which cedes a portion of art to ignorance.

Instead, they seek to reclaim those sorts of sculptures from that association, and they create artwork designed specifically to challenge those stereotypical assumptions and inspire questions and a deeper understanding in those who choose to fully engage in the art. While many aesthetes set up a studio in a particular location in order to focus on their work and produce masterpieces for art shows, galleries, and exhibitions, others seek to go on grand adventures and travel the lands in search of artwork and artistic designs near and far. This allows adventuring aesthetes to learn from broader influences and evolve their artwork further.

Aesthetes tend to be chaotic more than lawful, and they don't have any particular tendencies toward good or evil.

Stheno Settlements

From their beginning only a short century ago, sthenos have journeyed to distant lands and joined in with societies of all sorts. This has also had a major influence on their burgeoning culture and the shape of their settlements. Thus, stheno settlements reflect a blend of the prevailing societal norms in the area and a uniquely stheno perspective. For instance, in one such settlement, winding buildings and streets reminiscent of a slithering snake at first seem unfathomable and inconvenient for carriages and passersby, but then at a closer look, this unusual architecture allows superior sun exposure to the homes of many residents living on that thoroughfare.

Flower Bearers

Flower bearers are sthenos who have devoted themselves especially deeply to the deity who helped Stheno escape the nightmare curse. In the world of Alacar, this is either Chira, the goddess of spring and love, Lilin, the goddess of art and change, or both. This makes them more likely to be aesthetes alongside their outlook as flower bearers, especially if they favor Lilin's teachings.

In addition to their religious faith, flower bearers are so named due to their belief in the inherent value and potential for redemption in all living creatures, even those with a curse or other metaphysical facet that pushes them towards evil. The flowers are metaphorical, representing the offer of forgiveness rather than recrimination, judgment, or vengeance for their past deeds.

Flower bearers are always willing to welcome anyone into their ranks, even non-sthenos or formerly unrepentant evildoers like nightmare dreamers. That doesn't mean they are naive or foolish about it, however. They're able to offer a chance for redemption to those who earnestly seek it without recrimination, even if they have trouble avoiding a return to their old ways. At the same time, they're still wary of those who would attempt to deceive them and take advantage of their good natures to make it easier to continue committing evil deeds. They don't see a contradiction in these two things, and they recognize that sometimes the only way to tell the difference between a truly repentant soul who is slipping and a mastermind using a false redemption as a cover is to trust but verify. Flower bearers tend to be neutral good most of the time, and adventuring flower bearers are often redeemers. Those who worship Lilin are sometimes chaotic good instead.

Gorgoneions

Most sthenos spend significant effort to disambiguate themselves from medusas, given that ignorant townsfolk mistaking them for medusas can lead to fear, mistrust, and worry that they might turn others to stone at a glance. Gorgoneions, however, see medusas as glorious and wondrous paragons, and they seek to emulate their kin rather than repudiate their connection. When they learn magic, they prefer petrification magic. If they're an artist, rather than shy away from lifelike stone sculptures or attempt to reclaim the medium like some aesthetes do, gorgoneions eagerly embrace the associations with medusas, sculpting exceptionally lifelike figures with faces contorted in fear or anguish, as if they had fallen victim to a medusa's gaze.

Needless to say, many gorgoneions seek out and locate medusas and offer to serve them. Many a medusa oracle has gorgoneion servants attending to their whims, making life much more comfortable for them even if they live in a remote location. Meanwhile, medusa crime lords and guildmasters count gorgoneions as peerlessly loyal adjutants who tend to be immune to temptation to betray their trust; this is incredibly useful and can be a literal lifesaver in occupations where intrigue and backstabbing are common. For these reasons, medusas tend to treasure gorgoneion servants and treat them exceptionally well. After all, just because a medusa is evil, it doesn't mean they treat their underlings poorly, especially if it's in their own self-interest. Sadly, though, a few medusas instead take the opportunity to sorely mistreat their gorgoneion minions, knowing that their loyalty and respect for medusas runs deep enough that the abuse won't shake it.

Just as medusas tend towards lawful alignments, particularly lawful evil, so too do gorgoneions, though gorgoneions of all alignments exist.

Nightmare Dreamers

While nearly all sthenos celebrate the heroism and faith of the original Stheno and her act of defiance against the nightmarish curse of the euryale, there are inevitably a few people in any community who look upon even the most vile chapters of the past with rose-tinted glasses. In just this way, some sthenos forget the loss of agency, descent into depravity, and inevitable fall into the nightmares inherent in the curse, even though Stheno died to set them free. Their perspective is skewed, and they can only dream jealously of the immense power Stheno gave up, power that, by their reckoning, could have been theirs if only she hadn't foolishly turned her back on it. Or worse still, they welcome all those insidious side effects as not drawbacks but features of the nightmare curse. These are nightmare dreamers, and they are frighteningly easy to recruit to religions of evil deities, shadowy organizations, wicked armies, or any other group that offers them the power they so zealously crave. Many nightmare dreamers hate that the common name for their ancestry is stheno, after the woman who they feel made such a terrible decision. Some nightmare dreamers have devised their own name for the ancestry, the two most popular of which are euryalite and medusakin; euryalite is most popular overall, but medusakin is a favorite of nightmare dreamers who are also gorgoneions. Chances are if you see a stheno who tries to hide their distaste for the ancestry name under a polite mask, they might be a nightmare dreamer.

Fortunately, nightmare dreamers are extremely rare. In fact, even people who know of stheno at all have rarely heard of this outlook. As such, other sthenos are almost never held under suspicion of being nightmare dreamers; if they're going to run into trouble, it'll nearly always be from communities that have no idea what they are and think they're medusas instead. Unfortunately, nightmare dreamers find it easy to take advantage of that benefit of the doubt, allowing them to victimize communities used to friendly and neighborly sthenos with ease while claiming that those suspicious of their true nature were prejudiced against all sthenos for their resemblance to medusas. For all these reasons, many other sthenos despise nightmare dreamers, though flower bearers still seek to empathize with, counsel, and redeem them rather than focusing on punishing them.

Nightmare dreamers are almost always evil, and they are usually chaotic evil.

Ophidiacs

Nearly all sthenos have some affinity for snakes. After all, they live with a number of snakes as their hair, who are part of their own bodies and selves. So the average affinity for snakes among sthenos is already fairly high. And then you have ophidiacs. Ophidiacs find snakes adorable, and they aren't shy about telling you. They commission art (or create it themselves) of their favorite kinds of snakes and show the art off to everyone around them, stheno or not, asking questions like "Isn't he so adorable?" or "Look at her little snoot!" That's when they aren't bringing their snakes around in a carrier and taking them out, offering to let others hold them too. Humans often compare ophidiacs to the most avid cat and dog lovers they know.

Since most snakes are harmless, curious, and easygoing reptiles, these behaviors are most likely to cause issues when an ophidiac runs into another person who dislikes snakes or is afraid of them, rather than due to any mischief on the snakes' part. On the other hand, some ophidiacs get a bit out of hand with their snake collections (or "families," as some of them call their snakes), including rare and sometimes dangerously poisonous snakes that don't like to be handled by anyone other than the ophidiac. It's worth noting that most ophidiacs work hard to train the ability to speak with snakes that many stheno possess, so even then, if the ophidiac is paying attention, they can usually avoid a lethal mistake.

Ophidiacs are easily excited by the opportunity to meet rare snakes, especially those that have been previously undiscovered, or that have magical powers, and merchants quickly realize that ophidiac adventurers are excellent targets for snake-themed gear and magic items, or companion items focused around snake animal companions. On the other hand, ophidiacs share the creativity of other sthenos, and many have designed accessories for their snakes, including one ophidiac who claims to have created the world's first comfortable snake barding for snake animal companions.

Much like other pet owners and animal lovers, ophidiacs can be of any alignment. Even good-aligned ophidiacs can sometimes find it difficult not to talk people's ears off about snakes, however. Others find that the best strategy is to let them talk for a bit and then either find a way to change the topic or politely excuse themselves from the conversation. Those who have spoken at length with ophidiacs recommend to engage them on the details only if you want to get sucked into a discussion for hours, though if you choose to do so, you might havee made a friend for life.

Progenitor Disciples

To sthenos, the term "progenitor" refers to the original hundred sthenos born from the original Stheno's hair when she was struck down. Since this happened just over a century ago and most sthenos age similarly to humans, most sthenos have assumed that all but a few of the progenitors have died of old age by now. There are rumors, however, that the progenitors are longer- lived than other sthenos, or that many of them have found ways to extend their lifespans. These rumors claim that most of these surviving progenitors have gone into hiding or otherwise escaped the public eye.

Regardless of whether these claims are true or false, the fact is that a significant number of sthenos hold outlooks greatly shaped by one or more of the progenitors. Whether they learned directly from a progenitor, simply seek to emulate that progenitor's teachings or way of life, or even secretly still serve the aims of a still-living progenitor, progenitor disciples' lives are deeply influenced by one or more of the first sthenos. As recently as fifty years ago, the vast majority of progenitors were openly active and interacting with stheno communities across the lands, and so older shtenos are more likely to be progenitor disciples, though sthenos of all ages hold this outlook. Even young adults far removed from a progenitor might be influenced by the exciting tales of famous progenitors they heard as a child, listening in wide-eyed wonder.

Progenitor disciples' goals, actions, and inspirations tend to follow from whichever progenitor they emulate or serve, and so too do their alignments. Thus, their alignments follow the exact same ranges as other sthenos, same as the progenitors' did.

Two Skies

Many stheno feel at least some level of belonging to the two skies outlook, named by a famous stheno poet who tried to help others understand her life by comparing it to living under two skies at once. Two skies stheno have a stheno parent and a parent of another ancestry, often a human. If they have siblings, most likely some of the siblings are also stheno and some are of the other ancestry as well. Because of the rate at which sthenos start families with members of other ancestries, most sthenos outside of the few stheno-majority settlements have at least one close relative who shares this family situations, though such sthenos are not a monolith and only some subscribe to the two skies outlook.

The quintessential feeling for a two skies stheno is of belonging to both ancestries and neither. Even though they appear like other sthenos, they often have features and cultural identities that match their parent and siblings from the other ancestry as well. These make them stand out from other sthenos, while their snake hair will always make them stand out from members of the other ancestry. On the one hand, two skies sthenos can feel loved and blessed to have special traditions from both sides of their family that help them live a richer life with a broader perspective. But on the other hand, it can be harder for them to find a space to define who they are. When another stheno is organizing an event to show off stheno art in particular, would they want the two skies stheno to submit their artwork? In almost all cases, the answer turns out to be yes, but there can be a level of self-doubt involved that make identity and belonging more difficult.

Fortunately, since their ancestry is so new, many sthenos are in the same situation, and they work to support each other. The poet who created the term two skies, in particular, seeks to help raise awareness of these feelings among stheno communities. Often those who don't have a two skies outlook (even including some sthenos who have a parent from another ancestry who simply don't share the outlook) don't realize how two skies sthenos feel without learning about it from someone else. Once they do learn, however, sthenos of nearly all outlooks work hard to support two skies sthenos and make it crystal clear to them that they are always welcome and included.

Aitheria's Notes: Two Skies Sthenos

In a world like ours where significant travel between nations and even continents has been common for centuries and ancestries of all sorts work together to build communities, many of us can commiserate with the feelings of two skies sthenos. Even though I'm human, not a two skies stheno, I know I've had similar experiences in my life, whether it be from fitting in with both of my parents' cultures or from the way my beliefs and my magic combine to offer me something unique, though ultimately not fitting into anyone else's mental buckets for what they expect from a spellcaster. Some out there might not understand from your own lives, but I ask that you give some consideration for the unique life experience of each individual two skies stheno. Don't approach them with assumptions or try to speak for them. Instead, be there to listen and support them, and let them choose what to talk about and when.

For instance, speaking from my own experience, I sometimes have to push myself to join discussions or events my cousins on my mother's side are organizing specifically for people with ties to my mother's homeland, even though most of me recognizes that I would be welcome. Some two skies sthenos might have similar feelings. But that doesn't mean I should assume that's the case. As in all things, just treat people like people… as complicated as the word "people" is for us to define.

Alignment and Religion

Sthenos vary widely in alignment depending on their outlook and the other cultures around them, with very few tendencies toward any particular alignment as a whole. They have an ever-so-slight leaning towards good and away from evil thanks to the influence of flower bearers being more pronounced than nightmare dreamers or gorgoneions, but ultimately, you can't predict a stheno's alignment without getting to know them first and understanding their passions and worldview.

Sthenos tend to worship deities of beauty, love, art, and redemption, as well as ophidian or snake deities. The one exception are nightmare dreamers, who seek out evil deities for more power. On Alacar, Lilin the Mother and Chira the Flower Child are the two most common deities for most sthenos, while nightmare dreamers usually worship Enigmas, rather than any of the deities of the Balance. Names Many stheno names tend to have a similar etymology and style to "Stheno," "Medusa," and "Euryale," often ending in vowels. A significant number have also named their children after one of the progenitors, or Stheno herself, though in Stheno's case, typically via variants of the name. The progenitors, for their part, chose their own names, leading some of them to have names that diverged wildly from the others, a trend that has passed on due to the naming of children after progenitors.

Sample Names

Asthena, Eleusis, Eudora, Eurynome, Kybele, Ophion, Pythia, Sthenobia, Sthenetos.

Unusual Parents

Many of the assumptions of how a stheno looks rely on the idea that their non-stheno ancestors are some kind of roughly humanoid-shaped ancestry, but those aren't necessarily the only possible parents for a stheno. An ancestry's compatibility with sthenos is up to the GM to decide as the final arbiter. If you'd like your stheno character to have an especially unusual ancestry as a parent or ancestor, work together with your GM to determine what you look like. In some cases, you might decide to use a versatile heritage in order to add mechanical aspects of the other ancestry more directly to your character.

Stheno Heritages

Sthenos were only born recently as an ancestry, and their heritages reflect their descendance from one hundred unique individuals who had distinctive traits and features. While there aren't one hundred different heritages and many of the original hundred sthenos shared the same heritage, it still means you have a 1 in 20 chance or better of being related to other sthenos with your heritage if you go back a few generations. Choose one of the following stheno heritages at 1st level.

Euryale Atavism

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.iTnV67uWwG6ctDwv]{Euryale Atavism}

While most sthenos look more like a medusa, with humanoid bodies and snake hair, you are one of the few whose body is a throwback to euryales instead, with a snake tail for a body. This might be because you are descended from a progenitor with such a form and all your relatives have snake tails as well, or it might be an unexpected anomaly that makes you different. Your tail makes you especially stable. You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to your Fortitude or Reflex DC against attempts to @Compendium[pf2e.actionspf2e.Shove]{Shove} or @Compendium[pf2e.actionspf2e.Trip]{Trip} you. This bonus also applies to saving throws against spells or effects that attempt to knock you @Compendium[pf2e.conditionitems.Prone]{Prone}. In addition, if any effect would force you to move 10 feet or more, you are moved only half the distance.

Faithspark

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.9qYei8zKPql6ddZB]{Faithspark}

You’ve always had a stronger connection to the lingering divine energy of Stheno’s faith. Choose a common cantrip from the divine list or another divine cantrip to which you have access. You can cast that cantrip as a divine innate spell. As normal, cantrips are automatically heightened to half your level, rounded up.

Fanghair

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.Bce7vr1KqZKHK1XL]{Fanghair}

While most sthenos’ snake hair are made up of harmless snake species, yours have large fangs, like a viper. You gain a snake fangs unarmed attack in the brawling weapon group that deals [[/r 1d4[piercing]]] and has the agile and finesse traits. Unlike most unarmed attacks in the brawling weapon group, your snake fangs have a different critical specialization effect: the target takes [[/r 1d6[persistent,poison]]]. You gain an item bonus to this poison damage equal to the weapon’s item bonus to attack rolls. Although your snakes help with the snake fangs attack, you’re still the one making the attack, and it applies all your special abilities, modifiers, bonuses, and penalties, including your multiple attack penalty among many others.

Moonsight

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.Dz79gcam6jyj6U1B]{Moonsight}

You and the snakes that make up your hair have always had better vision in the light of the stars and moon. You gain low-light vision, allowing you to see in dim light as though it were bright light, and thus ignore the @Compendium[pf2e.conditionitems.Concealed]{Concealed} condition due to dim light. Your hair snakes also gain low-light vision, granting them the same benefits for the snake search ability.

Museborn

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.Pw7N7L2kwxZ2vgUW]{Museborn}

The inspiration for art flows through your body with a verve carried down through both mind and life force, perhaps inspired by the deific ears that heard Stheno’s prayers. You gain the trained proficiency rank in Crafting, or in another skill of your choice if you are already trained in Crafting. You gain the @Compendium[pf2e.feats-srd.QLeMH5mQgh28sa5o]{Specialty Crafting} skill feat twice, once in artistry, and once in another specialty of your choice.

Venomproof

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.zsoWbSMYxfrHWycu]{Venomproof}

You and the snakes that make up your hair have developed a natural resistance to poison and are highly resistant to venom, especially snake venom. You gain resistance to poison damage equal to half your level (minimum 1), and each of your successful saving throws against a poison affliction reduces its stage by 2, or by 1 for a virulent poison. Each critical success against an ongoing poison reduces its stage by 3, or by 2 for a virulent poison. You gain a +1 circumstance bonus on saving throws against snake venom.
