Faerie scions are creatures touched by the sidhe, whether they're children of mortals and sidhe, those who have been spirited away, or those incorporated into their magical narrative by the strands of Fate. Ensnared in the enigmatic fey logic of sidhe otherworlds, faerie scions play a special role: they have the power to bridge the gap between mortalkind and the fey realms beyond. If you want to play a character who dives deep into the world of the sidhe and other fey while retaining some semblance of your mortal sensibilities, you should play a faerie scion.

You Might...

  • Blur the line between the mortal world and the fairy logic of fey realms.
  • Seem just a little out of place in both the mortal world and the faerie courts.
  • Possess increased magical abilities, leadership skills, or narrative-bending powers.

Others Probably...

  • Assume you have strange and mysterious powers.
  • Rely on you to understand the alien nature of sidhe and other fey.
  • Sometimes mistake you for a half-elf or a different type of fey.

Physical Description

Faerie scions look mostly similar to other members of their ancestry, but they have one or more features revealing the sidhe side of their nature. These might be long and pointy ears, colorful hair or eyes, or a variety of other traits that vary from lineage to lineage. Almost all faerie scions, at least, have long ears and a tall, slender physique.

Blood scions, those connected to the narrative and Fate of baobhan sidhe, usually have pale skin and crimson hair and eyes, and they sometimes share a baobhan sidhe's graceful appearance and the captivating allure of a predator.

Cat scions, those connected to the narrative and Fate of cait sidhe, tend to have slightly feline eyes and black hair, or hair that matches the color of a cat breed.

Dog scions, those connected to the narrative and Fate of cu sidhe, often have hair colors and styles reminiscent of a specific dog breed, either mundane or magical, with the most common being shaggy and dark green like a typical cu sidhe.

First scions, those connected to the narrative and Fate of aes sidhe, are most likely to have a light pastel color to their hair, skin, and eyes that is unusual for other members of their ancestry. Their features might be refined like aes sidhe, and a few of them give off the seemingly ancient aura of infinity reminiscent of aes sidhe.

Muse scions, those connected to the narrative and Fate of leanan sidhe, often have an air of aesthetic beauty without the otherworldly perfection seen in some other lineages of faerie scion. Some possess the bewitching approachability of a leanan sidhe, allowing them to lure in others with a comfortable feeling.

Noble scions, those connected to the narrative and Fate of daoine sidhe, are most likely to have a dark, rich, and unusual color to their hair, skin, and eyes. Their features might be strong, like daoine sidhe, and a few of them give off the air of seemingly impossible

Society

The name "faerie scion" gives off the impression of a child of the fey, and that's because the stories of families and bloodlines excite the imaginations of mortals. Even though the true mortal descendants of sidhe, known as heirs, are less common than invited faerie scions, they've become the iconic faerie scions. In the world of the sidhe, dictated by the narrative of Fate, being iconic has power, and thus there's something special about the role of heirs. They tend to become part of deep webs of intrigue, stunning twists, and tales with unusual outcomes. Heirs come about when sidhe consort with mortals, often either during a brief sojourn to the mortal world or a mortal's fleeting visit to the sidhe's home realm. When the sidhe parent is a sidhe hero living in the mortal world or the mortal parent is an invited faerie scion, they might have a stabler family. Even then, Fate has a way of complicating things.

Due to a combination between a penchant for mischief and plots and a culture of fostering among sidhe, it's not uncommon for an heir's sidhe parent to be different than the sidhe they expected. In this case, it's possible that the faerie scion's parent is inevitably revealed at the most dramatic moment possible, potentially after a major conflict with the sidhe revealed to be their parent. Unfortunately, one of the stories Fate loves to spin is a battle (potentially to the death) between parent and child who haven't recognized each other until it's too late. In rare cases, an heir might even be the child of two sidhe, fostered in the mortal realm in secret by using magic to make them into a faerie scion and exchanging them for a child of two mortals (who is in turn typically raised in the fey otherworlds as an invited). In this case, Fate always reveals their nature as a full sidhe at the most dramatic moment possible. A perfect example is that the faerie scion might have appeared to die, only to have their mortal form cast off as they used a sidhe ability like Not Today or Plot Armor to survive, becoming a sidhe in full in exchange for the part of their nature tethered to mortality. It's also not unusual, thanks to Fate's meddling, for the artificial heir raised in the mortal world to meet the invited with whom they were swapped as an ally, lover, or mortal foe, without realizing the truth until the fated reveal.

Invited are mortals brought into the faerie world through contact with sidhe, typically intentionally on the sidhe's part, hence the name "invited." Sidhe popularized the use of invited to describe these faerie scions, while some mortals believe that it's a euphemism for a process that's not always as benign as an invitation. These mortals preferred to call an invited "kidnapped" or "spirited away." Invited are the most common faerie scions, though of course they're still quite rare among the whole population of mortals. Many cautionary tales exist that center around invited as the heroes or victims of the story. Those who consume faerie food and wine, participate in faerie dances, or otherwise immerse themselves in the story of a sidhe otherworld may transform into a faerie scion, only to discover that by the time they return home, a century has elapsed and everything they once knew has vanished. Meanwhile, some sidhe foster a mortal child in exchange for teaching their own child more of the mortal realm with a mortal foster family. These cultural exchanges might be voluntary on the part of the mortals, or they might be nothing more than abductions by another name. Unlike many other versatile heritages, invited faerie scions often experience the invitation later in life. Perhaps Fate feels that those with the most tangled connections to others and families of their own will experience the greatest reversals if those connections are snipped by the shears of destiny. If a character is invited during play, they can potentially retrain their heritage to that of a faerie scion, abandoning a piece of their mortality in exchange for the faerie essence of the sidhe.

Rarest among all faerie scions are fated, those who became faerie scions due not to their parentage or sidhe involvement, but entirely through an act of Fate. This might be due to intersecting storylines, where the mortal became entangled in the weft and weave of the narratives of one or more sidhe. In this case, the mortal's own meddling or bad luck cascades into a deeper and inescapable connection due to Fate's intervention. On the other hand, sometimes it comes completely out of nowhere from the mortal's perspective, and becoming a fated faerie scion is their first exposure to the world of sidhe. Sometimes, Fate reveals another connection to the fey realms later on, only after this fated became a faerie scion, while other times they truly had no connection at all. In those cases, Fate determined that their engagement as a faerie scion might add something truly interesting to the narrative, and acted to increase the drama. Much like invited, this can happen later in life, and might lead to a character retraining their heritage (and potentially a lineage feat as well) as they lose a piece of their mortality.

Faerie Scion Settlements

Faerie scions live among either mortals or sidhe, sometimes moving between both worlds and serving as an ambassador. When one rarely establishes a settlement, it's inevitably incredibly eventful in the narrative, as Fate delights in the novelty. For this reason, faerie scion settlements are rare but consequential. Sometimes they become the nucleus of a brand new mortal kingdom.

Alignment and Religion

Being born as or becoming a faerie scion doesn't guarantee that the scion's own morality resembles the alien ethics and logic of the courts of sidhe. Still, faerie scions tend to find sidhe logic oddly compelling, or at least more comprehensible than other mortals do. Individual faerie scions also have alignment tendencies toward the sidhe that match their lineage, though only a faint one. For invited and especially for fated, that's more often because the mortal matched the outlook of that particular type of sidhe and thus became a faerie scion, rather than becoming a faerie scion changing the mortal's alignment to become closer to the sidhe.

While not all faerie scions worship Fate, nearly all of them at least acknowledge Fate's outsized influence on their lives. This is especially true for fated. Sometimes faerie scions find it easier to worship mortal deities with a philosophy compatible with the sidhe concept of Fate, such as deities of fate or fortune, or deities of stories and narratives. On Alacar, this includes the Three Stories pantheon, which includes Jata the deity of heroes, Sewa the deity of tricksters, or Sumaworo the deity of villains.

Names

Faerie scions typically have names similar to other mortals of their ancestry, at least at first. Usually, the only exceptions are those heirs or fostered invited, raised, and named by a sidhe parent. However, famous invited or fated sometimes take on a title for their place in Fate's plans and sidhe society, and they encourage sidhe to use it instead of their mortal name. There are a variety of reasons for this practice. For one, it's a common trope that allowing a sidhe to learn your actual name gives them power over you, akin to using a sidhe's true name. When the story resonates enough, it can start to become real to sidhe. While that worry is a more distant one, it's a fact that sidhe are more likely to respect a faerie scion introduced as "The Winnower of Tales" than a scion introduced as "John." In this case, the titled name grants the faerie scion a status among sidhe that separates them from mortals and elevates their presumed importance.

Sample Names

Connla, Emrys, Oisin, The Fated Heir, The Green Knight, The Once and Future King, The Winnower of Tales.

Faerie Scion Adventurers

Faerie scions often have little choice except to become an adventurer, if they weren't one already. The classes they choose tend to match their lineage, though bard and thaumaturge are common among all faerie scions. Their backgrounds also tend to be connected to how they became a faerie scion, such as animal whisperer for a cat scion or dog scion, artist or musical prodigy for a muse scion, or noble or royalty for a noble scion.

Faerie Scion (Rare Heritage)

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.W60Qbyn46qTQfu63]{Faerie Scion}

Whether you're descended from sidhe, invited by sidhe into their courts and revels, or chosen by fate, part of you is sidhe and will always yearn for the faerie realm. You gain the faerie scion trait in addition to the traits from your ancestry. You also gain low-light vision, or your choice of the @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-feats.Item.JNS0QeQzX8fDiYxw]{Midnight Sight} sidhe ancestry feat or the Sidhe Stride faerie scion ancestry feat if you already have low-light vision. You can choose from faerie scion feats and feats from your ancestry whenever you gain an ancestry feat. Select a sidhe lineage from the list of heritages for the sidhe ancestry, in order to determine your physical appearance. This doesn't allow you to take feats that require a specific sidhe heritage as a prerequisite, unless you first take the appropriate lineage feat.

Ancestry Feats

The following ancestry feats are available to faerie scions. Faerie scions have only a few ancestry feats uniquely available only to them, but they unlock the potential to the vast majority of sidhe ancestry feats, listed below in Additional Feats. Additional feats with a heritage prerequisite require a faerie scion to take the associated lineage feat.

Additional Feats

Slimeheart.name: 黏液之心 Slimeheart
Slimeheart.text:
有些人一半是天使，一半是恶魔，甚至一半是龙；这虽然罕见，但人们仍然很容易理解。黏液之心则不同。成为“一半黏液”究竟意味着什么？对敌对黏液怪的过度暴露、在黏液怪或其他生物无法独自存活时形成的共生联系，或其他各种离奇事件，都可能导致黏液怪与另一种生物的融合。黏液之心那黏稠的力量与外表使他们无论走到哪里都格外显眼。如果你想扮演一个拥有黏液力量但又不是完整黏液怪的角色，你就应该选择黏液之心。

你可能会……

  • 寻求探索你黏液之心的本质，并理解你为何会成为黏液之心，或你的力量如何运作。
  • 试着推动自己并适应，以精炼你的黏液能力。
  • 对所有其他族裔的角色保持开放的心态。毕竟，你有一部分是黏液，你知道世界很大！

其他人很可能会……

  • 分不清你究竟是拥有黏液力量的本族裔成员，还是某只黏液怪或其他软泥怪化作了你族裔成员的形态。
  • 担心你黏液之心的本质是否具有传染性，并试图限制任何接触以防万一（或者如果他们想把你的力量据为己有，就会故意让自己暴露其中）。
  • 根据你身体的外观，认定你必定很灵活，至少在某种程度上是无定形的。

生理描述

黏液之心通常看起来像其族裔成员，但带有类黏液怪的特征；这些特征的程度从微小到几乎可忽略不计，到极其显著、无法忽视不等。对大多数黏液之心而言，他们的身体部分由色彩斑斓的软泥构成，因此除非他们费尽心力去遮掩（例如穿着覆盖性极强的衣物或全身护甲），否则他们的传承显而易见。

在更隐蔽的那一端，许多黏液之心拥有一颗实体的心脏，其外观更像黏液怪的核，而不是其物种生物的正常心脏。这也是“黏液之心”这一名称起源的一部分。然而，这一生理特征深藏在体内器官之中，即使对黏液之心自己也很难察觉——直到他们死后……至少对大多数拥有这一特征的黏液之心来说是如此。另一方面，有少数黏液之心的皮肤几乎透明，使他们的器官对旁观者一览无余，这也让那不寻常的心脏对他们自己与能看见的人都显而易见。

在少数黏液之心中还存在其他更为罕见的特征。例如，有些黏液之心的骨骼比其族裔的其他成员更少或更柔韧，使他们能够以看似不该弯折的角度扭曲身体。还有些人异常有弹性或反弹力十足，或当他们的皮肤、鳞片或其他体表覆盖本应干燥时，却总是诡异地潮湿黏滑。

社会

关于黏液之心最常见的故事，是黏液之心的起源，在同伴间俗称为“被黏化”（slimed）——这个称呼用一点幽默为一段往往骇人或创伤性的经历抹上一层轻松。被黏化者是从与软泥怪的致命遭遇中幸存下来的生物，往往是九死一生，而这段经历令他们永远发生了改变。

在被黏化者之间，存在某种“先有鸡还是先有蛋”的疑问：他们是因为在濒死经历中活下来才成为黏液之心，还是因为成为黏液之心才得以在濒死经历中活下来？对大多数被黏化者而言，这种区分只是一种学术讨论；重要的是他们还活着，而且现在与事故前不同了。被黏化者的存在，也是为何那些“懂一点点却又无知得很彻底”的人有时会担心黏液之心具有传染性的一部分原因。毕竟，如果接触黏液怪能把人变成黏液之心，那么按他们站不住脚的逻辑，接触黏液之心也可能引发某种流行病。更别提从未有过任何此类记录；那些试图煽动对黏液之心恐惧的人，依赖的是不确定性与“如果……会怎样”，而不是数据。他们宣称宁可谨慎也不要后悔，以此来制造恐惧与影响他人。

“实验体”成为黏液之心的路径与被黏化者有些相似，但实验体起源的关键就写在名字里：他们是通过某种实验成为黏液之心的。虽然这可能完全是一次魔法或炼金爆炸的随机副作用，但更常见的是，实验体黏液之心会将其起源追溯到一次有意的尝试：用炼金与魔法的混合手段，将某些软泥怪特征灌注进另一族裔的受试者体内。是否自愿则取决于实验者的伦理；许多人在自己身上做实验。表面上看，这似乎像是某个被放逐的炼金术士在隐秘巢穴中进行的鲁莽边缘研究，并企图实施某种难以解释的邪恶阴谋；但事实是，软泥怪（尤其是黏液怪）具有许多益处，善意的研究者可能会选择加以利用。首先，由于其作为生物的结构相对简单，软泥怪拥有惊人的韧性、恢复能力与寿命。若能将软泥怪的某些方面注入其他族裔，可能会为多种疾病与其他医疗状况带来极其积极的成果，也能提升寿命。而且认为成功可行并不荒唐：至少存在一种古老过程，能将自愿受试者完全转化为其旧我之“可变形软泥怪版本”，而该过程的幸存者自那以来已存续数千年。无论相关研究者是否足够清醒、成为黏液之心是否达成了项目目标，实验体往往会把自己的黏液之心状态当作一项科学实验来对待，测试自己能做什么、以及是否能通过练习做到更多。

“宿主”是指在黏液怪、另一生物、或两者都濒死之际出现的黏液之心；在那一刻，黏液怪的核发生演化，使黏液怪能够成为共生体，拯救一条或两条生命，并将两者合并为一个“整体大于部分之和”的黏液之心。黏液怪与其他软泥怪一样，拥有求生的意志，因此垂死的黏液怪为了避免不可避免的灭亡而依附在附近的某个愿意救它的人身上，这并不难理解；但当黏液怪成为共生体去拯救另一生命时，就更出人意料了。是什么促使黏液怪采取这一行动？在某些情况下，黏液怪与濒死生物曾共同相处过一段时间；也许黏液怪的核具有感知能力（即使不具备理智），他们通过共同在致命处境中存活而建立了信任纽带。另一些时候，这可能只是黏液怪的本能：它感知到宿主的意愿与非凡潜力，意识到若觉醒出与此宿主融合的能力，自己就能成为更强大的存在。与“融合体”不同——融合体是由两个或更多角色合而为一的族裔——宿主是单一生物，尽管其意识可能因黏液怪与另一生物而呈现多个侧面（你可以在 Battlezoo Ancestries: Fusions 中找到更多关于融合的信息）。在大多数情况下，成为宿主是单向过程，这个新的黏液之心的两部分将永远纠缠为一体。然而，在极其罕见的情况下，共生黏液怪可能获得从宿主中脱离的能力。若一名新玩家在故事中途加入，这可能是一个将新黏液怪角色引入战役的有趣机会：黏液之心玩家可以在共生黏液怪离开后，将黏液之心多用传承作为共处时间的后效应保留下来，或若共生体分离时黏液特征也随之离体，则可能重训为不同的传承。

所有类型黏液之心中最罕见者被称为“分裂体”。正如其名所示，他们并非原本是其他族裔成员因接触软泥怪而成为黏液之心；相反，他们天生如此。在为该起源命名的经典案例中，一只黏液怪在为繁殖而分裂前不久吞食了某个生物的一部分，而那生物的大量生物材料与新的核混合在一起，结果诞生的并非新的黏液怪，而是一个后代：其外观极为接近该生物族裔的婴儿、幼童，甚至成人。若黏液怪不具备感知、也没有照料分裂体的本能，那么分裂体黏液之心可能难以自行进食并独自存活。大多数能活下来的分裂体，都是幸运地被善良的陌生人、原生物的盟友（其生物材料参与了他们的诞生）、甚至原生物本人发现并照料（该过程并不必然保证原生物无法存活）。常被半开玩笑地归类为分裂体（尽管起源略有不同）的，还包括某些黏液之心的子女：他们不知为何将类软泥怪的本质传递给下一代。通常黏液之心的后代不会保留任何黏液之心特征，而有些黏液之心根本无法生育，因为其黏液部分的繁殖方式与另一部分根本不同。此外，即便黏液之心状态能从父母传给子女跨越一代，也很少能延续到第二代或更远。第四代黏液之心几乎闻所未闻。

黏液之心聚居地

尽管证据恰恰相反，黏液之心有时仍会面对来自担心其传承具有传染性的社群偏见。因此，他们有时会建立聚居地，作为所有黏液之心的避难所，使其能免于迫害，自由而安全地生活。当然，这并不等于黏液之心可以随意加入聚居地然后毁坏一切或犯下恶行。创建这些聚居地的黏液之心以宽容著称，但若新居民滥用他们的慷慨，他们同样也有底线。

阵营与宗教

黏液之心的阵营倾向通常与其族裔的其他成员一致，但会因起源而有少数例外。分裂体由软泥怪分裂形成，因此其阵营更可能更接近其父母软泥怪（对大多数软泥怪来说通常是中立）。同样地，共生体可能会体验到自我与体内软泥共生体的交融，产生类似影响，使其阵营更趋中立，并以满足基本需求为核心。被黏化者在经历与软泥怪相关的命运事故后，可能会变得更愤世嫉俗或更谨慎。实验体的阵营倾向取决于实验的性质与是否自愿，不过这些多半是他们在成为黏液之心之前的阵营指标，而非其黏液之心传承的效果。通常来说，在自己身上做实验、将软泥怪灌注进体内的人，比起守序更可能偏向混乱，尽管也有例外：他们将软泥怪视为完全守序的生命形态。对他人进行非自愿实验者则无一例外是邪恶的，而此类阴谋的受害者可能因此改变阵营：出于复仇而走向邪恶，或出于拯救他人免遭同样命运的愿望而走向善良。

与其族裔的其他成员相比，黏液之心并没有特别强烈的宗教倾向。有些人选择崇拜与软泥怪或黏液怪有关的神祇，但多数人仍延续此前的信仰。亲自参与自身实验的实验体，可能会崇拜推崇边缘科学与肉体扭曲的神祇。分裂体有时会选择崇拜能帮助他们解释自己在更广阔宇宙中异常位置的宗教——毕竟他们是从黏液怪分裂而来；除此之外，他们往往会追随在其脆弱幼年时期帮助其存活的养父母的宗教倾向。

名字

除分裂体外，黏液之心通常拥有与其族裔其他凡人相似的名字。然而，共生体有时会改名或额外采用一个名字，以纪念体内的黏液共生体；而有些实验体在变形后选择从事英雄或反派活动，则会为其替身身份起一个战名，例如“绿色黏胶”（The Green Goo）。分裂体的名字往往取决于抚养他们的生物，或由他们自己选择。被黏化者几乎从不改用与其经历涉及软泥怪的濒死体验之前不同的名字。

示例名字

Alexandria Mock, Cassidy Carnage, Edward Venom, Ink, Ivan Slime, Metamorph, Odo, Plasmus, The Green Goo.

黏液之心冒险者

黏液之心常常成为冒险者，而被黏化者、共生体与分裂体原本就更可能是冒险者：就遭遇软泥怪的概率而言，“冒险者”几乎位居所有职业之首。只有实验体拥有更广泛的非冒险者成员。黏液之心往往会选择能最大化其类软泥怪本性的职业，例如武僧，或能让他们研究与调查这种本性的职业，例如炼金术士。他们的背景各异，不过一些罕见背景——如魔法实验或机械共生的变体——可能很合适。

黏液之心（稀有传承）

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.Pq2SLgyMOqhLbVMK]{黏液之心}

无论你是从被软泥怪“黏化”中幸存、尝试将软泥怪灌注进自己体内的实验者、在软泥共生体帮助下从致命状态中恢复，还是在某只黏液怪分裂前吞食了另一族裔材料后由其分裂而生，你除了自身的其他族裔之外，也有一部分是黏液怪。你获得黏液之心特征，此外仍保有你族裔的特征。由于你的半黏液解剖结构，你还获得对精准伤害的抗力。你获得等于 1 + 你等级一半（[[1+floor(@actor.level/2)]]）的精准伤害抗力。此外，你可以选择以“伪足”徒手攻击取代“拳头”徒手攻击，但其余数据相同。若你选择会改变伪足徒手攻击的黏液怪专长，这会很有用。

族裔专长

以下族裔专长可供黏液之心选择。黏液之心独有的族裔专长只有少数，但他们会解锁下方“额外专长”中所列的大多数黏液怪族裔专长的潜力。“软泥怪的韧性”族裔专长不会与“山岳健壮”或任何其他每等级提供额外生命值的族裔专长叠加。

5级专长

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-feats.Item.zWNFsEY6K7ufZC97]{运动感知}

Some people are part angel, part demon, or even part dragon, and that's unusual but still easy enough for people to understand. Slimehearts are different. What does it even mean to be part slime? Overexposure to hostile slime, a symbiotic link when a slime or another creature couldn't survive on their own, or a variety of other unusual events can lead to the merging of a slime and another creature. A slimeheart's oozy powers and appearance cause them to stand out wherever they go. If you want to play a character who has ooze powers but isn't fully an ooze, you should play a slimeheart.

You Might...

  • Seek to explore your slimeheart nature and understand how you came to be a slimeheart or how your powers function.
  • Attempt to push yourself and adapt in order to refine your slime abilities.
  • Keep an open mind about characters of all other ancestries. After all, you're part slime, and you know the world is a big place!

Others Probably...

  • Can't tell whether you're a member of your ancestry with ooze powers or a slime or other ooze taking the shape of a member of your ancestry.
  • Worry whether your slimeheart nature might be contagious and attempt to limit any exposure just in case (or expose themselves on purpose if they're seeking to gain your powers for themselves)
  • Assume that you must be flexible and at least somewhat amorphous, considering the way your body looks.

Physical Description

Slimehearts normally appear as members of their ancestry with oozelike features, running the gamut from small and possible to overlook to extremely salient and unmissable. For most slimehearts, their body is partially composed of colorful ooze, and so their heritage is obvious unless they go to great lengths to conceal it, such as wearing extremely covering clothing or full body armor.

On the more surreptitious end of the spectrum, many slimehearts have a physical heart that looks like a slime's nucleus rather than a normal heart for a creature of their species. This is part of the origin of the name slimeheart. However, it's difficult to notice this physical feature deep within their body's internal organs, even for the slimeheart themselves, until after they die... at least for most slimehearts who possess it. On the other hand, a few slimehearts have mostly transparent skin, leaving their organs readily visible to onlookers, which also makes their unusual hearts obvious to themselves and others who can see them.

Other, even rarer features exist as well among small numbers of slimehearts. For instance, some slimehearts have fewer or more flexible bones than other members of their ancestry, allowing them to bend at angles their body seems like it wasn't meant to bend. Others are unusually rubbery or bouncy, or they are always strangely moist and wet when their skin, scales, or other body covering would otherwise be dry.

Society

The most common tale for a slimeheart is that of the slimeheart origin, colloquially known as "slimed" among their fellows, a name that shines a bit of good humor on a situation that was often horrific or traumatic. Slimed are creatures who survived a life-threatening encounter with an ooze, often by the skin of their teeth, and were left forever transformed by the experience.

Among slimed, there's something of a chicken and egg question: did they become a slimeheart because they survived their near death experience, or did they survive their near death experience because they became a slimeheart? For most slimed, the distinction is academic; what matters is that they're still alive, and now they're different than they were before the incident. The existence of slimed is part of why those with the perfect cocktail of a tiny bit of knowledge and a heavy heaping of ignorance sometimes become concerned that being a slimeheart is contagious. After all, if exposure to a slime can turn someone into a slimeheart, their tenuous logic holds, then exposure to a slimeheart could start some sort of epidemic. Never mind that nothing of the sort has ever been recorded; those who seek to stir up fear about slimehearts thrive on uncertainty and "what ifs" rather than data, postulating that it's better to be safe than sorry in order to stir up fear and influence.

Experiments are somewhat similar to slimed in their path to becoming a slimeheart, but the important part of an experiment's origin is right there in the name: they became a slimeheart through some form of experiment. While it could have been a completely random side effect to a magical or alchemical explosion, most often an experiment slimeheart traces their origin to an intentional attempt to use a blend of alchemy and magic to instill some ooze features in a test subject of another ancestry. Whether willing to otherwise depends on the ethics of the experimenter in question; many performed their experiments on themselves. While it might seem like a reckless fringe research only performed by an outcast alchemist in a hidden lair bent on some sort of inexplicably diabolical scheme, the truth is that there are many beneficial aspects of oozes (and slimes in particular) that well-meaning researchers might choose to harness. For one thing, due to their simplicity as organisms, oozes have incredible resilience, recuperative capabilities, and lifespans. Being able to infuse select aspects of oozes into other ancestries could yield incredibly positive outcomes for numerous diseases and other medical conditions, as well as for longevity. And it's not outrageous to assume that success is possible: at least one ancient process existed to transfuse a willing subject entirely into a shapeshifting ooze version of their former self, and survivors of this process have lasted for millennia since then. Regardless of the clear-headedness of the researchers involved and whether or not becoming a slimeheart met the project's goal, experiments tend to treat their slimeheart state a bit like a science experiment, testing out what they can do and whether they can do more with practice.

Hosts are slimehearts who arise when a slime, another creature, or both are on the brink of death, and in that moment, the slime's nucleus evolves to allow the slime to become a symbiote, saving one life or two and merging the two together into a slimeheart greater than the sum of their parts. Slimes, like other oozes, possess a will to survive, so it's apparent why a dying slime might latch onto someone else around willing to save it from an otherwise inevitable demise, but it's more unexpected when a slime becomes a symbiote to save another being's life. What causes the slime to take this action? In some cases, the slime and the dying creature worked together for a time; perhaps the slime's nucleus was sentient, if not sapient, and they forged a bond of trust by surviving life-threatening situations together. Other times, it might simply be an instinct on the slime's part that it could become something greater if it awoke to the ability to merge with this host, sensing the host's willingness and unusual potential to help the slime grow. Unlike a fusion, an ancestry that's two or more characters in one, a host is a single creature, though potentially with multiple facets to their consciousness from the slime and the other creature (you can find more information on fusions in Battlezoo Ancestries: Fusions). In most cases, becoming a host is a one-way process, and the two pieces of the new slimeheart are now forever entwined as one. However, in extremely rare cases, a symbiote slime might gain the ability to divest from the host. This might be an interesting opportunity to introduce a new slime character to the campaign if a new player joins partway through the story, with the slimeheart player either keeping their slimeheart versatile heritage as an aftereffect of their time together with the symbiote slime or potentially retraining to a different heritage if the slime features leave their body when the symbiote separates out.

The rarest of all types of slimehearts are called divisions. Like the name implies, they weren't originally members of another ancestry who became slimehearts due to exposure to oozes but instead, they were born that way. In the classic case that named the origin, a slime consumes a portion of a creature just before dividing to reproduce, and a great deal of that creature's biological material is mixed in with the new nucleus, resulting not in a new slime but in an offspring that greatly resembles an infant, child, or even an adult of that creature's ancestry. If the slime isn't sentient and has no instinct to help take care of the division, the division slimeheart might have difficulty feeding themselves and surviving on their own. Most who do so were lucky enough to be found by a kind stranger, an ally of the original creature whose biological material was part of their birth, or even the original creature themselves (there's nothing particular about the process that would guarantee the original creature didn't survive it). Often classified tongue in cheek as divisions, even though the origin is slightly different, are the children of slimehearts who somehow pass on their oozelike nature to the next generation. Normally a slimeheart's offspring don't retain any slimeheart features, and some slimeheart are unable to have offspring at all, as the slime part of them reproduces in such a fundamentally different way as the other part. Furthermore, even if the slimeheart status passes from parent to child across one generation, it rarely crosses into a second generation or beyond. Fourth generation slimehearts are virtually unheard of.

Slimeheart Settlements

Slimehearts sometimes face prejudice from communities that fear their heritage is contagious, despite evidence to the contrary. As a result, they sometimes form settlements as sanctuaries for all slimehearts to live free and safe from persecution. Of course, this isn't carte blanche for slimehearts to join the settlement and wreck the place or commit malefic deeds. Slimehearts who create these settlements are notoriously forgiving, but even they have a limit if a new resident abuses their generosity..

Alignment and Religion

Slimehearts tend to have the same alignment as all other members of their ancestry, with a few exceptions based on their origins. Divisions are formed when an ooze splits, so they are more likely to have an alignment closer to the parent ooze (typically neutral for most oozes). Similarly, symbiotes might experience a melding of their self with the ooze symbiote within them, to much the same effect, centering around a more neutral alignment focused on achieving basic needs. While slimed might become a bit more cynical or cautious after experiencing their fateful mishap with an ooze. Experiments' alignment tendencies depend on the nature of the experiments and whether they were voluntary, though these are mostly indicators of their alignment prior to becoming a slimeheart, rather than an effect of their silmeheart heritage. Normally someone who experiments on their own body to infuse themselves with an ooze is more likely to be chaotic than lawful, though exceptions exist who see oozes as a perfectly lawful life form. Those who engage in nonconsensual experimentation on others are invariably evil, and victims of such schemes might change their alignment as a result, becoming evil out of vengeance, or becoming good out of a desire to save others from the same fate.

Slimehearts don't have especially strong religious tendencies compared to other members of their ancestry. While some decide to worship deities of ooze or slime, most continue with their prior worship. Experiments who were involved with their own experimentation might worship deities promoting fringe science and fleshwarping. Divisions sometimes choose to worship religions that can help them explain their unusual place in the wider universe, given they divided from a slime, and otherwise they follow the religious tendencies of the adoptive parent or parents who helped them survive their vulnerable early years.

Names

Slimehearts other than divisions typically have names similar to other mortals of their ancestry. However, symbiotes sometimes either change their name or adopt an additional name to honor the slime symbiote within them, and some experiments who choose to engage in heroic or villainous activities after their transformation come up with a nom de guerre to use for their alternate guise, like "The Green Goo." Divisions tend to have names depending on the creature or creatures who raised them, or else names they chose for themselves. Slimed virtually never have different names than the ones they possessed before their near-death experience involving oozes.

Sample Names

Alexandria Mock, Cassidy Carnage, Edward Venom, Ink, Ivan Slime, Metamorph, Odo, Plasmus, The Green Goo.

Slimeheart Adventurers

Slimehearts often become adventurers, and slimed, symbiotes, and divisions were much more likely to be adventurers in the first place, given that adventurer is near the top of all occupations in terms of the probability of encountering an ooze in the first place. Only experiments have a wider array of nonadventurer members. Slimehearts often choose classes that allow them to make the most of their oozelike nature, such as monk, or those that allow them to study and investigate it, like alchemist. Their backgrounds vary, though some rare ones like magical experiment or a variant of mechanical symbiosis may be appropriate.

Slimeheart (Rare Heritage)

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-year-of-monsters-pf2e.yom-heritages.Item.Pq2SLgyMOqhLbVMK]{Slimeheart}

Whether you survived being slimed by an ooze, experimented with infusing oozes into yourself, recovered from a fatal condition with the help of an ooze symbiote, or divided from an slime that consumed material from another ancestry just before splitting, you are part slime, in addition to your other ancestry. You gain the slimeheart trait in addition to the traits from your ancestry. You also gain resistance to precision damage from your part-slime anatomy. You gain resistance to precision damage equal to 1 + half your level ([[1+floor(@actor.level/2)]]). Additionally, you can make pseudopod unarmed attacks if you choose instead of fist unarmed attacks, though they otherwise have the same statistics. This can be useful if you take slime feats that alter your pseudopod unarmed attacks.

Ancestry Feats

The following ancestry feats are available to slimehearts. Slimehearts have only a few ancestry feats uniquely available only to them, but they unlock the potential to the vast majority of slime ancestry feats, listed below in Additional Feats. The Ooze's Resilience ancestry feat isn't cumulative with Mountain's Stoutness or any other ancestry feat that grants additional Hit Points for each level.
